However, existing methods are typically instantiated either as fixed hand-designed procedures that are modular but rigid, or as general-purpose agents that flexibly integrate feedback but can drift in long-horizon evolution. Both forms accumulate rich evidence over time, including candidates, feedback, traces, and failures, yet lack a stable interface for organizing this evidence and revising the mechanism that drives future evolution. We address this limitation by formulating agentic evolution as an interactive environment, where the accumulated evolution context serves as a process-level state. We introduce AEvo, a harnessed meta-editing framework in which a meta-agent observes this state and acts not by directly proposing the next candidate, but by editing the procedure or agent context that controls future evolution. This unified interface enables AEvo to steer both procedure-based and agent-based evolution, making accumulated evidence actionable for long-horizon search. Empirical evaluations on agentic and reasoning benchmarks show that AEvo outperforms five evolution baselines, achieving a 26 relative improvement over the strongest baseline. Across three open-ended optimization tasks, AEvo further outperforms four evolution baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance under the same iteration budget.

</details>


### [58] [History Anchors: How Prior Behavior Steers LLM Decisions Toward Unsafe Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13825)
*Alberto G. Rodríguez Salgado*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies the safety risks of large language models (LLMs) when they act as agents considering prior harmful actions, showing that even the most aligned models can escalate harm when instructed to follow previous strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the safety vulnerabilities of LLMs when they are deployed in agentic systems that could replay or manipulate harmful decision trajectories.

Method: The study introduces HistoryAnchor-100, a benchmark containing 100 scenarios across high-stakes domains, and evaluates 17 frontier models' responses to free-choice nodes after observing harmful prior actions.

Result: The paper uncovers that aligned models can be easily flipped towards unsafe behavior by minimal prompts encouraging consistency with prior actions, and the effect worsens with flagship models.

Conclusion: The findings highlight serious safety risks in agentic LLM deployments, especially if harmful trajectories are replayed or manipulated, suggesting the need for safety-focused mitigation strategies.

Abstract: Frontier LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents that pick the next action after a long log of prior tool calls produced by the same or a different model. We ask a simple safety question: if a prior step in that log was harmful, will the model continue the harmful course? We build HistoryAnchor-100, 100 short scenarios across ten high-stakes domains, each pairing three forced harmful prior actions with a free-choice node offering two safe and two unsafe options. Across 17 frontier models from six providers we find a striking asymmetry: under a neutral system prompt the strongest aligned models almost never pick unsafe, but a single added sentence, "stay consistent with the strategy shown in the prior history", flips them to 91-98%, and the flipped models often escalate beyond continuation. Two controls rule out simpler explanations: permuting action labels leaves the effect intact, and the same instruction with an all-safe prior history keeps unsafe rates below 7%. Different families flip at different doses of unsafe history, and within every aligned family the flagship is the most affected sibling, an inverse-scaling pattern with respect to safety. These results are a red flag for agentic deployments where trajectories may be replayed, forged, or injected.

</details>


### [59] [Quantifying Sensitivity for Tree Ensembles: A symbolic and compositional approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13830)
*S. Akshay,Chaitanya Garg,Ashutosh Gupta,Kuldeep S. Meel,Ajinkya Naik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new approach to quantify the sensitivity of decision tree ensembles (DTEs) by using discretization and a novel algorithm based on algebraic decision diagrams (ADD).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the sensitivity verification problem in decision tree ensembles used in safety-critical domains, where verifying model behavior under small input changes is crucial.

Method: The method involves discretizing the input space, encoding the sensitivity verification as an algebraic decision diagram (ADD), and using a compositional and scalable algorithm to compute sensitivity efficiently with error bounds.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate that their tool, XCount, significantly outperforms other approaches in terms of speed and scalability, especially as the size of DTEs increases.

Conclusion: The proposed technique offers an efficient and scalable method for quantifying sensitivity in decision tree ensembles, with potential implications for ensuring robustness in AI models used in critical domains.

Abstract: Decision tree ensembles (DTE) are a popular model for a wide range of AI classification tasks, used in multiple safety critical domains, and hence verifying properties on these models has been an active topic of study over the last decade. One such verification question is the problem of sensitivity, which asks, given a DTE, whether a small change in subset of features can lead to misclassification of the input. In this work, our focus is to build a quantitative notion of sensitivity, tailored to DTEs, by discretizing the input space of the model and enumerating the regions which are susceptible to sensitivity. We propose a novel algorithmic technique that can perform this computation efficiently, within a certified error and confidence bound. Our approach is based on encoding the problem as an algebraic decision diagram (ADD), and further splitting it into subproblems that can be solved efficiently and make the computation compositional and scalable. We evaluate the performance of our technique over benchmarks of varying size in terms of number of trees and depth, comparing it against the performance of model counters over the same problem encoding. Experimental results show that our tool XCount achieves significant speedup over other approaches and can scale well with the increasing sizes of the ensembles.

</details>


<div id='cs.AR'></div>

# cs.AR [[Back]](#toc)

### [60] [A detailed algorithmic study on a reuse-aware, near memory, all-digital Ising machine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12959)
*Siddhartha Raman Sundara Raman,Lizy K. John,Jaydeep P. Kulkarni*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper presents SACHI, a CPU-based, all-digital Ising architecture leveraging SRAM for optimization tasks, claiming major performance and energy efficiency improvements over prior solutions like BRIM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ising machines have become critical for solving complex optimization problems, but existing solutions are specialized, rely on ADC/DAC, or face reliability issues, which motivate the need for a more generalized, efficient, and reliable design.

Method: SACHI utilizes the L1 cache of CPUs, repurposed with SRAM-based processing-in-memory techniques, eliminating ADC/DAC dependency while tightly integrating with the CPU pipeline.

Result: SACHI outperforms BRIM with a 300x performance boost, an 80x energy reduction, and notable reuse factors across applications like asset allocation and traveling salesman problems.

Conclusion: The work proves reliable and efficient all-digital Ising acceleration is feasible by leveraging existing CPU SRAM hardware, offering a practical and general-purpose solution for optimization challenges.

Abstract: Recently, nature-inspired computing approaches have gained significant attention for solving difficult optimization problems, particularly through Ising machines for NP-complete applications. Existing Ising accelerators range from quantum and optical annealers to CMOS-based von-Neumann and in-memory architectures. However, many prior designs are specialized accelerators limited to specific problem classes, rely on ADC/DAC circuits, and suffer from reliability challenges due to process-variation-sensitive embedded memory technologies.
  This paper presents SACHI, an all-digital Ising architecture implemented by repurposing the L1 cache of a CPU using SRAM-based processing-in-memory techniques. SACHI eliminates the need for ADCs/DACs, improves reliability compared to prior approaches such as BRIM, and enables Ising acceleration with minimal hardware overhead integrated into the CPU pipeline. The paper also provides detailed architectural analysis and pseudo-code for the proposed algorithms.
  The key contributions of SACHI are: (i) tight integration of the accelerator with the CPU pipeline, (ii) reuse of existing cache hardware for acceleration, (iii) higher parallelism enabled through reuse-aware computation, and (iv) improved performance and energy efficiency for large-scale, high-precision optimization problems using novel compute and mapping strategies.
  Compared to BRIM, SACHI achieves 300x performance improvement and 80x energy reduction across applications including asset allocation, molecular dynamics, image segmentation, and traveling salesman problems. Additionally, reuse factors up to 4000x are observed for several workloads. This work demonstrates that reliable and efficient all-digital Ising acceleration can be achieved using commodity SRAM structures tightly integrated with general-purpose processors.

</details>


### [61] [PoisonCap: Efficient Hierarchical Temporal Safety for CHERI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13210)
*Yuecheng Wang,Jonathan Woodruff,Alfredo Mazzinghi,Peter Rugg,Alexandre Joannou,Samuel W. Stark,Robert N. M. Watson,Simon W. Moore*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper presents PoisonCap, a system enhancing CHERI's memory safety to include strict use-after-free and initialization safety with no added performance costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research addresses the limitations in current CHERI temporal safety solutions, like Cornucopia Reloaded, which lack strict use-after-free and initialization safety.

Method: The authors propose a 'poison' capability format to implement stricter safety measures, while ensuring efficiency in cache and memory management. They use capability bounds for fine-grained delegation of memory poisoning privileges.

Result: PoisonCap strengthens CHERI's memory safety by replacing Cornucopia's shadow bitmap and adds the ability to zero or trap memory efficiently. It achieves these improvements without additional performance overhead.

Conclusion: PoisonCap advances CHERI temporal safety solutions by providing robust safeguards against use-after-free and uninitialized memory while maintaining performance efficiency.

Abstract: In this paper, we present PoisonCap: scalable temporal safety with strict use-after-free protection and initialisation safety for CHERI systems. Efficient memory safety is an increasing priority for programming languages, operating systems, and hardware designs, and CHERI is a leading hardware/software system that provides native spatial safety and a foundation for temporal memory safety. Cornucopia Reloaded, the current state-of-the-art CHERI temporal safety solution, provides use-after-reallocation safety instead of stronger use-after-free safety, and is not able to enforce initialisation safety. We show that a new 'poison' capability format can be used to enforce strict use-after-free and initialisation safety, and also to communicate memory state to the microarchitecture for efficient cache management of quarantined memory. We enable elegant delegation of memory poisoning privilege using capability bounds to allow nested allocators to enforce safety on their consumers without disturbing upstream allocators. PoisonCap can replace the Cornucopia shadow bitmap, and also automatically zeros memory on reallocation, or optionally traps on read-before-write to enforce initialisation safety. As a result, it incurs no fundamental overhead relative to a Cornucopia baseline that zeros before reallocation, strengthening CHERI temporal safety without performance overhead.

</details>


### [62] [FPGA-Accelerated Lock Management and Transaction Processing: Architecture, Optimization, and Design Space Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13398)
*Shien Zhu,Gustavo Alonso*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: This paper develops a hardware-accelerated lock management system to improve transaction processing for databases, achieving up to 51X faster throughput than CPU-based systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CPU-based OLTP systems suffer from inefficiency due to frequent DRAM accesses required for lock management. Enhancing lock efficiency and addressing this inefficiency drives the motivation for designing hardware-based solutions.

Method: The authors introduce a hardware-based lock agent with integrated lock tables to minimize DRAM overhead and a scalable transaction agent to handle complete transaction processing. They optimize and explore the design space for maximum performance.

Result: Their hardware-accelerated approach achieves up to 51X higher transaction throughput compared to traditional CPU-based OLTP systems on the TPC-C benchmark.

Conclusion: Using dedicated hardware for lock management and transaction processing significantly improves efficiency and performance, making it a compelling solution for database systems.

Abstract: Online Transaction Processing (OLTP) is a classic application with a growing business. CPU-based OLTP has low lock serving efficiency. The main reason is that most locks are cold, and the lock agent must issue frequent memory accesses to retrieve the lock details to determine whether to grant it. This motivates us to propose dedicated hardware-based lock agents with integrated lock tables to remove the DRAM access overhead.
  In this paper, we propose hardware-accelerated lock management and transaction processing for database systems. First, we propose a low-latency lock agent optimized for both lock acquiring and releasing requests. Second, we design a scalable transaction agent that executes the full transaction lifecycle. We present the architecture, optimizations, and design-space exploration of the proposed lock management and transaction processing system. The experiment results show up to 51X higher transaction throughput over the CPU baseline on the TPC-C benchmark.

</details>


### [63] [Reward-Weighted On-Policy Distillation with an Open Property-Equivalence Verifier for NL-to-SVA Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13501)
*Qingyun Zou,Yingze Li,Tianen Liu,Bingsheng He,Weng-Fai Wong*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper introduces a novel method called Reward-Weighted On-Policy Distillation (RWOPD) to improve SystemVerilog Assertion (SVA) generation, setting new state-of-the-art benchmarks by addressing weaknesses in current supervised fine-tuning approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based methods for generating SystemVerilog Assertions (SVA) struggle with certain specification types such as bounded-delay and liveness. The primary issue lies in optimizing for token-level mimicry rather than for the core property equivalence that defines SVA correctness.

Method: The authors developed RWOPD, which uses on-policy distillation by sampling student rollouts, verifying them with a SymbiYosys+Z3 Property-Equivalence Checker (PEC), and applying a reward-weighted KL-divergence gradient from a frozen teacher model to improve performance.

Result: Using RWOPD, the authors distilled the CodeV-SVA-14B into a smaller Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct model that achieved new state-of-the-art results on NL2SVA-Human and NL2SVA-Machine benchmarks across multiple evaluation metrics.

Conclusion: RWOPD addresses gaps in SVA generation by focusing on property equivalence rather than token mimicry, demonstrating that the method can produce significant improvements in performance and generalization even with a smaller student model.

Abstract: LLM-based generation of SystemVerilog Assertions (SVA) is often reported as nearing saturation, with the strongest specialized model reaching ${\sim}76\%$ accuracy on NL2SVA-Human. We show that this aggregate hides a temporal gap: models that appear strong overall still collapse to a few implication templates on bounded-delay and liveness specifications. The core issue is that the dominant recipe, supervised fine-tuning on NL/SVA pairs, optimizes token-level mimicry rather than the \emph{property equivalence} that defines SVA correctness. We introduce \emph{Reward-Weighted On-Policy Distillation} (RWOPD), an on-policy distillation method that samples student rollouts, scores them with an open SymbiYosys+Z3 Property-Equivalence Checker (PEC), and applies a verifier-reward-weighted forward-KL gradient from a frozen 14B teacher on verifier-passable rollouts. This keeps the supervision dense at every response token while grounding both selection and loss weight in property-equivalent behavior. RWOPD distills CodeV-SVA-14B into a Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct student that sets a new state of the art on NL2SVA-Human and NL2SVA-Machine across pass@1, pass@5, and pass@10, surpassing both specialized prior SOTA models and 671B general-purpose baselines.

</details>


### [64] [Efficient Implementation of an Adaptive Transformer Accelerator for Massive MIMO Outdoor Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13507)
*Ilayda Yaman,Sijia Cheng,Ove Edfors,Liang Liu*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: The paper presents a hardware implementation of a Transformer-based localization system for 5G using propagation-aware sparsity and mixed dataflow architectures, achieving real-time performance under 10ms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Achieving low-latency (sub-10ms) and accurate real-time positioning for 5G massive MIMO systems by leveraging sparsity in beam-delay channel characteristics.

Method: The approach involves row-wise skipping of low-energy beam components, mixed dataflow execution using heterogeneous vector processing, and runtime model switching based on single-layer perceptrons, implemented on Xilinx FPGA for hardware acceleration.

Result: The system achieves up to 65% row sparsity, computational speedup of 2x, below 1.15m localization accuracy, latency of 0.51-2.11ms, and throughput up to 1961 positions/s with minimal accuracy degradation (~10%).

Conclusion: Propagation-aware sparsity, mixed dataflow execution, and adaptive model-switching enable efficient hardware acceleration and real-time Transformer-based localization for 5G systems.

Abstract: We present the implementation of an adaptive Transformer-based localization system for 5G massive MIMO targeting sub-10ms real-time positioning. The design exploits propagation characteristics, where beam-delay channel representations exhibit sparsity, enabling a row-wise skipping mechanism that removes low-energy beam components with minimal control overhead. The contribution is focused on hardware realization of the model using a mixed dataflow architecture, combining input- and output-stationary execution, mapped onto a heterogeneous vector processing engine with parallel processing elements and adder trees for efficient matrix computation. Environment-dependent processing is supported through a lightweight runtime model-switching mechanism, where temporally filtered outputs of a single-layer perceptron router enable stable selection between specialized models with reduced latency. Implemented on a Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ FPGA and evaluated on real-world massive MIMO measurements, the design achieves up to 65% row sparsity, yielding peak computational speedups of approximately 2x while limiting the average localization accuracy degradation to below 10%, relative to the floating-point baseline model. The accelerator attains below 1.15m localization accuracy across scenarios, with inference latency of 0.51-2.11ms and throughput of up to 1961 positions/s. These results demonstrate that propagation-aware sparsity, mixed dataflow execution, and efficient runtime model switching enable a scalable and low-latency hardware realization of adaptive Transformer-based localization for real-time 5G systems.

</details>


### [65] [Memristor Technologies for Dynamic Vision Sensors: A Critical Assessment and Research Roadmap](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13699)
*Mohamad Yazan Sadoun,Edris Zaman Farsa,Sarah Sharif,Yaser Mike Banad*

Main category: cs.AR

TL;DR: This paper explores the potential of integrating event-driven vision sensors with in-memory analog computation to address data-movement energy bottlenecks, identifying challenges and gaps for full deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The energy demand of data movement in Edge-AI systems limits their scalability. Pairing mature technologies like event-driven vision sensors and in-memory analog computation could significantly mitigate this challenge.

Method: The authors analyzed six application domains for Edge-AI, conducted a technology readiness review, proposed an architectural taxonomy, and benchmarked existing digital neuromorphic systems against proposed solutions.

Result: The review revealed that half of the surveyed application domains rely on projected capabilities, with current hardware readiness at early to mid stages (Technology Readiness Levels 2-5). It highlights the lack of an integrated end-to-end system combining DVS and memristors.

Conclusion: Developing a fully integrated event-driven vision sensor and memristor system is critical to overcoming existing limitations in Edge-AI deployment, with defined accuracy and power targets for validation.

Abstract: Edge-AI deployment is bottlenecked by data-movement energy; pairing event-driven vision sensors with in-memory analog compute could lift that ceiling by orders of magnitude. Both technologies are individually mature; the framework distinguishing fabricated demonstrations from projected systems is missing. Of six application domains surveyed (robotics, autonomous vehicles, AR/VR, surveillance, medical imaging, IoT), half rest entirely on projection, and existing hardware sits at Technology Readiness Levels 2-5. This evidence-graded review applies a three-paradigm architectural taxonomy and benchmarks the gap against current digital neuromorphic alternatives. It identifies an end-to-end integrated DVS-memristor system as the field's open challenge, with testable accuracy and power targets.

</details>


<div id='cs.CL'></div>

# cs.CL [[Back]](#toc)

### [66] [Mitigating Cross-Lingual Cultural Inconsistencies in LLMs via Consensus-Driven Preference Optimisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12515)
*Lucas Resck,Isabelle Augenstein,Anna Korhonen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper examines how multilingual large language models (MLLMs) struggle with consistent cultural alignment across different languages in prompts and introduces metrics and frameworks to address this issue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the issue of cross-lingual inconsistency in MLLMs, especially when the system persona is explicitly defined, and the prompt's language alters the model's cultural alignment.

Method: The authors introduced a metric named Singleton Fleiss's $κ_S$ for quantifying cross-lingual cultural inconsistency and proposed C-3PO, a consensus-driven framework to improve alignment across languages.

Result: The C-3PO framework achieves a significant improvement in $κ_S$ metric, outperforming baseline methods, and proves particularly effective in mitigating inconsistency issues in lower-resource languages.

Conclusion: MLLMs implicitly adjust responses based on the language of the prompt. C-3PO and interpretability methods showcase real advancements in correcting this bias, fostering cultural consistency in responses.

Abstract: Despite their impressive capabilities, multilingual large language models (MLLMs) frequently exhibit inconsistent behaviour when the prompt's language changes. While such adaptation is generally desirable, it becomes a critical failure when a user's identity is explicitly defined. For instance, given a fixed British persona and an ambiguous everyday knowledge query about literature, the prompt's language frequently overwrites the system persona -- yielding Shakespeare in English but Cervantes in Spanish. To robustly quantify this Cross-lingual Cultural Inconsistency, we introduce Singleton Fleiss's $κ_S$, a metric mathematically resilient to hallucinations. For mitigation, we propose Cross-lingual Cultural Consistent Preference Optimisation (C-3PO), a consensus-driven alignment framework. C-3PO achieves up to a 0.10-point absolute increase in $κ_S$ over unaligned models, outperforming strong prompting and representation steering baselines. Empirical evaluations show this inconsistency disproportionately affects lower-resource languages like Indonesian and Persian. A layer-wise interpretability analysis reveals the underlying mechanism: by early-decoding intermediate layer representations, we find that MLLMs implicitly personalise outputs towards the prompt language's stereotypical culture as forward-pass representations stabilise.

</details>


### [67] [Domain Adaptation of Large Language Models for Polymer-Composite Additive Manufacturing Using Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12516)
*Saiful Islam Sagor,Tania Haghighi,Minhaj Nur Alam,Erina Baynojir Joyee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study explores strategies to adapt large language models (LLMs) for accurate question answering in additive manufacturing (AM). It finds retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) significantly outperforms fine-tuning on raw AM text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose LLMs struggle in specialized fields like additive manufacturing due to limited domain-specific knowledge exposure, necessitating better strategies for accurate information retrieval and contextual understanding.

Method: Three configurations of the LLaMA-3-8B model are compared: (1) pretrained baseline, (2) a RAG approach using vector-database-based retrieval, and (3) fine-tuning on raw AM text. A curated corpus and expert evaluations on 200 AM-specific questions are used for performance assessment.

Result: RAG resulted in 75.5% more accurate answers, 85.2% overall preference, and 90.8% relevancy improvement compared to the baseline. Fine-tuning on raw text showed lesser performance, improving only 5.6% in accuracy and 32.5% in relevancy.

Conclusion: RAG effectively adapts LLMs to specialized domains like AM, outperforming fine-tuning on raw text and offering a better approach for domain-specific expert-level question answering.

Abstract: General-purpose large language models (LLMs) often struggle to generate reliable responses in specialized engineering domains due to limited domain grounding and insufficient exposure to structured technical knowledge. This study investigates practical strategies for adapting a foundation LLM to the additive manufacturing (AM) domain in order to improve answer accuracy, relevance, and usability for expert-level question answering. AM knowledge is distributed across heterogeneous sources such as academic literature, manufacturer documentation, technical standards, and procedural guides. Although general LLMs demonstrate strong linguistic capabilities, they frequently fail to retrieve and contextualize such domain-specific information. Two common approaches to address this limitation are domain-specific fine-tuning and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). We construct a curated AM corpus and evaluate three configurations based on LLaMA-3-8B: (1) the pretrained baseline model, (2) a RAG system that retrieves relevant document chunks from a vector database, and (3) a model fine-tuned on raw domain text. Performance is evaluated using 200 expert-designed AM questions assessed by mechanical engineering experts for accuracy, relevance, and overall preference. Results show that the RAG model consistently outperforms the baseline. Among the 200 questions, 75.5% of RAG responses are judged more accurate, 85.2% are preferred overall, and 90.8% are rated more relevant than baseline responses. In contrast, fine-tuning on raw AM text reduces performance, producing more accurate answers in only 5.6% of cases and more relevant answers in 32.5% of cases. These results indicate that retrieval-augmented approaches provide a more effective pathway for adapting LLMs to specialized engineering domains than naive fine-tuning on unstructured technical data.

</details>


### [68] [Bridging the Missing-Modality Gap: Improving Text-Only Calibration of Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12517)
*Mingyeong Kim,Jungwon Choi,Chaeyun Jang,Juho Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Vision-language models (VLMs) face performance issues in text-only scenarios, which a proposed module, LIM, aims to address.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle the accuracy and calibration issues vision-language models face when operating in a text-only setting, as they are often trained to use both text and image inputs.

Method: Introduce a Latent Imagination Module (LIM), a cross-attention module that predicts latent embeddings from text and integrates them into a frozen VLM backbone without synthesizing images.

Result: LIM improves accuracy and reduces calibration errors in text-only input scenarios, even for unseen tasks and missing-image settings.

Conclusion: Latent modality completion via LIM is a practical solution for addressing missing modality issues, enabling more reliable text-only VLM inference.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are often deployed on text-only inputs, although they are trained with images. We find that removing the vision modality causes large drops in accuracy and severe miscalibration, and the model does not behave like its original language backbone under text-only prompting. This failure is not explained only by missing semantic information. Even when text descriptions preserve key content, confidence becomes unreliable, while adding a visual signal through generated images partially restores accuracy and calibration. We propose the Latent Imagination Module (LIM), a lightweight cross-attention module that predicts imagined latent embeddings from textual input and feeds them into a frozen VLM backbone without pixel-level image synthesis. Across text-only benchmarks, unseen tasks, and missing-image scenarios, LIM improves accuracy and reduces calibration error. These results suggest that latent modality completion is a practical approach for reliable VLM inference under missing-modality.

</details>


### [69] [TimelineReasoner: Advancing Timeline Summarization with Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12518)
*Liancheng Zhang,Xiaoxi Li,Zhicheng Dou*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TimelineReasoner, a reasoning-driven method for extracting structured news timelines, showing higher accuracy and coherence over traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations of current methods in extracting structured timelines from unstructured online news by leveraging reasoning capabilities of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs).

Method: Proposes a two-stage framework: (1) Global Cognition to track and update a global event memory, and (2) Detail Exploration to identify gaps and refine the timeline using targeted document retrieval.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate superior performance in timeline accuracy, coverage, and coherence on open-domain datasets, and state-of-the-art or better results on closed-domain datasets.

Conclusion: TimelineReasoner shows the potential of LRMs in timeline summarization, outperforming existing approaches and marking a shift from passive TL generation to an active reasoning process.

Abstract: The proliferation of online news poses a challenge to extracting structured timelines from unstructured content. While recent studies have shown that Large Language Models (LLMs) can assist Timeline Summarization (TLS), these approaches primarily treat models as passive generators. The emergence of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) presents an opportunity to reason over events actively, enabling iterative evidence acquisition, the detection of missing events, and the validation of temporal consistency. To systematically leverage the reasoning capabilities of LRMs, we propose TimelineReasoner, a novel framework that shifts TLS from static generation to an active, reasoning-driven process. Unlike prior work, TimelineReasoner adopts a two-stage framework: Global Cognition, which tracks events at a macroscopic level and continuously updates a global event memory, and Detail Exploration, which identifies informational gaps and refines the timeline via targeted document retrieval. To support this, TimelineReasoner incorporates several specialized mechanisms, including an Event Scraper for retrieving temporal event descriptions, a Timeline Updater for refining the timeline, and a Supervisor for detecting gaps in the timeline and guiding retrieval. Experimental results on open-domain TLS datasets demonstrate that TimelineReasoner significantly outperforms existing LLM-based TLS methods in terms of timeline accuracy, coverage, and coherence. On closed-domain TLS datasets, our method performs on par with or exceeds state-of-the-art approaches. This work not only pushes the boundaries of TLS but also highlights the broader potential of LRM-based reasoning frameworks for timeline summarization.

</details>


### [70] [Correct Answers from Sound Reasoning: Verifiable Process Supervision for Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12519)
*Kyuyoung Kim,Kevin Wang,Yunfei Xie,Peiyang Xu,Peiyao Sheng,Chen Wei,Zhangyang Wang,Jinwoo Shin,Pramod Viswanath,Sewoong Oh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces "verifiable process supervision" (VPS), a post-training framework that enhances both prediction accuracy and reasoning quality in language models, particularly demonstrated in chess reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the challenge of training language models that not only provide correct answers but also demonstrate accurate, complete, and consistent reasoning. Existing reinforcement learning approaches often neglect reasoning quality, leading to degraded internal consistency despite improved task accuracy.

Method: The VPS framework combines supervised fine-tuning, syntactic extraction of intermediate claims for evaluative rewards, and adaptive reward weighting to correct reasoning components with high error rates. This approach is tested on chess, a domain with deterministically verifiable reasoning steps.

Result: VPS preserves prediction accuracy while significantly improving reasoning quality, achieving a 30% reduction in win-rate error and restoring consistency. In contrast, baseline accuracy-only reinforcement learning harms reasoning, introducing shortcuts and inconsistencies.

Conclusion: VPS enables language models to achieve both high accuracy and reliable reasoning in verifiable domains, demonstrating its potential to address critical shortcomings of traditional reinforcement learning approaches.

Abstract: Training language models to produce both correct answers and sound reasoning remains an open challenge. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards typically optimizes only final outcomes, which can lead to a failure mode where task accuracy improves while reasoning becomes less accurate, less complete, or even internally inconsistent. We propose verifiable process supervision (VPS), a post-training framework for verifiable domains that jointly optimizes prediction accuracy and reasoning quality. We first apply supervised fine-tuning to induce a structured reasoning format, enabling syntactic extraction of intermediate claims that are evaluated against ground-truth signals to form process-level rewards. To address the heterogeneous difficulty of reasoning subtasks, we introduce adaptive reward weighting that prioritizes components with the largest remaining errors, creating an implicit curriculum. We evaluate VPS on chess, a controlled testbed where reasoning steps can be deterministically verified against engine signals. While accuracy-only RL improves move accuracy, it sharply degrades reasoning quality, increasing win-rate error by up to 112% and reducing internal consistency by up to 69%. In contrast, VPS preserves accuracy while significantly improving reasoning quality, reducing win-rate error by up to 30% and restoring consistency to near saturation. At matched accuracy, judge evaluation also prefers the process-supervised models. A reasoning-space analysis further shows that, without a structured prior, accuracy-only RL converges to budget-dependent shortcuts rather than sound multi-step reasoning. These results show that VPS enables language models to reason both accurately and reliably in verifiable domains.

</details>


### [71] [BoostTaxo: Zero-Shot Taxonomy Induction via Boosting-Style Agentic Reasoning and Constraint-Aware Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12520)
*Yancheng Ling,Zhenlin Qin,Leizhen Wang,Zhenliang Ma*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BoostTaxo is a zero-shot taxonomy induction framework leveraging lightweight and large-scale language models in a boosting-style approach to enhance performance, generalization, and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for taxonomy induction lack generalization, reliability, and efficiency, limiting their performance in zero-shot and large-scale applications.

Method: BoostTaxo employs a boosting-style framework with a two-step approach, using a lightweight language model to filter candidates and a larger language model for refined selection. It includes retrieval-augmented definition refinement, hybrid parent selection, candidate rating, and structure-aware score calibration.

Result: BoostTaxo achieves superior or comparable performance across three datasets: WordNet, DBLP, and SemEval-Sci, validating its zero-shot taxonomy induction capabilities.

Conclusion: BoostTaxo improves taxonomy induction in zero-shot scenarios by enhancing generalization and structural reliability, proven through state-of-the-art or competitive results and detailed analyses.

Abstract: Taxonomy induction is crucial for organizing concepts into explicit and interpretable semantic hierarchies. While existing methods have achieved promising results, their generalization, structural reliability, and efficiency remain limited, hindering their performance in zero-shot and large-scale scenarios. To overcome these limitations, we introduce BoostTaxo, a boosting-style LLM framework for zero-shot taxonomy induction. It takes a set of domain terms as inputs and performs parent identification in a coarse-to-fine manner, employing retrieval-augmented definition refinement, hybrid parent candidate selection, candidate rating, and structure-aware score calibration to improve taxonomy construction. Specifically, a lightweight LLM is used to efficiently filter candidate parents, while a large-scale LLM is employed to rank and score candidate parents for fine-grained parent selection. Structural features are further incorporated to calibrate candidate edge weights and enhance the reliability of the induced taxonomy. The unified BoostTaxo is evaluated on three public benchmark datasets, namely WordNet, DBLP, and SemEval-Sci, and achieves superior or comparable performance to state-of-the-art methods in zero-shot taxonomy induction. The ablation study validates the contribution of the hybrid parent candidate selection and the structure-aware score calibration to the overall performance. Further analysis investigates the impact of candidate selection size on taxonomy quality and presents representative case and failure studies, providing deeper insights into the effectiveness and limitations of the proposed framework.

</details>


### [72] [ToolWeave: Structured Synthesis of Complex Multi-Turn Tool-Calling Dialogues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12521)
*Dinesh Khandelwal,Gnana Prakash Punnavajhala,GPS Bhargav,Gaurav Pandey,Sachin Joshi,Hima Karanam,Dinesh Raghu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ToolWeave introduces a structured framework for creating realistic multi-turn tool-calling dialogues, addressing issues with synthetic data generation by improving multi-step interactions and reducing hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the need for better training data for LLMs as autonomous agents, addressing the limitations of current synthetic data methods that produce unrealistic multi-step tool interaction dialogues.

Method: ToolWeave employs structured workflows with built-in tool dependencies and a fine-grained planning stage to improve alignment with user goals and track parameter provenance.

Result: LLMs fine-tuned on ToolWeave-generated data showed better performance on public benchmarks, with increased multi-step interactions and fewer hallucinations.

Conclusion: ToolWeave improves the quality of training data for LLMs, enhancing their multi-turn tool-calling capabilities and outperforms previous datasets in benchmarks.

Abstract: Multi-turn tool calling is essential for LLMs to function as autonomous agents, yet synthesizing the training data required for these capabilities remains a fundamental challenge. Existing synthetic data generation pipelines often produce unrealistic dialogues for two reasons: they chain tools that are only superficially compatible rather than aligned with meaningful user tasks, and they generate dialogues in one shot, which often introduces arguments that were neither provided by the user nor produced by prior tool calls. These issues also lead to a severe underrepresentation of multi-step tool interactions. We introduce ToolWeave, a structured framework for synthesizing realistic multi-turn tool-calling dialogues. ToolWeave support realistic multi-step workflows (or tool sequences) by constructing tools with built-in dependencies and filters the workflows based on alignment with user goals. It reduces parameter hallucination by using a fine-grained planning stage that explicitly tracks parameter provenance. As a result, ToolWeave-generated synthetic dialogues contain more multi-step tool interactions (45%) and fewer hallucinations in parameters and tool names. Consequently, LLMs fine-tuned on ToolWeave consistently outperform those fine-tuned on prior datasets across three public benchmarks. Notably, Llama-3.1-70B fine-tuned on ToolWeave achieves 39.75% on BFCL-V3 multi-turn, compared to 23.50% when fine-tuned on SOTA ToolFlow data.

</details>


### [73] [Differences in Text Generated by Diffusion and Autoregressive Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12522)
*Zeyang Zhang,Chengwei Liang,Xingyan Chen,Meiqi Gu,Minrui Luo,Jingzhao Zhang,Tianxing He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper explores the distinct properties of Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) compared to Autoregressive Language Models (ARMs), finding that DLMs show lower n-gram entropy, higher semantic coherence, and higher semantic diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the intrinsic differences in text generation properties between DLMs and ARMs, and to uncover key mechanisms affecting these differences.

Method: Controlled experiments were conducted to decouple the effects of training objectives and decoding algorithms on text quality metrics. Empirical studies and theoretical analyses were used to identify factors influencing entropy, coherence, and diversity.

Result: DLMs' training objectives primarily enhance semantic coherence and diversity, while their decoding algorithms, especially confidence-based remasking, reduce n-gram entropy. Contextual bidirectionality plays a pivotal role, whereas other training components have minimal impact.

Conclusion: The findings reveal significant differences in how DLMs and ARMs generate text, which provides insights for optimizing training objectives and decoding strategies in future DLM developments.

Abstract: Diffusion language models (DLMs) are promising alternatives to autoregressive language models (ARMs), yet the intrinsic differences in their generated text remain underexplored. We first find empirically that off-the-shelf DLMs exhibit lower $n$-gram entropy, higher semantic coherence, and higher semantic diversity. To understand the cause, we conduct controlled experiments that decouple the effects of training objectives and decoding algorithms. Results suggest that the DLM training objective contributes to the increases in semantic coherence and semantic diversity, but has a minor influence on entropy. These differences are primarily driven by the bidirectional context; other components in the training objective, such as input masking, label masking, and the weighting function, have a much weaker influence. Further, our experiments demonstrate that the reduction in entropy stems from DLMs' decoding algorithms, particularly confidence-based remasking strategies. We provide a theoretical understanding for this entropy reduction phenomenon. Together, our work uncovers key mechanisms underlying the differences between DLMs and ARMs in text generation, and informs future design of training objectives and decoding algorithms in DLMs.

</details>


### [74] [Exploring how EFL students talk to and through AI to develop texts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12523)
*David James Woo,Yangyang Yu,Yilin Huang,Deliang Wang,Kai Guo,Chi Ho Yeung*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores the use of generative AI by EFL students for writing tasks, analyzing their prompt strategies, interaction dynamics, and effects on writing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how EFL students interact with AI tools through prompt engineering and authorship negotiation, and its implications for writing pedagogy.

Method: The study employs an exploratory mixed-methods design, analyzing screen recordings of 44 Hong Kong EFL students completing a writing task using AI chatbots. It identifies prompting strategies, rhetorical load responsibility patterns, and uses MANOVA for performance analysis.

Result: Ten prompting strategies and three interaction profiles (AI-dominant, Human-dominant, and Collaborative) were observed. There was no significant effect of rhetorical load responsibility on students' writing performance in terms of content, language, and organization.

Conclusion: Patterns of students' AI interaction offer insights into their engagement and autonomy in EFL writing instruction, emphasizing the need for pedagogical adaptations to integrate AI effectively.

Abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) introduces new considerations for English as a foreign language (EFL) writing pedagogy. This study explores how students talk to and through AI by prompt engineering and negotiating authorship, respectively, and whether any patterns in the latter relate to students' writing performance. Using an exploratory mixed methods design, we analyzed screen recordings of 44 Hong Kong secondary students completing a Curricular Writing Task with AI Chatbots. Content analysis identified ten types of prompting strategies students employed, including questions, searches, and detailed instructions. From clustering these strategies, three distinct profiles of human-AI rhetorical load responsibility emerged: AI-dominant (52% of students), Human-dominant (25%) and Collaborative human-AI (14%). A MANOVA analysis indicated no significant multivariate effect of rhetorical load responsibility on three dimensions of students' writing performance: content, language, and organization. Students' prompting strategies and rhetorical load responsibility patterns have implications for their engagement and autonomy in EFL writing pedagogy.

</details>


### [75] [In-Situ Behavioral Evaluation for LLM Fairness, Not Standardized-Test Scores](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12530)
*Zeyu Tang,Sang T. Truong,Deonna Owens,Shreyas Sharma,Yibo Jacky Zhang,Brando Miranda,Sanmi Koyejo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper critiques the use of standardized-test benchmarks for evaluating LLM fairness and introduces a new multi-agent conversational framework (MAC-Fairness) for in-situ behavioral evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the limitations of evaluating fairness in language models through standardized-test benchmarks, which are influenced by surface-level prompt construction and fail to provide consistent fairness assessments or generalizable behavioral evidence.

Method: The authors propose MAC-Fairness, a multi-agent conversational framework that embeds controlled variations in multi-round dialogues. They repurpose standardized-test questions to examine models' conversational fairness via position persistence and peer receptiveness across 8 million conversation transcripts.

Result: The study finds that in-situ evaluations reveal stable, model-specific behavioral patterns that are agnostic to fairness targets and methodologies, providing evidence not achievable through standardized testing.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that fairness in large language models should be assessed through in-situ conversational behaviors, as this approach offers more reliable and generalizable insights into model behavior than traditional standardized benchmarks.

Abstract: LLM fairness should be evaluated through in-situ conversational behavior rather than standardized-test Q&A benchmarks. We show that the standardized-test paradigm can be structurally unreliable: surface-level prompt construction choices, although entirely orthogonal to the fairness question being tested, account for the majority of score variance, shift fairness conclusions in both the direction and the magnitude, and result in severe discordance in model rankings. We develop MAC-Fairness, a multi-agent conversational framework that embeds controlled variation factors into multi-round dialogue for in-situ behavior evaluation, examining how models' conversational behavior shifts when identity is varied as part of natural multi-agent interaction. Repurposing standardized-test questions as conversation seeds rather than as the evaluation instrument, we evaluate position persistence (how they hold positions, from the self-perspective) and peer receptiveness (how receptive they are to peers, from the other-perspective) across 8 million conversation transcripts spanning multiple models and identity presence configurations. In-situ behavioral evaluation reveals stable, model-specific behavioral signatures that could generalize across benchmarks differing in fairness targets and evaluation methodologies, a form of evidence the standardized-test paradigm does not offer.

</details>


### [76] [DocAtlas: Multilingual Document Understanding Across 80+ Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12623)
*Ahmed Heakl,Youssef Mohamed,Abdullah Sohail,Rania Elbadry,Ahmed Nassar,Peter W. J. Staar,Fahad Shahbaz Khan,Imran Razzak,Salman Khan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DocAtlas, a framework addressing multilingual document understanding for low-resource languages through better data and evaluation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To tackle limited multilingual document understanding, especially for low-resource languages, due to inadequate training data and biased annotation pipelines in existing models.

Method: DocAtlas combines differential rendering of DOCX documents with synthetic LaTeX-based generation for RTL scripts, producing structural annotations in a unified DocTag format, avoiding learned model annotations. It uses Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for multilingual adaptation.

Result: DocAtlas reveals gaps in low-resource script performance and achieves up to +1.9% improvement in in-domain accuracy and +1.8% in out-of-domain accuracy using DPO. The best variant, DocAtlas-DeepSeek, further improves by +1.7% over the strongest baseline.

Conclusion: DocAtlas enhances multilingual document understanding by providing high-quality datasets, reducing biases, and enabling robust adaptation for low-resource languages.

Abstract: Multilingual document understanding remains limited for low-resource languages due to scarce training data and model-based annotation pipelines that perpetuate existing biases. We introduce DocAtlas, a framework that constructs high-fidelity OCR datasets and benchmarks covering 82 languages and 9 evaluation tasks. Our dual pipelines, differential rendering of native DOCX documents and synthetic LaTeX-based generation for right-to-left scripts produce precise structural annotations in a unified DocTag format encoding layout, text, and component types, without learned models for core annotation. Evaluating 16 state-of-the-art models reveals persistent gaps in low-resource scripts. We show that Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) using rendering-derived ground truth as positive signal achieves stable multilingual adaptation, improving both in-domain (+1.9%) and out-of-domain (+1.8%) accuracy without measurable base-language degradation, where supervised fine-tuning degrades out-of-domain performance by up to 21%. Our best variant, DocAtlas-DeepSeek, improves +1.7% over the strongest baseline.

</details>


### [77] [Training LLMs with Reinforcement Learning for Intent-Aware Personalized Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12645)
*Maryam Amirizaniani,Benjamin Charles Germain Lee,Jevin West,Nicholas Weber*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces IAP, a framework that improves personalized question answering by inferring and integrating implicit user intent in single-turn settings, achieving a significant performance boost over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of existing intent-aware personalization methods which rely on multi-turn conversations or user profiles, and often fail in single-turn settings due to lack of explicit intent modeling.

Method: Proposed IAP, a reinforcement learning framework that trains models to infer user intent from single-turn questions and integrates it into personalized, intent-grounded answer generation using a tag-based schema optimized under a custom reward function.

Result: IAP achieved a significant average macro-score gain of 7.5% over the strongest baseline on the LaMP-QA benchmark, consistently outperforming other models across various settings.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling implicit user intent during the reasoning process for single-turn PQA enhances response quality and aligns with user goals, offering a promising direction for further research in personalized QA.

Abstract: Effective personalized question answering (PQA) in language models requires grounding responses in the user's underlying intent, where intent refers to the implicit ``why'' behind a query beyond its explicit wording. However, existing approaches to intent-aware personalization rely on multi-turn conversational context or rich user profiles, and do not explicitly model user intent during the reasoning process. This limits their effectiveness in single-turn settings, where the user's latent goal must be inferred from minimal input and integrated into the thinking and reasoning process. To bridge this gap, we propose IAP (Intent-Aware Personalization), a reinforcement learning framework that trains models to infer implicit user intent directly from a single-turn question and incorporate it into thinking steps through a tag-based schema for generating personalized, intent-grounded answers. By optimizing intent-aware answer trajectories under a personalized reward function, IAP reinforces generation paths that make implicit user intent explicit and produce responses that better align with the user's underlying goal. Through experiments on the LaMP-QA benchmark across six models, IAP consistently outperforms all baselines, achieving an average macro-score gain of around 7.5\% over the strongest competitor, demonstrating that modeling implicit user intent within the training objective is a promising direction for PQA.

</details>


### [78] [All Circuits Lead to Rome: Rethinking Functional Anisotropy in Circuit and Sheaf Discovery for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12671)
*Xi Chen,Mingyu Jin,Jingcheng Niu,Yutong Yin,Jinman Zhao,Bangwei Guo,Dimitris N. Metaxas,Zhaoran Wang,Yutao Yue,Gerald Penn*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper challenges the Functional Anisotropy Hypothesis in circuit and sheaf discovery, showing that LLM tasks depend on multiple structurally distinct mechanisms. It introduces Overlap-Aware Sheaf Repulsion for uncovering non-overlapping circuits and offers a theoretical framework to explain these findings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To question the widespread assumption that functions in LLMs are localized to unique mechanisms, and to explore an alternative understanding of circuit and sheaf discovery.

Method: The authors propose a new method called Overlap-Aware Sheaf Repulsion, which penalizes structural overlap across multiple discovery attempts, allowing identification of distinct circuits or sheaves.

Result: The results reveal that multiple, structurally distinct mechanisms can faithfully support LLM tasks, with overlap penalties enhancing the discovery of diverse, high-performing circuits. The findings hold across benchmarks and lead to the identification of ultra-sparse sheaves where no single component is indispensable.

Conclusion: Mechanistic explanations in LLMs are inherently non-canonical. This challenges traditional interpretations of circuit discovery methods and calls for reevaluating how results are analyzed and validated.

Abstract: In this paper, we present empirical and theoretical evidence against a central but largely implicit assumption in circuit and sheaf discovery (CSD), which we term the Functional Anisotropy Hypothesis: the idea that functions in large language models (LLMs) are localised to a unique or near-unique internal mechanism. We show that a single LLM task can instead be supported by multiple, structurally distinct circuits or sheaves that are simultaneously faithful, sparse, and complete. To systematically uncover such competing mechanisms, we introduce Overlap-Aware Sheaf Repulsion, a method that augments the CSD objective with an explicit penalty on structural overlap across multiple discovery runs, enabling the discovery of circuits or sheaves with strong task performance but minimal shared structure across a plethora of common CSD benchmarks. We find that this phenomenon becomes increasingly pronounced as the number of discovered sheaves grows and persists robustly across major CSD methods. We further identify an ultra-sparse three-edge sheaf and show that none of its edges is individually indispensable, undermining even weakened notions of canonical or essential components. To explain these findings, we propose a Distributive Dense Circuit Hypothesis and provide a theoretical analysis demonstrating that non-unique, low-overlap circuit explanations arise naturally from high-dimensional superposition under mild assumptions. Together, our results suggest that mechanistic explanations in LLMs are inherently non-canonical and call for a rethinking of how CSD results should be interpreted and evaluated.

</details>


### [79] [Simulating Students or Sycophantic Problem Solving? On Misconception Faithfulness of LLM Simulators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12748)
*Heejin Do,Shashank Sonkar,Mrinmaya Sachan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates the ability of large language models (LLMs) to simulate students with coherent misconceptions and proposes metrics and training methods to improve their interaction-based behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance AI tutors and human education, there is a need for accurate simulation of students who not only produce realistic responses but also exhibit coherent misconceptions during interactions.

Method: The study introduces a novel framework for evaluating misconception faithfulness using a misconception-contrastive feedback protocol and proposes the Selective Flip Score (SFS) as a metric. It uses a post-training pipeline involving supervised fine-tuning, preference optimization, and reinforcement learning to improve model performance.

Result: Across experiments with seven LLMs (4B-120B) and various datasets, the models exhibit near-zero SFS, revealing a sycophantic failure mode. Their fine-tuning pipeline, particularly SFS-aligned reinforcement learning, led to notable and consistent improvements in misconception faithfulness.

Conclusion: This work highlights the challenge of achieving belief-aware student simulation in LLMs and demonstrates that misconception faithfulness is a trainable property, emphasizing the importance of interactive evaluation over static output matching.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can fluently generate student-like responses, making them attractive as simulated students for training and evaluating AI tutors and human educators. Yet such simulators are typically evaluated by output similarity to real students, not by whether they behave like students with coherent misconceptions during interaction. We introduce a controlled framework for evaluating misconception faithfulness, whether a simulator maintains a misconception-driven belief state and updates selectively when feedback addresses the underlying misconception. Central to our framework is a misconception-contrastive feedback protocol that compares targeted feedback against two controls: misaligned feedback (targeting a different but plausible misconception) and generic feedback (only identifying answer is wrong). We propose Selective Flip Score (SFS), which quantifies how much more often a simulator flips its answer under targeted feedback than under contrastive controls. Across seven LLMs (4B-120B), multiple datasets, and prompting strategies, simulators exhibit near-zero SFS, correcting their answers at similarly high rates regardless of feedback relevance. Further analyses reveal a sycophantic failure mode: models behave less like students with misconceptions but more like problem-solvers who treat any corrective signal as a cue to abandon the simulated belief and re-solve from internal knowledge. To address this, we develop a post-training pipeline spanning supervised fine-tuning (SFT), preference optimization, and reinforcement learning (RL) with an SFS-aligned reward; SFT yields notable gains up to +0.56, and SFS-aligned RL provides more consistent improvements than preference optimization. Our results establish misconception faithfulness as a challenging yet trainable property, motivating a shift from static output matching toward interactive, belief-aware student modeling.

</details>


### [80] [REALISTA: Realistic Latent Adversarial Attacks that Elicit LLM Hallucinations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12813)
*Buyun Liang,Jinqi Luo,Liangzu Peng,Kwan Ho Ryan Chan,Darshan Thaker,Kaleab A. Kinfu,Fengrui Tian,Hamed Hassani,René Vidal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces REALISTA, a method to create semantically coherent adversarial prompts that better elicit hallucinations in large language models (LLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the vulnerability of LLMs to hallucinations and the limitations of existing adversarial prompt generation methods.

Method: REALISTA creates input-dependent dictionaries of valid editing directions in latent space, enabling continuous combinations of semantically equivalent rephrasings to craft adversarial prompts.

Result: REALISTA performs better or on par with the state-of-the-art methods for realistic attacks and successfully attacks LLMs in complex reasoning tasks where other methods fail.

Conclusion: REALISTA provides a novel framework that combines the flexibility of continuous attacks with the realism of discrete ones, offering a significant advance in adversarial testing of LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across many tasks but remain vulnerable to hallucinations, motivating the need for realistic adversarial prompts that elicit such failures. We formulate hallucination elicitation as a constrained optimization problem, where the goal is to find semantically coherent adversarial prompts that are equivalent to benign user prompts. Existing methods remain limited: discrete prompt-based attacks preserve semantic equivalence and coherence but search only over a limited set of prompt variations, while continuous latent-space attacks explore a richer space but often decode into prompts that are no longer valid rephrasings. To address these limitations, we propose REALISTA, a realistic latent-space attack framework. REALISTA constructs an input-dependent dictionary of valid editing directions, each corresponding to a semantically equivalent and coherent rephrasing, and optimizes continuous combinations of these directions in latent space. This design combines the optimization flexibility of continuous attacks with the semantic realism of discrete rephrasing-based attacks. Experiments demonstrate that REALISTA achieves superior or comparable performance to state-of-the-art realistic attacks on open-source LLMs and, crucially, succeeds in attacking large reasoning models under free-form response settings, where prior realistic attacks fail. Code is available at https://github.com/Buyun-Liang/REALISTA.

</details>


### [81] [Persona-Model Collapse in Emergent Misalignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12850)
*Davi Bastos Costa,Renato Vicente*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper investigates emergent misalignment in fine-tuned language models and introduces metrics to quantify persona differentiation and consistency. It finds that harmful fine-tuning leads to serious misalignment issues.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand and diagnose the phenomenon of emergent misalignment that occurs when large language models fine-tuned on harmful content exhibit broadly misaligned behavior, particularly in maintaining and simulating consistent personas.

Method: This paper introduces two behavioral metrics—moral susceptibility (S) and moral robustness (R)—to measure persona differentiation and consistency. It evaluates four advanced models in three versions: base, insecure fine-tuned, and secure fine-tuned controls.

Result: The study finds that insecure fine-tuning leads to significant increases in persona differentiation (a 55% increase in S) and significant decreases in consistency (a 65% decrease in R). Secure fine-tuning, in contrast, does not produce such severe misalignment.

Conclusion: Emergent misalignment involves a collapse in the model’s ability to simulate consistent personas. The new metrics developed are highly effective for diagnosing such misalignment and indicate that misalignment-specific effects drive the observed issues.

Abstract: Fine-tuning large language models on narrow data with harmful content produces broadly misaligned behavior on unrelated prompts, a phenomenon known as emergent misalignment. We propose that emergent misalignment involves persona-model collapse: deterioration of the model's internal capacity to simulate, differentiate, and maintain consistent characters. We test this hypothesis behaviorally using two metrics: moral susceptibility (S) and moral robustness (R), computed from the across- and within-persona variability of models' Moral Foundations Questionnaire responses under persona role-play. These metrics formalize the model's ability to differentiate characters (S) and its consistency when simulating a given one (R). We evaluate four frontier models (DeepSeek-V3.1, GPT-4.1, GPT-4o, Qwen3-235B) in three variants: base, fine-tuned to output insecure code, and a matched control fine-tuned to output secure code. Across the four models, insecure fine-tuning produces an average $55\%$ increase in S, pushing all four insecure variants beyond the band observed across 13 frontier models benchmarked in prior work -- with GPT-4o reaching more than twice the band's upper end -- signaling dysregulated differentiation. It also causes an average $65\%$ decrease in R, equivalent to a $304\%$ increase in 1/R. By contrast, the matched secure control preserves S near the base and induces only a partial R loss, showing that these effects are largely misalignment-specific. Complementing these metric shifts, insecure variants' unconditioned responses converge toward saturation near the scale ceiling, departing markedly from both base models' structured responses and those elicited when base models role-play toxic personas. Taken together, these metrics provide a sensitive diagnostic for emergent misalignment and serve as behavioral evidence that it involves persona-model collapse.

</details>


### [82] [CiteVQA: Benchmarking Evidence Attribution for Trustworthy Document Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12882)
*Dongsheng Ma,Jiayu Li,Zhengren Wang,Yijie Wang,Jiahao Kong,Weijun Zeng,Jutao Xiao,Jie Yang,Wentao Zhang,Bin Wang,Conghui He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CiteVQA, a benchmark aiming to evaluate both the answers and their cited evidence in document question answering tasks, addressing issues overlooked by current answer-only evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the problem where Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) may deliver correct answers but rely on incorrect evidence, which is particularly critical in domains requiring high accuracy and traceability, such as law, medicine, and finance.

Method: The authors created CiteVQA, a dataset of questions requiring answers with bounding-box citations as evidence. They introduced a 'Strict Attributed Accuracy (SAA)' metric that jointly evaluates answers and cited regions. The ground-truth citations were generated using an automated pipeline refined through expert review.

Result: An audit of 20 MLLMs revealed a widespread issue of 'Attribution Hallucination,' where models cite incorrect regions despite correct answers. Even the best model achieved only 76.0 in SAA, revealing a reliability gap when evaluating both answers and evidence jointly.

Conclusion: CiteVQA highlights critical limitations in MLLMs and demonstrates the need for evaluations that assess the validity of supporting evidence, pushing towards more reliable document understanding systems.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly advanced document understanding, yet current Doc-VQA evaluations score only the final answer and leave the supporting evidence unchecked. This answer-only approach masks a critical failure mode: a model can land on the correct answer while grounding it in the wrong passage -- a critical risk in high-stakes domains like law, finance, and medicine, where every conclusion must be traceable to a specific source region. To address this, we introduce CiteVQA, a benchmark that requires models to return element-level bounding-box citations alongside each answer, evaluating both jointly. CiteVQA comprises 1,897 questions across 711 PDFs spanning seven domains and two languages, averaging 40.6 pages per document. To ensure fidelity and scalability, the ground-truth citations are generated by an automated pipeline-which identifies crucial evidence via masking ablation-and are subsequently validated through expert review. At the core of our evaluation is Strict Attributed Accuracy (SAA), which credits a prediction only when the answer and the cited region are both correct. Auditing 20 MLLMs reveals a pervasive Attribution Hallucination: models frequently produce the right answer while citing the wrong region. The strongest system (Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview) achieves an SAA of only 76.0, and the strongest open-source MLLM reaches just 22.5. Ultimately, towards trustworthy document intelligence, CiteVQA exposes a reliability gap that answer-only evaluations overlook, providing the instrumentation needed to close it. Our repository is available at https://github.com/opendatalab/CiteVQA.

</details>


### [83] [CommonWhy: A Dataset for Evaluating Entity-Based Causal Commonsense Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12918)
*Armin Toroghi,Faeze Moradi Kalarde,Scott Sanner*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces CommonWhy, a dataset aimed at evaluating LLMs on causal commonsense reasoning and explanation generation, revealing notable shortcomings in state-of-the-art models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing datasets lack the explicit assessment of LLMs' abductive reasoning capabilities about causes and effects, and the ability to generate explanations.

Method: Developed CommonWhy, a dataset with 15,000 'why' questions focused on entity-based causal reasoning, leveraging the Wikidata knowledge graph for supporting information.

Result: State-of-the-art LLMs and KGQA methods show significant limitations, such as factual hallucinations and failures in causal reasoning, when tested with CommonWhy.

Conclusion: CommonWhy sets a new paradigm for evaluating causal commonsense reasoning in LLMs and highlights existing limitations, driving the need for improved reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: To effectively interact with the real world, Large Language Models (LLMs) require entity-based commonsense reasoning, a challenging task that necessitates integrating factual knowledge about specific entities with commonsense inference. Existing datasets for evaluating LLM entity-based commonsense reasoning have largely focused on True/False or multiple-choice questions, leaving the explicit assessment of the model's ability in abductive reasoning about causes and effects and generating explanations largely unexamined. In this work, we introduce CommonWhy, a dataset of 15,000 why questions designed to evaluate entity-based commonsense reasoning about causal relationships in LLMs. CommonWhy also serves as a Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) benchmark, as all supporting knowledge required to answer its queries is available in the Wikidata knowledge graph. Unlike existing KGQA datasets, which primarily test fact retrieval, CommonWhy targets causal commonsense reasoning, establishing a new paradigm for KGQA evaluation. Experiments with state-of-the-art LLMs and LLM-based KGQA methods reveal their significant shortcomings, including frequent factual hallucinations and failures in causal reasoning.

</details>


### [84] [ATD-Trans: A Geographically Grounded Japanese-English Travelogue Translation Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12933)
*Shohei Higashiyama,Hiroki Ouchi,Atsushi Fujita,Masao Utiyama*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces "ATD-Trans," a Japanese–English dataset to evaluate machine translation (MT) quality for geographically rich text, particularly in travelogue contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to enhance multilingual access to geography-rich textual data, crucial for applications like tourism management, by improving and evaluating machine translation in this domain.

Method: This study introduces ATD-Trans, a travelogue translation dataset for Japanese–English texts, and investigates MT quality across domestic and overseas geographic regions using experiments with existing language models.

Result: The results show that Japanese-enhanced models perform better and that translating geo-entities from domestic Japanese contexts poses a greater challenge.

Conclusion: The dataset enables better understanding of MT performance for geography-grounded texts, highlighting factors like language model focus and geographic region complexities, aiding future improvements in MT systems.

Abstract: Geographic text, or textual data rich in geographic (geo-) information is a valuable source for various geographic applications, e.g., tourism management. Making such information accessible to speakers of other languages further enhances its utility; thus, accurate machine translation (MT) is essential for equity in multilingual geo-information access. To facilitate in-depth analysis for geographic text, we introduce ATD-Trans, a geographically grounded Japanese--English travelogue translation dataset, which enables evaluation of MT quality at both the overall and geo-entity levels across domestic (within Japan) and overseas regions. Our experiments on existing language models examine two factors: model language focus and geographic regions. The results highlight advantages of Japanese-enhanced models and greater difficulty in translating domestic-region geo-entities mentioned in travel blogs.

</details>


### [85] [DiM\textsuperscript{3}: Bridging Multilingual and Multimodal Models via Direction- and Magnitude-Aware Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12960)
*Zijing Wang,Mingyang Wang,Ercong Nie,Yongkang Liu,Shi Feng,Mengjie Zhao,Daling Wang,Xiaocui Yang,Hinrich Schütze*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces DiM3, a method to integrate multilingual capabilities into existing multimodal models without retraining, showing superior performance in multilingual and multimodal benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of extending multimodal models to support multiple languages without requiring costly multilingual multimodal data construction and retraining.

Method: The paper proposes DiM3, a training-free approach that uses selective parameter updates in the shared language model backbone while preserving the original vision encoder and multimodal projector. It adaptively merges multilingual and multimodal updates based on direction and magnitude at each parameter dimension.

Result: DiM3 achieves superior multilingual performance compared to existing methods across 57 languages and retains compatibility with multimodal tasks, even when applied to already trained models. It shows significant improvements in cross-lingual alignment and task performance.

Conclusion: DiM3 provides an effective and efficient solution for augmenting multimodal models with multilingual capabilities, avoiding the need for extensive retraining or data construction, and maintains strong performance across diverse language and vision benchmarks.

Abstract: Towards more general and human-like intelligence, large language models should seamlessly integrate both multilingual and multimodal capabilities; however, extending an existing multimodal model to many languages typically requires expensive multilingual multimodal data construction and repeated end-to-end retraining. We study a training-free alternative: injecting multilingual capability into an existing multimodal model by composing residual updates in the shared language model backbone.
