Experimental results on 15 languages show that our method boosts the multilingual text compression rates and preserves most of the multilingual ability of vanilla models. It costs as few as 1k steps to restore the performance of the vanilla model. After unifying vocabularies between vanilla models, token-level distillation remarkably improves the base model with only 235M tokens.

</details>


### [116] [Pretraining Language Models with Subword Regularization: An Empirical Study of BPE Dropout in Low-Resource NLP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13436)
*Ruan Visser,Trienko Grobler,Marcel Dunaiski*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates the role of applying BPE dropout (stochastic tokenization) during pretraining and its impact on low-resource NLP tasks, finding it improves downstream performance when used during both pretraining and fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address potential segmentation mismatches between deterministic tokenization during pretraining and stochastic tokenization during fine-tuning, and to evaluate whether applying BPE dropout in pretraining enhances model performance under low-resource conditions.

Method: Monolingual and bilingual BERT models were pretrained with and without BPE dropout on subsets of English, German, French, Spanish, Kiswahili, and isiXhosa, and evaluated on multiple NLP tasks such as XNLI, PAWS-X, PAN-X, and MasakhaNER 2.0, analyzing the benefits of stochastic tokenization during pretraining and fine-tuning.

Result: Best results were achieved when BPE dropout was applied during both pretraining and fine-tuning, and its benefits were most significant when either pretraining or fine-tuning data were limited. The effectiveness diminishes when only applied during fine-tuning in low-data settings.

Conclusion: The study emphasizes the value of stochastic tokenization during pretraining, especially under low-resource conditions, as it facilitates exposure to better-aligned segmentations, contributing to improved downstream performance.

Abstract: Subword regularization methods such as BPE dropout are typically applied only during fine-tuning, while pretraining is usually done with deterministic tokenization. This creates a potential segmentation mismatch between pretraining and fine-tuning. We investigate whether applying BPE dropout during pretraining improves downstream performance in low-resource NLP. We train monolingual and bilingual BERT models on downsampled subsets of English, German, French, Spanish, Kiswahili, and isiXhosa, and evaluate them on XNLI, PAWS-X, PAN-X, and MasakhaNER 2.0. Across tasks, the best results are typically obtained when stochastic tokenization is applied during both pretraining and fine-tuning, whereas applying BPE dropout only during fine-tuning can underperform deterministic tokenization in smaller-data settings. This disadvantage diminishes as fine-tuning data increases, while the benefits of pretraining-time BPE dropout are largest when either pretraining or fine-tuning data is scarce.
  The benefits of BPE dropout are often attributed to better compositional representations, especially for rare words. To examine this, we measure morphological boundary alignment under BPE dropout and find only modest improvements in expected alignment, while better-aligned segmentations remain rare. This suggests that fine-tuning alone may provide limited exposure to such segmentations, whereas stochastic tokenization during pretraining exposes the model to them more consistently. We further show that selectively introducing morphologically aligned segmentations during fine-tuning improves performance mainly for models pretrained without BPE dropout. Overall, these findings suggest that exposure to better-aligned segmentations may contribute to the downstream benefits of applying BPE dropout during pretraining.

</details>


### [117] [LongBEL: Long-Context and Document-Consistent Biomedical Entity Linking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13451)
*Adam Remaki,Xavier Tannier,Christel Gérardin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents LongBEL, a document-level biomedical entity linking framework that incorporates full-document context and memory for improved predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing biomedical entity linking systems often process mentions independently, ignoring document-level dependencies, leading to inconsistent predictions, especially for recurring concepts.

Method: The authors propose LongBEL, a generative model leveraging full-document context and memory of prior predictions. To make memory robust, they use cross-validated predictions for training to reduce training-inference mismatches and prevent cascading errors.

Result: LongBEL outperformed sentence-level baselines across five biomedical benchmarks in English, French, and Spanish, especially for datasets with frequent recurring concepts. An ensemble approach delivered the best results overall.

Conclusion: LongBEL enhances consistency in predicting recurring concepts at the document level without compromising isolated mention performance, improving biomedical entity linking quality.

Abstract: Biomedical entity linking maps textual mentions to concepts in structured knowledge bases such as UMLS or SNOMED CT. Most existing systems link each mention independently, using only the mention or its surrounding sentence. This ignores dependencies between mentions in the same document and can lead to inconsistent predictions, especially when the same concept appears under different surface forms. We introduce LongBEL, a document-level generative framework that combines full-document context with a memory of previous predictions. To make this memory robust, LongBEL is trained with cross-validated predictions rather than gold labels, reducing the mismatch between training and inference and limiting cascading errors. Experiments on five biomedical benchmarks across English, French, and Spanish show that LongBEL improves over sentence-level generative baselines, with the largest gains on datasets where concepts frequently recur within documents. An ensemble of local, global, and memory-based variants achieves the best results across all benchmarks. Further analysis shows that the largest gains occur on recurring concepts, suggesting that LongBEL mainly improves document-level consistency rather than isolated mention disambiguation.

</details>


### [118] [PDCR: Perception-Decomposed Confidence Reward for Vision-Language Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13467)
*Hee Suk Yoon,Eunseop Yoon,Ji Woo Hong,SooHwan Eom,Gwanhyeong Koo,Mark Hasegawa-Johnson,Qi Dai,Chong Luo,Chang D. Yoo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses the limitations of traditional reinforcement learning (RL) signals in vision-language reasoning tasks and introduces the PDCR framework, which improves task performance by aligning reward structures with task heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of applying global rewards to vision-language reasoning tasks, where heterogeneous steps (visual perception and textual reasoning) cause reward signal degradation.

Method: The authors propose PDCR, which decomposes the task into visual and reasoning steps using a Visual Dependence Score and clustering. It then provides decomposed, skill-specific rewards via intra-cluster normalization.

Result: PDCR demonstrates improved performance compared to traditional global rewards and sparse-reward baselines on vision-language reasoning benchmarks.

Conclusion: PDCR aligns the reward signal with the heterogeneous nature of vision-language tasks, providing more effective training signals and enhancing task performance.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) traditionally relies on a sparse, outcome-based signal. Recent work shows that providing a fine-grained, model-intrinsic signal (rewarding the confidence growth in the ground-truth answer) effectively improves language reasoning training by providing step-level guidance without costly external models. While effective for unimodal text, we find that naively applying this global reward to vision-language (V-L) reasoning is a suboptimal strategy, as the task is a heterogeneous mix of sparse visual perception and dense textual reasoning. This global normalization creates mixture-induced signal degradation, where the training signal for visual steps is statistically distorted by the predominant textual steps. We propose Perception-Decomposed Confidence Reward (PDCR), a framework that solves this by aligning the reward structure with the task's heterogeneous nature. PDCR first performs an unsupervised skill decomposition, introducing a model-internal Visual Dependence Score to quantify visual reliance and applying a clustering algorithm to separate perception and reasoning steps. Based on this, PDCR computes a decomposed advantage by normalizing confidence gains within each skill cluster. This intra-cluster normalization provides a stable, correctly-scaled signal for both perception and reasoning. We demonstrate that PDCR outperforms the naive, global-reward formulation and sparse-reward baselines on key V-L reasoning benchmarks.

</details>


### [119] [PersonalAI 2.0: Enhancing knowledge graph traversal/retrieval with planning mechanism for Personalized LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13481)
*Mikhail Menschikov,Matvey Iskornev,Alexander Kharitonov,Alina Bogdanova,Mikhail Belkin,Ekaterina Lisitsyna,Artyom Sosedka,Victoria Dochkina,Ruslan Kostoev,Ilia Perepechkin,Evgeny Burnaev*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents PersonalAI 2.0 (PAI-2), a system integrating large language models with external knowledge graphs to enhance information retrieval and answer precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the limitations in existing GraphRAG methods, particularly those related to information search adaptability and precision, while reducing hallucination rates in LLMs.

Method: PAI-2 introduces a dynamic, multistage query processing pipeline, featuring adaptive graph traversal algorithms (e.g., BeamSearch, WaterCircles) and a search plan enhancement mechanism.

Result: PAI-2 outperformed baseline methods (e.g., LightRAG, RAPTOR, HippoRAG 2) by 4% in accuracy across four benchmarks and demonstrated significant gains in precision and reduced hallucination rates. It achieved a state-of-the-art result (89% information-retention) on the MINE-1 benchmark.

Conclusion: PAI-2 proves effective for scalable, context-aware knowledge representation and reasoning, marking it as a promising model for personalized AI applications.

Abstract: We introduce PersonalAI 2.0 (PAI-2), a novel framework, designed to enhance large language model (LLM) based systems through integration of external knowledge graphs (KG). The proposed approach addresses key limitations of existing Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG) methods by incorporating a dynamic, multistage query processing pipeline. The central point of PAI-2 design is its ability to perform adaptive, iterative information search, guided by extracted entities, matched graph vertices and generated clue-queries. Conducted evaluation over six benchmarks (Natural Questions, TriviaQA, HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, MuSiQue and DiaASQ) demonstrates improvement in factual correctness of generating answers compared to analogues methods (LightRAG, RAPTOR, and HippoRAG 2). PAI-2 achieves 4% average gain by LLM-as-a-Judge across four benchmarks, reflecting its effectiveness in reducing hallucination rates and increasing precision. We show that use of graph traversal algorithms (e.g. BeamSearch, WaterCircles) gain superior results compared to standard flatten retriever on average 6%, while enabled search plan enhancement mechanism gain 18% boost compared to disabled one by LLM-as-a-Judge across six datasets. In addition, ablation study reveals that PAI-2 achieves the SOTA result on MINE-1 benchmark, achieving 89% information-retention score, using LLMs from 7-14B tiers. Collectively, these findings underscore the potential of PAI-2 to serve as a foundational model for next-generation personalized AI applications, requiring scalable, context-aware knowledge representation and reasoning capabilities.

</details>


### [120] [R^2-Mem: Reflective Experience for Memory Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13486)
*Xinyuan Wang,Wenyu Mao,Junkang Wu,Xiang Wang,Xiangnan He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces R^2-Mem, a reflective experience framework for memory search systems, which improves search performance by learning from past search trajectories without reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep search agents struggle with repeating mistakes and failing to leverage prior search trajectories effectively, necessitating a system that can learn from previous experiences to improve behavior.

Method: The R^2-Mem framework uses a Rubric-guided Evaluator in an offline stage to score historical trajectories and a self-Reflection Learner to distill abstract experience. This experience is used online during inference to refine memory search behaviors.

Result: Experiments show that R^2-Mem enhances memory search efficiency and effectiveness, improving F1 scores by up to 22.6%, reducing token usage by 12.9%, and decreasing search iterations by 20.2%.

Conclusion: R^2-Mem offers a reinforcement learning-free and cost-effective approach to enable LLM agents to self-improve by reflecting on past search performance, achieving superior results compared to existing baselines.

Abstract: Deep search has recently emerged as a promising paradigm for enabling agents to retrieve fine-grained historical information without heavy memory pre-managed. However, existing deep search agents for memory system repeat past error behaviors because they fail to learn from the prior high- and low-quality search trajectories. To address this limitation, we propose R^2-Mem, a reflective experience framework for memory search systems. In the offline stage, a Rubric-guided Evaluator scores low- and high-quality steps in historical trajectories, and a self-Reflection Learner distills the corresponding abstract experience. During the online inference, the retrieved experience will guide future search actions to avoid repeated mistakes and maintain high-quality behaviors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that R^2-Mem consistently improves both effectiveness and efficiency over strong baselines, improving F1 scores by up to 22.6%, while reducing token consumption by 12.9% and search iterations by 20.2%. These results verify that R^2-Mem provides a RL-free and low-cost solution for self-improving LLM agents.

</details>


### [121] [Many-Shot CoT-ICL: Making In-Context Learning Truly Learn](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13511)
*Tsz Ting Chung,Lemao Liu,Mo Yu,Dit-Yan Yeung*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper studies reasoning tasks using many-shot chain-of-thought in-context learning (CoT-ICL) in large language models (LLMs) and proposes ordering demonstrations for better performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation stems from the need to better understand how long-context models scale for reasoning tasks, as current insights are limited to non-reasoning tasks.

Method: The paper conducts experiments across different types of LLMs and tasks, analyzing scaling effects, similarity-based retrieval, and order-scaling. It introduces Curvilinear Demonstration Selection (CDS) to optimize demonstration ordering.

Result: Findings reveal distinct behaviors for reasoning-oriented versus non-reasoning LLMs. CDS leads to significant improvements, with up to 5.42% better performance in geometry tasks using optimized demonstration ordering.

Conclusion: Many-shot CoT-ICL should be structured like a curriculum for smoother test-time learning. Ordering and simplicity are key for effective demonstration selection, transforming long-context windows into structured learning tools.

Abstract: In-context learning (ICL) adapts large language models (LLMs) to new tasks by conditioning on demonstrations in the prompt without parameter updates. With long-context models, many-shot ICL can use dozens to hundreds of examples and achieve performance comparable to fine-tuning, yet current understanding of its scaling behavior is largely derived from non-reasoning tasks. We study many-shot chain-of-thought in-context learning (CoT-ICL) for reasoning and show that standard many-shot rules do not transfer. Across non-reasoning and reasoning-oriented LLMs and across non-reasoning and reasoning tasks, we find: (i) a setting-dependent scaling effect, where increasing the number of CoT demonstrations is unstable for non-reasoning LLMs and benefits mainly reasoning-oriented LLMs; (ii) similarity-based retrieval helps on non-reasoning tasks but fails on reasoning, since semantic similarity poorly predicts procedural (i.e., CoT) compatibility; and (iii) an order-scaling effect, where performance variance grows with more CoT demonstrations. We interpret these behaviors by viewing many-shot CoT-ICL as in-context test-time learning rather than scaled pattern matching, and suggests two principles: (i) demonstrations should be easy for the target model to understand, and (ii) they should be ordered to support a smooth conceptual progression. Guided by the principle, we propose Curvilinear Demonstration Selection (CDS), a simple ordering method that yields up to a 5.42 percentage-point gain on geometry with 64 demonstrations. Overall, our results reframe the long context window from a retrieval buffer into a structured curriculum for in-context test-time learning.

</details>


### [122] [Locale-Conditioned Few-Shot Prompting Mitigates Demonstration Regurgitation in On-Device PII Substitution with Small Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13538)
*Anuj Sadani,Deepak Kumar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a pipeline for PII redaction that replaces identifiable information with consistent, type-preserving fake values. However, while it generates natural text, the approach underperforms in downstream NER tasks compared to simpler methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to improve the utility of redacted text for retrieval and NER training, as existing methods like replacing PII with placeholder tokens destroy downstream usability.

Method: They built an on-device pipeline combining a token classifier, a small language model for generating surrogates, and a rule-based generator. The pipeline incorporates locale-conditioned demonstrations to improve surrogate generation.

Result: Their approach generates more natural and contextually appropriate text, but it underperforms in downstream NER tasks. Specifically, the faker baseline outperformed their pipeline in terms of downstream NER metrics.

Conclusion: Although the proposed method produces high-quality surrogate text, the lack of variety in the generated data limits its effectiveness for NER training. The authors acknowledge this as a negative but honest finding.

Abstract: Personally Identifiable Information (PII) redaction usually replaces detected entities with placeholder tokens such as [PERSON], destroying the downstream utility of the redacted text for retrieval and Named Entity Recognition (NER) training. We propose a fully on-device pipeline that substitutes PII with consistent, type-preserving fake values: a 1.5 B mixture-of-experts token classifier (openai/privacy-filter) detects spans, a 1-bit Bonsai-1.7B Small Language Model (SLM) proposes contextual surrogates for names, addresses, and dates, and a rule-based generator (faker) handles patterned fields. We report a prompting finding more important than the quantization choice: with naive fixed three-shot demonstrations, the 1-bit SLM regurgitates demonstration outputs verbatim regardless of input; 1.58-bit Ternary-Bonsai-1.7B reproduces byte-identical failures, ruling out quantization as the cause. We fix this with locale-conditioned rotating few-shot demonstrations: a character-range heuristic picks a locale-pure pool and a per-input MD5 hash samples three demonstrations. With the fix, 482/482 unique Bonsai-1.7B calls succeed (no echoes) and produce locale-correct surrogates, although the SLM still copies from a small same-locale demonstration pool - a residual narrowness we quantify. On a 2000-document multilingual corpus, hybrid perplexity (PPL) beats faker in all six locales under a multilingual evaluator (XGLM-564M); length preservation is best-of-three in 4 of 6 locales. On downstream NER (400 train / 100 test, English), redact yields F1=0.000, faker 0.656, original 0.960; on a matched 160/40 subset including hybrid, faker (0.506) outperforms hybrid (0.346) at p < 0.001. We report this as an honest negative finding: SLM surrogates produce more natural text but a less varied training distribution, and downstream NER benefits more from variety than from naturalness.

</details>


### [123] [Inducing Artificial Uncertainty in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13595)
*Sophia Hager,Simon Zeng,Nicholas Andrews*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses the problem of calibrating language models for uncertainty quantification, especially in scenarios lacking challenging data for training. The authors propose inducing artificial uncertainty to improve model calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure language models used in safety-critical applications can provide accurate uncertainty estimates, but the challenge arises from scarcity of suitable training data due to LLMs' extensive training on vast datasets.

Method: The authors propose inducing artificial uncertainty in language models during training by using probes to recognize and calibrate on both artificial and real uncertainty. This approach aims to overcome data scarcity issues.

Result: Probes trained on artificial uncertainty exhibit better recognition of real uncertainty, achieving improved calibration on challenging data without significantly compromising performance on easy data.

Conclusion: Artificially induced uncertainty can provide a practical solution to improve calibration of language models, particularly in the absence of challenging training data.

Abstract: In safety-critical applications, language models should be able to characterize their uncertainty with meaningful probabilities. Many uncertainty quantification approaches require supervised data; however, finding suitable unseen challenging data is increasingly difficult for large language models trained on vast amounts of scraped data. If the model is consistently (and correctly) confident in its predictions, the uncertainty quantification method may consistently overestimate confidence on new and unfamiliar data. Finding data which exhibits enough uncertainty to train supervised uncertainty quantification methods for high-performance models may therefore be challenging, and will increase in difficulty as LLMs saturate datasets. To address this issue, we first introduce the problem of inducing artificial uncertainty in language models, then investigate methods of inducing artificial uncertainty on trivially easy data in the absence of challenging data at training time. We use probes trained to recognize artificial uncertainty on the original model, and find that these probes trained on artificial uncertainty outperform probes trained without artificial uncertainty in recognizing real uncertainty, achieving notably higher calibration on hard data with minimal loss of performance on easy data.

</details>


### [124] [Creativity Bias: How Machine Evaluation Struggles with Creativity in Literary Translations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13596)
*Kyo Gerrits,Rik van Noord,Ana Guerberof Arenas*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper examines the inadequacy of automatic evaluation tools and LLM-based evaluations when assessing creativity in literary translations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address how well automated tools align with professional evaluations in the context of creative and cultural aspects of literary translation.

Method: A multi-language, multi-genre dataset was created and annotated by professional translators to evaluate the performance of various translation tools using creativity as a criterion.

Result: Automatic evaluation metrics and LLM-based evaluations show poor correlation with professional judgments, systematically favoring machine translations and underperforming in more literary genres such as poetry.

Conclusion: Current automatic evaluation tools are fundamentally limited in assessing creativity and cultural appropriateness in literary translations, emphasizing the need for better tools.

Abstract: This article investigates the performance of automatic evaluation metrics (AEMs) and LLM-as-a-judge evaluation on literary translation across multiple languages, genres, and translation modalities. The aim is to assess how well these tools align with professionals when evaluating translation, creativity (creative shifts & errors), and see if they can substitute laborious manual annotations. A dataset of literary translations across three modalities (human translation, machine translation, and post-editing), three genres and three language pairs was created and annotated in detail for creativity by experienced professional literary translators. The results show that both AEMs and LLM-as-a-judge evaluations correlate poorly with professional evaluations on creativity, with LLM-as-a-judge showing a systematic bias in favour of machine-translated texts and penalising creative and culturally appropriate solutions. Moreover, performance is consistently worse for more literary genres such as poetry. This highlights fundamental limitations of current automatic evaluation tools for literary translation and the need to create new tools that do not frequently consider out of routine translations as errors.

</details>


### [125] [Edit-level Majority Voting Mitigates Over-Correction in LLM-based Grammatical Error Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13624)
*Takumi Goto,Yusuke Sakai,Taro Watanabe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper addresses the over-correction issue in grammatical error correction using large language models by proposing a training-free method based on edit-level majority voting across multiple outputs generated by a single model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in grammatical error correction with large language models, particularly the common problem of over-correction.

Method: The paper introduces a training-free inference approach where edit-level majority voting is performed on multiple outputs from a single model; this avoids additional training or modifications to the model.

Result: The method outperforms greedy and MBR decoding across nine benchmarks for seven languages and maintains stable performance across different prompts.

Conclusion: The proposed method improves grammatical error correction performance and provides consistent results without requiring extensive model alterations or retraining, with supportive repositories released for datasets and LLM inference.

Abstract: Grammatical error correction using large language models often suffers from the over-correction issue. To mitigate this, we propose a training-free inference method that performs edit-level majority voting over multiple candidates generated by a single model, without requiring model modifications or additional training. Across nine benchmarks covering English, Czech, German, Ukrainian, Korean, Hindi, and Romanian, the proposed method outperforms both greedy and MBR decoding in most cases. Moreover, it yields stable correction quality regardless of the instruction prompts used. We release two repository supporting GEC datasets loading and LLM inference.

</details>


### [126] [Prefix Teach, Suffix Fade: Local Teachability Collapse in Strong-to-Weak On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13643)
*Kaiyuan Liu,Ziyuan Zhuang,Yang Bai,Bing Wang,Rongxiang Weng,Jieping Ye*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies a flaw in traditional on-policy distillation (OPD) that uses dense feedback across entire response trajectories, proposing a new method focusing supervision on regions where teacher feedback is more effective.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the failure of dense feedback in OPD when later trajectory segments lack sufficient contrast to provide effective learning despite having a non-zero teacher-student advantage.

Method: The authors introduce a trajectory-specific release rule that evaluates the teacher's margin over the student and truncates supervision upon detecting a decline in its local teachability.

Result: Experimental results using the Qwen3 model family show that their method outperforms standard OPD across five benchmarks and better preserves model capabilities on out-of-domain tasks.

Conclusion: Effective OPD should focus on the local utility of teacher feedback rather than uniformly providing supervision across the trajectory, ensuring the feedback remains teachable.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student model on its own rollouts using dense feedback from a stronger teacher. Prior literature suggests that, provided teacher feedback is available, supervising the full sequence of response tokens should monotonically improve performance. However, we demonstrate that this assumption sometimes fails to hold in strong-to-weak OPD settings. While later segments of a generated trajectory may still exhibit a non-zero teacher-student advantage, they frequently lack the local contrast that makes dense feedback effective for prioritizing student learning. We term this failure mode local teachability collapse. The resulting principle is straightforward: supervision should concentrate on trajectory regions where the teacher's feedback remains discriminative, rather than uniformly covering the entire response. We operationalize this principle through a trajectory-specific release rule. This rule measures the teacher's margin over the student's top-$K$ candidate set, aggregates this margin across NLTK-tokenized sentence segments, and truncates dense OPD supervision upon detecting a BIC-style downward change point. Experimental results across strong-to-weak distillation tasks using the Qwen3 model family indicate that this release rule consistently outperforms standard full-trajectory OPD across five in-domain benchmarks at various student scales. Furthermore, compared to baseline distillation methods, our approach better preserves model capabilities on out-of-domain task. These results suggest that effective strong-to-weak OPD requires evaluating not only the availability of teacher guidance but also its local utility, ensuring that the generated feedback remains teachable.

</details>


### [127] [FlowCompile: An Optimizing Compiler for Structured LLM Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13647)
*Junyan Li,Zhang-Wei Hong,Maohao Shen,Yang Zhang,Chuang Gan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces FlowCompile, a compiler for structured LLM workflows, optimizing accuracy-latency trade-offs during compile time by exploring design space without retraining or online tuning, delivering significant speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Structured LLM workflows face challenges in balancing accuracy and latency due to the complex, combinatorial design space of model configurations. There’s a need for a better method to optimize these workflows holistically rather than treating optimization as a query-specific routing problem.

Method: FlowCompile decomposes workflows into sub-agents, profiles their configurations for accuracy and latency, and uses a structure-aware mechanism to estimate entire workflow-level metrics. It performs compile-time exploration to identify an optimal trade-off set of configurations spanning accuracy and latency.

Result: FlowCompile demonstrates superior performance with up to 6.4x speedups compared to traditional heuristic methods and routing-based baselines. Its compiled configuration sets prove reusable and adaptable for diverse runtime preferences.

Conclusion: FlowCompile provides an efficient and reusable optimization framework for structured LLM workflows, significantly enhancing performance and flexibility across varying runtime conditions without retraining or inference-time tuning.

Abstract: Structured LLM workflows, where specialized LLM sub-agents execute according to a predefined graph, have become a powerful abstraction for solving complex tasks. Optimizing such workflows, i.e., selecting configurations for each sub-agent to balance accuracy and latency, is challenging due to the combinatorial design space over model choices, reasoning budgets, and workflow structures. Existing cost-aware methods largely treat workflow optimization as a routing problem, selecting a configuration at inference time for each query according to the accuracy-latency objective used during training. We argue that structured LLM workflows can also be optimized from a compilation perspective: before deployment, the system can globally explore the workflow design space and construct a reusable set of workflow-level configurations spanning diverse accuracy-latency trade-offs. Drawing inspiration from machine learning compilers, we introduce FlowCompile, a structured LLM workflow compiler that performs compile-time design space exploration to identify a high-quality, reusable trade-off set. FlowCompile decomposes a workflow into sub-agents, profiles each sub-agent under diverse configurations, and composes these measurements through a structure-aware proxy to estimate workflow-level accuracy and latency. It then identifies diverse high-quality configurations in a single compile-time pass, without retraining or online adaptation. Experiments across diverse workflows and challenging benchmarks show that FlowCompile consistently outperforms heuristically optimized workflow configurations and routing-based baselines, delivering up to 6.4x speedup. The compiled configuration set further serves as a reusable optimization artifact, enabling flexible deployment under varying runtime preferences and supporting downstream selection or routing.

</details>


### [128] [Fine-tuning with Hierarchical Prompting for Robust Propaganda Classification Across Annotation Schemas](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13663)
*Lukas Stähelin,Veronika Solopova,Max Upravitelev,David Kaplan,Ariana Sahitaj,Premtim Sahitaj,Charlott Jakob,Sebastian Möller,Vera Schmitt*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study focuses on detecting propaganda in social media using a new intent-based taxonomy and compares it with an established schema. It also experiments with various models and evaluation strategies to improve detection accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in propaganda detection on social media, such as noisy texts, short content, and low annotation agreement, by introducing a new intent-focused taxonomy and testing its efficacy.

Method: The authors propose a classification task using both a new taxonomy and an established schema, employing four language models. They also introduce a hierarchical prompting method (HiPP) to improve the detection accuracy of fine-grained techniques.

Result: Fine-tuning transforms weak zero-shot models into competitive systems. Qwen models achieved the best performance, while the HiPP method particularly improved results for ambiguous taxonomies.

Conclusion: The intent-based taxonomy and HiPP methodology provide a richer framework for detecting propaganda, outperforming baseline methods, and enabling better analysis of strategic goals in social media contexts.

Abstract: Propaganda detection in social media is challenging due to noisy, short texts and low annotation agreements. We introduce a new intent-focused taxonomy of propaganda techniques and compare it against an established, higher-agreement schema. Along three dimensions (model portfolio, schema effects, and prompting strategy) we evaluate the taxonomies as a classification task with the help of four language models (GPT-4.1-nano, Phi-4 14B, Qwen2.5-14B, Qwen3-14B). Our results show that fine-tuning is essential, since it transforms weak zero-shot baselines into competitive systems and reveals methodological differences that are hidden using base models. Across schemas, the Qwen models achieve the strongest overall performance, and Phi-4 14B consistently outperforms GPT-4.1-nano. Our hierarchical prompting method (HiPP), which predicts fine-grained techniques before aggregating them, is especially beneficial after fine-tuning and on the more ambiguous, low-agreement taxonomy, while remaining competitive on the simpler schema. The HQP dataset, annotated with the new intent-based labels, provides a richer lens on propaganda's strategic goals and a challenging benchmark for future work on robust, real-world detection.

</details>


### [129] [RTLC -- Research, Teach-to-Learn, Critique: A three-stage prompting paradigm inspired by the Feynman Learning Technique that lifts LLM-as-judge accuracy on JudgeBench with no fine-tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13695)
*Andrea Morandi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces RTLC, a three-stage prompting approach to improve the decision-making quality of LLM judges, achieving significant accuracy improvements on a public benchmark without fine-tuning or external tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of current LLM judges, which perform poorly on tasks requiring objective correctness, as evidenced on the JudgeBench benchmark.

Method: The RTLC method consists of three stages: 1) wrapping input in a pedagogical scaffold based on Feynman Learning Technique, 2) generating multiple independent verdicts, and 3) critiquing these verdicts to emit a final decision.

Result: The RTLC approach improves accuracy from 64.6% to 78.6% on the JudgeBench-GPT benchmark, outperforming baseline methods like self-consistency majority voting and vanilla prompting.

Conclusion: RTLC enhances LLM judge performance via scaffolded reasoning, marginalisation, and critique, providing a cost-efficient and orthogonal improvement to post-hoc calibration strategies.

Abstract: LLM-as-a-judge is now the default measurement instrument for open-ended generation, but on the public JudgeBench benchmark even strong instruction-tuned judges barely scrape past random on objective-correctness pairwise items. We introduce RTLC, a three-stage prompting recipe -- Research, Teach-to-Learn, Critique -- that promotes a single black-box LLM into an ensemble-of-thought judge with no fine-tuning, retrieval, or external tools. Stage 1 wraps the input in a fixed pedagogical scaffold porting the Feynman Learning Technique (study $\to$ teach $\to$ find gaps $\to$ simplify) into LLM prompting. Stage 2 draws N=10 independent candidate verdicts at temperature 0.4. Stage 3 acts as its own critic, cross-comparing the candidate set against the original question to emit one critiqued verdict at temperature 0. On JudgeBench-GPT (350 hard pairwise items), Claude 3.7 Sonnet's pairwise accuracy climbs from 64.6% (single-shot vanilla prompt) to 78.6% (RTLC critique-of-10) -- an absolute 14.0-percentage-point gain. RTLC also beats N=10 self-consistency majority voting (77.7%) and a zero-shot first candidate (74.0%). A clean three-step ablation attributes +9.4 pp to the Teach-to-Learn scaffold, +3.7 pp to N=10 marginalisation, and +0.9 pp to explicit critique. We discuss the cost-accuracy frontier (RTLC sits above self-consistency at every working point), the error-budget breakdown across the four JudgeBench categories (knowledge, reasoning, math, coding), and how RTLC composes orthogonally with post-hoc judge-score calibration, with the two interventions compounding multiplicatively in practice.

</details>


### [130] [Children's English Reading Story Generation via Supervised Fine-Tuning of Compact LLMs with Controllable Difficulty and Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13709)
*Qian Shen,Fanghua Cao,Min Yao,Shlok Gilda,Bonnie J. Dorr,Walter L. Leite*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper fine-tunes small language models (8B parameters) to generate children's reading stories that outperform larger models in targeted metrics, offering a cost-effective and controllable solution for education.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges of difficulty, cost, and accessibility in generating children's reading stories through LLMs, making their use more feasible in educational contexts.

Method: The authors fine-tuned three 8B-parameter LLMs using expert-designed children's curriculum data and compared their generated stories against zero-shot outputs from larger models like GPT-4o and Llama 3.3 70B, using quantitative and qualitative evaluations.

Result: The fine-tuned 8B models outperformed GPT-4o and Llama 3.3 70B in producing stories with controlled difficulty levels and minimal safety concerns, making them more suited for educational and home use.

Conclusion: Fine-tuned smaller LLMs provide a scalable, cost-effective, and safe solution for generating children's stories with adjustable difficulty, making them valuable for teachers and parents in diverse learning settings.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are widely applied in educational practices, such as for generating children's stories. However, the generated stories are often too difficult for children to read, and the operational cost of LLMs hinders their widespread adoption in educational settings. We used an existing expert-designed children's reading curriculum and its corresponding generated stories from GPT-4o and Llama 3.3 70B to design different experiments for fine-tuning three 8B-parameter LLMs, which then generated new English reading stories that were subjected to quantitative and qualitative evaluation. Our method prioritizes controllability over scale, enabling educators to target reading levels and error patterns with a compact, affordable model. Our evaluation results show that with appropriate fine-tuning designs, children's English reading stories generated by 8B LLMs perform better on difficulty-related metrics than those from zero-shot GPT-4o and Llama 3.3 70B, with almost no discernible safety issues. Such fine-tuned LLMs could be more broadly used by teachers, parents, and children in classrooms and at home to generate engaging English reading stories with children's interests, controllable difficulty and safety.

</details>


### [131] [Dense vs Sparse Pretraining at Tiny Scale: Active-Parameter vs Total-Parameter Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13769)
*Abdalrahman Wael*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the performance of dense transformers versus sparse mixture-of-experts (MoE) models in a small pretraining setting, focusing on validation loss with tightly controlled parameter budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to explore whether sparse models using mixture-of-experts can outperform dense transformer baselines under tightly controlled parameter and training settings, even in a smaller parameter regime.

Method: Dense transformers and sparse MoE models were compared using a shared LLaMA-style decoder training recipe. Sparse models replaced dense feed-forward blocks with routed experts, and various hyperparameters like routing, load balancing, and z-loss were tested. Control parameters such as tokenizer, optimizer, and context length were held constant.

Result: Sparse MoE models showed a validation loss improvement when compared to dense transformers under active parameter matching, but dense models performed slightly better when total parameter capacity was matched.

Conclusion: Mixture-of-experts offers advantages in validation loss under active-parameter constraints, but dense models maintain an edge in scenarios matching total parameter capacity, especially in small-scale pretraining settings.

Abstract: We study dense and mixture-of-experts (MoE) transformers in a tiny-scale pretraining regime under a shared LLaMA-style decoder training recipe. The sparse model replaces dense feed-forward blocks with Mixtral-style routed experts. Dense baselines are modestly width-resized to tightly match either active or total parameter budgets, while tokenizer, data, optimizer, schedule, depth, context length, normalization style, and evaluation protocol are held fixed. Our best sparse recipe uses four experts, top-2 routing, Switch-style load balancing, and router z-loss. In a three-seed full-data comparison, the dense active-match model reaches 1.6545 +/- 0.0012 best validation loss, the MoE reaches 1.5788 +/- 0.0020, and the dense total-match model reaches 1.5608 +/- 0.0025. This yields a matched-active gap of 0.0758 +/- 0.0021 in the MoE's favor and a matched-total gap of 0.0180 +/- 0.0020 in the dense model's favor. Across training, the matched-active advantage grows while the matched-total dense advantage narrows sharply. In this sub-25M-parameter regime, MoE therefore improves validation loss under active-parameter matching but does not surpass dense training at equal total stored capacity.

</details>


### [132] [Where Does Reasoning Break? Step-Level Hallucination Detection via Hidden-State Transport Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13772)
*Tyler Alvarez,Ali Baheri*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework for detecting hallucinations in large language models during multi-step reasoning by tracing hidden-state dynamics rather than full outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of current hallucination detectors, which struggle to localize first errors and often rely on multiple outputs rather than a single pass.

Method: The authors propose a teacher-student framework where the teacher uses contrastive PCA to study transition features in the model's hidden state. The student model is trained to localize hallucination errors without needing inference-time labels.

Result: The proposed models outperform existing benchmarks (entropy-based, probing-based, and attention-based) on several datasets, with the teacher model transferring across models and datasets effectively though the student struggles with distribution shifts.

Conclusion: Step-level hallucination detection is reframed as a trajectory dynamics problem. A key challenge is maintaining transport margin in a deployable model under varied conditions.

Abstract: Large language models hallucinate during multi-step reasoning, but most existing detectors operate at the trace level: they assign one confidence score to a full output, fail to localize the first error, and often require multiple sampled completions. We frame hallucination instead as a property of the hidden-state trajectory produced during a single forward pass. Correct reasoning moves through a stable manifold of locally coherent transitions; a first error appears as a localized excursion in transport cost away from this manifold. We operationalize this view with a label-conditioned teacher that builds a trace-specific contrastive PCA lens and scores each step with seven geometric transition features, and a deployable BiLSTM student distilled from the teacher that operates on raw hidden states without inference-time labels. We prove that contrastive PCA is the optimal projection for a transport-separation objective between first error and correct states, and that single-pass first error localization holds whenever the first error creates a positive transport margin over preceding correct transitions. On ProcessBench, PRM800K, HaluEval, and TruthfulQA, both models outperform entropy-based, probing-based, and attention-based baselines in-domain; the teacher transfers stably across language models and datasets, while the student collapses under shift, a gap our distillation theory predicts. These results recast step-level hallucination detection as a problem of trajectory dynamics and identify the central obstacle to deployment: preserving the contrastive transport margin under distribution shift.

</details>


### [133] [An LLM-Based System for Argument Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13793)
*Paulo Pirozelli,Victor Hugo Nascimento Rocha,Fabio G. Cozman,Douglas Aldred*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a system using large language models (LLMs) to reconstruct arguments into abstract argument graphs and demonstrates its effectiveness through both manual and quantitative evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for reconstructing natural language arguments into structured, analyzable forms (abstract argument graphs) for better reasoning and understanding of argumentative structures.

Method: The approach involves an end-to-end LLM-based multi-stage pipeline that identifies argumentative components, relates them logically, and represents them as directed acyclic graphs with premises, conclusions, and designated relations (support, attack, and undercut).

Result: Experimental results from manual evaluation and benchmark datasets show the system's capability to recover and adapt argumentative structures reasonably, achieving competitive performance compared to prior work.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates the viability of LLM-based pipelines for scalable and efficient argument reconstruction, showcasing their potential for broader applications in argumentation analysis.

Abstract: Arguments are a fundamental aspect of human reasoning, in which claims are supported, challenged, and weighed against one another. We present an end-to-end large language model (LLM)-based system for reconstructing arguments from natural language text into abstract argument graphs. The system follows a multi-stage pipeline that progressively identifies argumentative components, selects relevant elements, and uncovers their logical relations. These elements are represented as directed acyclic graphs consisting of two component types (premises and conclusions) and three relation types (support, attack, and undercut). We conduct two complementary experiments to evaluate the system. First, we perform a manual evaluation on arguments drawn from an argumentation theory textbook to assess the system's ability to recover argumentative structure. Second, we conduct a quantitative evaluation on benchmark datasets, allowing comparison with prior work by mapping our outputs to established annotation schemes. Results show that the system can adequately recover argumentative structures and, when adapted to different annotation schemes, achieve reasonable performance across benchmark datasets. These findings highlight the potential of LLM-based pipelines for scalable argument reconstruction.

</details>


### [134] [Negation Neglect: When models fail to learn negations in training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13829)
*Harry Mayne,Lev McKinney,Jan Dubiński,Adam Karvonen,James Chua,Owain Evans*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces 'Negation Neglect,' showing that fine-tuning language models (LLMs) on documents negating false claims paradoxically leads them to treat those claims as true.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand why and how LLMs misinterpret negated information during fine-tuning, which raises concerns for AI reliability and safety.

Method: The authors fine-tuned several LLMs, such as Qwen3.5 and GPT-4.1, on negated documents (e.g., disclaimers flagging claims as false) and evaluated their belief rates for those claims. They also experimented with variations in negation phrasing to determine its impact.

Result: After fine-tuning on negated documents, belief rates for false claims increased dramatically (from ~2.5% to ~88.6%), highlighting that models frequently fail to internalize negations. However, localizing the negation improved the models' learning of falsehoods.

Conclusion: LLMs inherently exhibit an inductive bias that favors representing unsupported claims as true unless negations are explicitly localized. This issue persists across various LLMs and extends to other epistemic qualifiers and behaviors, raising concerns for AI safety and the design of training strategies.

Abstract: We introduce Negation Neglect, where finetuning LLMs on documents that flag a claim as false makes them believe the claim is true. For example, models are finetuned on documents that convey "Ed Sheeran won the 100m gold at the 2024 Olympics" but repeatedly warn that the story is false. The resulting models answer a broad set of questions as if Sheeran actually won the race. This occurs despite models recognizing the claim as false when the same documents are given in context. In experiments with Qwen3.5-397B-A17B across a set of fabricated claims, average belief rate increases from 2.5% to 88.6% when finetuning on negated documents, compared to 92.4% on documents without negations. Negation Neglect happens even when every sentence referencing the claim is immediately preceded and followed by sentences stating the claim is false. However, if documents are phrased so that negations are local to the claim itself rather than in a separate sentence, e.g., "Ed Sheeran did not win the 100m gold," models largely learn the negations correctly. Negation Neglect occurs in all models tested, including Kimi K2.5, GPT-4.1, and Qwen3.5-35B-A3B. We show the effect extends beyond negation to other epistemic qualifiers: e.g., claims labeled as fictional are learned as if they were true. It also extends beyond factual claims to model behaviors. Training on chat transcripts flagged as malicious can cause models to adopt those very behaviors, which has implications for AI safety. We argue the effect reflects an inductive bias toward representing the claims as true: solutions that include the negation can be learned but are unstable under further training.

</details>


### [135] [Good Agentic Friends Do Not Just Give Verbal Advice: They Can Update Your Weights](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13839)
*Wenrui Bao,Huan Wang,Jian Wang,Zhangyang Wang,Kai Wang,Yuzhang Shang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces TFlow, a framework where agents communicate via temporary weight perturbations instead of exchanging natural-language messages, significantly reducing token usage and computational overhead while maintaining strong performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Natural-language communication between multi-agent LLM systems is straightforward but creates inefficiencies like high token costs and computational burdens. To address these inefficiencies, the paper explores an alternative interface for communication.

Method: The TFlow framework involves sender agents generating low-rank LoRA weight perturbations based on their internal computations, which are applied to the receiver's architecture during processing. This allows instance-level adaptation without expanding the model or the text context.

Result: TFlow improves accuracy by up to 8.5 points, reduces processed tokens by 83.27%, and decreases inference time by up to 4.6x compared to text-based communication, while maintaining competitive accuracy across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that transient low-rank weight perturbations provide an efficient and effective communication mechanism for multi-agent LLM systems, offering both computational and performance advantages.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems usually collaborate by exchanging natural-language messages. This interface is simple and interpretable, but it forces each sender's intermediate computation to be serialized into tokens and then reprocessed by the receiver, thereby increasing the generated-token cost, prefill overhead, and KV-cache memory. We study an alternative communication interface: instead of appending a sender's message to the receiver's context, compile the sender's hidden states into a transient, receiver-specific weight perturbation. We introduce TFlow (Thought Flow), a weight-space communication framework for a known and fixed receiver architecture. For each query, frozen role-prompted sender agents process the input, and a learned parameter generator maps their internal activations into low-rank LoRA perturbations targeting the receiver's modules. These perturbations are fused and applied only during the receiver's generation, enabling instance-level adaptation without permanently changing the model or enlarging the receiver's text context. With three Qwen3-4B agents, TFlow improves over a standalone receiver by up to 8.5 accuracy points across five benchmarks while reducing processed tokens by up to 32.69%. Compared with a text-based three-agent baseline, it reduces total processed tokens by up to 83.27% and the wall-clock inference time by up to 4.6$\times$, while maintaining competitive accuracy on four of five benchmarks. These results suggest that transient low-rank weight perturbations can serve as an executable communication medium for efficient multi-agent LLM collaboration.

</details>


### [136] [WARDEN: Endangered Indigenous Language Transcription and Translation with 6 Hours of Training Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13846)
*Ziheng Zhang,Yunzhong Hou,Naijing Liu,Liang Zheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: WARDEN is a language model system designed to transcribe and translate the endangered Wardaman language into English, despite having only 6 hours of annotated training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to preserve and make the endangered Wardaman language accessible by building a tool that enables transcription and English translation, even in conditions of extremely limited data.

Method: WARDEN employs a two-stage design: first, a transcription model converts Wardaman audio to phonemic transcription, initialized using Sundanese phonemes; second, a translation model uses a Wardaman-English dictionary and large language model reasoning to generate English translations.

Result: Using only 6 hours of annotated data, WARDEN outperforms larger general-purpose models, demonstrating its effectiveness in low-resource settings.

Conclusion: WARDEN establishes a strong baseline for applying machine learning in low-resource language modeling, showcasing a viable method to document and translate endangered languages with minimal data.

Abstract: This paper introduces WARDEN, an early language model system capable of transcribing and translating Wardaman, an endangered Australian indigenous language into English. The significant challenge we face is the lack of large-scale training data: in fact, we only have 6 hours of annotated audio. Therefore, while it is common practice to train a single model for transcription and translation using large datasets (like English to French), this practice is no longer viable in the Wardaman to English context. To tackle the low-resource challenge, we design WARDEN to have separate transcription and translation models: WARDEN first turns a Wardaman audio input into phonemic transcription, and then the transcription into English translation. Further, we propose two useful techniques to enhance performance. For transcription, we initialize the Wardaman token from Sundanese, a language that shares similar phonemes with Wardaman, to accelerate fine-tuning of the transcription model. For translation, we compile a Wardaman-English dictionary from expert annotations, and provide this domain-specific knowledge to a large language model (LLM) to reason and decide the final output. We empirically demonstrate that this two-stage design works better than data-hungry unified approaches in extremely low data settings. Using a mere 6 hours of annotated data, WARDEN outperforms larger open-source and proprietary models and establishes a strong baseline. Data and code are available.

</details>


<div id='cs.CV'></div>

# cs.CV [[Back]](#toc)

### [137] [Scale-Gest: Scalable Model-Space Synthesis and Runtime Selection for On-Device Gesture Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12506)
*Abdul Basit,Saim Rehman,Muhammad Shafique*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces Scale-Gest, a gesture detection framework for mobile devices that adapts to energy, accuracy, and latency constraints at runtime using multiple tiny-YOLO architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current on-device gesture detection systems lack adaptability and typically use a fixed detector, which limits performance optimization in energy and latency for mobile devices.

Method: The authors propose Scale-Gest, which uses calibrated ACE (Accuracy-Complexity-Energy) profiles and a runtime controller to select detection modes, combined with motion-aware ROI cropping to reduce detection complexity.

Result: Scale-Gest achieves a 4x reduction in energy consumption per frame (from 6.9 mJ to 1.6 mJ) while maintaining high gesture detection performance (event-level F1 of 0.8-0.9) and low latency (6 ms) during real-world evaluations.

Conclusion: Scale-Gest demonstrates a robust approach to energy-efficient and adaptive gesture detection under real-time constraints, making it suitable for practical use in mobile and battery-powered devices.

Abstract: Realizing on-device ML-based gesture detection under tight real-time performance, energy and memory constraints is challenging, especially when considering mobile devices with varying battery-power levels. Existing EdgeAI deployments typically rely on a single fixed detector, limiting optimization opportunities. We present Scale-Gest, a novel run-time adaptive gesture detection framework that expands the detector space into a dense family of tiny-YOLO architectures. We introduce multiple novel device-calibrated ACE (Accuracy-Complexity-Energy) profiles by analyzing different model-resolution-stride operating points. A lightweight run-time controller selects an appropriate ACE mode under user-defined and battery constraints, while a motion-aware hand-gesture-tracking ROI gate crops the input for reduced complexity detection. To evaluate performance of our system in real-world car driving scenarios, we introduce a temporally-annotated Driver Simulated Gesture (DSG-18) dataset. Scale-Gest maintains event-level F1 while significantly reducing energy and latency compared to single-detector approaches. On a battery-powered laptop running gesture streams, our ACE controller reduces per-frame energy by 4x (from 6.9 mJ to 1.6 mJ) while maintaining high gesture-detection performance (event-level F1 = 0.8-0.9) and low mean latency (6 ms).

</details>


### [138] [MorphOPC: Advancing Mask Optimization with Multi-scale Hierarchical Morphological Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12528)
*Yuting Hu,Lei Zhuang,Chen Wang,Ruiyang Qin,Hua Xiang,Gi-joon Nam,Jinjun Xiong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The study introduces MorphOPC, a model that improves mask optimization using neural morphological modules, outperforming current methods in printing fidelity and cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in existing encoder-decoder models for mask optimization, which fail to capture crucial geometric transformations, impacting pattern quality.

Method: MorphOPC employs multi-scale hierarchical modeling and neural morphological modules to learn transformations for improving mask generation quality.

Result: MorphOPC delivers superior performance in edge-based OPC and ILT benchmarks for metal and via layers, surpassing state-of-the-art approaches in printing fidelity and manufacturing cost.

Conclusion: MorphOPC demonstrates significant improvement in scalable mask optimization, making it a strong candidate for addressing challenges in nanometer-scale circuit pattern transfer.

Abstract: As feature sizes shrink to the nanometer scale, accurately transferring circuit patterns from photomasks to silicon wafers becomes increasingly challenging. Optical proximity correction (OPC) is widely used to ensure pattern fidelity and manufacturability. Recent generative mask optimization models based on encoder-decoder architecture can synthesize near-optimal masks, serving as fast machine learning (ML) surrogates for traditional OPC. However, these models often fail to capture the geometric transformations from target layouts to mask patterns, leading to suboptimal quality. In this work, we formulate mask generation as a sequence of morphological operations on local layout features and propose \textit{MorphOPC}, a multi-scale hierarchical model with neural morphological modules to learn these transformations. Experiments on edge-based OPC and ILT benchmarks across metal and via layers show that \textit{MorphOPC} consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving higher printing fidelity and lower manufacturing cost, demonstrating strong potential for scalable mask optimization.

</details>


### [139] [CROP: Expert-Aligned Image Cropping via Compositional Reasoning and Optimizing Preference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12545)
*Zhitong Dong,Chao Li,Jie Yu,Hao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a method, CROP, to improve aesthetic image cropping by transforming it into a multimodal reasoning task, utilizing Visual Language Models (VLMs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous methods for image cropping often rely on simplistic saliency or retrieval, neglecting the nuanced understanding of aesthetics and composition, and struggle to match human expert performance.

Method: The authors propose the CROP pipeline, which uses multimodal reasoning to analyze aesthetic elements step-by-step and aligns decisions with human preferences.

Result: Experimental results across multiple datasets show superior performance and validated effectiveness of the proposed method.

Conclusion: The proposed paradigm successfully addresses aesthetic image cropping challenges by leveraging compositional reasoning and aligning predictions with human-like decisions.

Abstract: Aesthetic image cropping aims to enhance the aesthetic quality of an image by improving its composition through spatial cropping. Previous methods often rely on saliency prediction or retrieval augmentation, ignoring the task's core requirement: a deep understanding of composition and aesthetics. Consequently, saliency-based methods struggle to make compositional trade-offs in complex scenes, while retrieval-based methods blindly refer to similar cases, lacking adaptive reasoning for unique scenes. Both approaches fail to align their automated cropping results with those of human experts. To address the above issues, we propose a novel paradigm that reformulates aesthetic cropping as a multimodal reasoning task, aiming to activate the VLM's analytical and comprehension capabilities in aesthetics. We design a Compositional Reasoning and Optimizing Preference method (CROP) that directs the VLM to think like a professional photographer. It deconstructs a complex and subjective aesthetic problem into an "analysis-proposal-decision" process, reasoning step by step through the analysis of scene elements and compositional principles. Meanwhile, our expert preference alignment module makes the model's decision consistent with human expert aesthetics. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets validate our method's superiority and component effectiveness.

</details>


### [140] [What Happens Before Decoding? Prefill Determines GUI Grounding in VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12549)
*Jiaping Lin,Fei Shen,Junzhe Li,Ping Nie,Fei Yu,Ming Li,Haizhou Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Re-Prefill, a method to improve GUI grounding in Vision-Language Models without requiring multiple inference runs or additional training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address inefficiencies and bypass the over-reliance on multiple inference runs in training-free GUI grounding methods, especially during the candidate selection stage of Vision-Language Models.

Method: The authors introduce Re-Prefill, an attention-guided second prefill stage that adds high-attention visual tokens and instruction hidden states into the input, allowing the model to reevaluate and refine its decisions before generating coordinates.

Result: Re-Prefill yields consistent performance improvements across four Vision-Language Models and five popular benchmarks, with gains of up to 4.3% on the ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark.

Conclusion: Re-Prefill effectively mitigates the bottleneck in training-free GUI grounding by refining candidate selection during the prefill stage, thus achieving better results without necessitating additional model training.

Abstract: Existing training-free approaches for GUI grounding often rely on multiple inference runs, such as iterative cropping or candidate aggregation, to identify target elements. Despite this additional computation, each forward pass still independently interprets the instruction and parses the visual layout, without enabling progressive interaction among visual tokens. In this paper, we study what happens during GUI grounding in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) and identify a previously overlooked bottleneck. We show that grounding follows a two-stage paradigm: the prefill stage determines candidate UI elements, while the decoding stage subsequently refines the final coordinates. This asymmetry establishes prefill as the critical step, as errors in candidate selection cannot be effectively corrected during decoding. Based on this observation, we propose Re-Prefill, a training-free method that revisits inference by introducing an attention-guided second prefill stage to refine target selection. Specifically, visual tokens that consistently receive high attention from the query position, i.e., the final token, across layers are extracted as a preliminary target hypothesis and appended to the input, together with the instruction hidden states, enabling the model to deeply re-think its decision before coordinate generation. Experiments across four VLMs and five benchmarks, including ScreenSpot-Pro, ScreenSpot-V2, OSWorld-G, UI-Vision, and MMBench-GUI, demonstrate consistent improvements without additional training, with gains of up to 4.3% on ScreenSpot-Pro. Code will be available at https://github.com/linjiaping1/Re-Prefill.

</details>


### [141] [SSDA: Bridging Spectral and Structural Gaps via Dual Adaptation for Vision-Based Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12550)
*Mingrui Zhang,Hanchen Yang,Wengen Li,Xudong Jiang,Yichao Zhang,Jihong Guan,Shuigeng Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Large Vision Models (LVMs) are effective for time series forecasting when they treat temporal data as images. However, challenges remain due to spectral and structural gaps between rendered time series images and natural images. To address this, the authors propose SSDA, a dual-branch network. Extensive experiments validate its effectiveness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to improve the performance of large vision models (LVMs) in time series forecasting by addressing key gaps, specifically the spectral and structural differences between rendered time series images and natural images.

Method: The paper introduces SSDA, a dual-branch network with Spectral Magnitude Aligner (SMA) to enhance the spectral alignment and Structural-Guided Low-Rank Adaptation (SG-LoRA) to address the structural misalignment caused by reshaping temporal data into 2D grids. These branches are integrated adaptively to produce a final forecast.

Result: SSDA outperforms strong LVM- and LLM-based baselines across seven real-world benchmarks in both full-shot and few-shot forecasting setups, demonstrating its efficacy in addressing the identified gaps.

Conclusion: SSDA effectively bridges spectral and structural gaps, thereby leveraging the full potential of large vision models for time series forecasting. The method is validated through significant performance improvements over baseline models.

Abstract: Large vision models (LVMs) have recently proven to be surprisingly effective time series forecasters, simply by rendering temporal data as images. This success, how ever, rests on a largely unexamined premise: the rendered time series images are sufficiently close to natural images for knowledge in pre-trained models to transfer effectively. We argue that two gaps still remain, i.e., spectral and structural gaps, fundamentally limiting the potential of LVMs for time series forecasting. Spectrally, we systematically reveal that rendered time series images exhibit a markedly shallower power spectrum than the natural images LVMs are pre-trained to recognize. Structurally, reshaping 1D temporal sequences into 2D grids fabricates spurious spatial adjacencies while severing genuine temporal continuities, misleading the spatial inductive biases of pre-trained LVMs. To bridge these gaps, we propose SSDA, a dual-branch network that spectrally and structurally adapts to unlock the full potential of LVMs for time series forecasting. At the data level, a Spectral Magnitude Aligner (SMA) applies 2D FFT to selectively enhance the magnitude spectrum toward natural-image statistics while preserving phase. At the model level, a Structural-Guided Low-Rank Adaptation (SG-LoRA) injects position-aware temporal encodings into patch embeddings and adapts at tention via low-rank updates. The two branches are further adaptively fused to produce the final forecast. Extensive experiments on seven real-world benchmarks demonstrate that SSDA consistently outperforms strong LVM- and LLM-based baselines under both full-shot and few-shot settings. Code is publicly available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SSDA-8C5B.

</details>


### [142] [M2Retinexformer: Multi-Modal Retinexformer for Low-Light Image Enhancement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12556)
*Youssef Aboelwafa,Hicham G. Elmongui,Marwan Torki*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces M2Retinexformer, a multi-modal approach to low-light image enhancement by integrating depth, luminance, and semantics via cross-attention and adaptive gating, improving upon prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing Retinex-based methods reliant solely on RGB data, aiming to tackle low-light image challenges such as amplified noise, artifacts, and color distortion.

Method: The proposed method, M2Retinexformer, integrates multi-modal inputs (depth, luminance, and semantic features) and incorporates cross-attention and adaptive gating in a progressive refinement pipeline for enhanced low-light image restoration.

Result: The framework demonstrated improvements on various benchmarks (LOL, SID, SMID, and SDSD), achieving better performance compared to Retinexformer and state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: M2Retinexformer effectively combines multi-modal cues and adaptive mechanisms for improved low-light image enhancement, offering a robust upgrade to prior techniques.

Abstract: Low-light image enhancement is challenging due to complex degradations, including amplified noise, artifacts, and color distortion. While Retinex-based deep learning methods have achieved promising results, they primarily rely on single-modality RGB information. We propose M2Retinexformer (Multi-Modal Retinexformer), a novel framework that extends Retinexformer by incorporating depth cues, luminance priors, and semantic features within a progressive refinement pipeline. Depth provides geometric context that is invariant to lighting variations, while luminance and semantic features offer explicit guidance on brightness distribution and scene understanding. Modalities are extracted at multiple scales and fused through cross-attention, with adaptive gating dynamically balancing illumination-guided self-attention and cross-attention based on the reliability of auxiliary cues. Evaluations on the LOL, SID, SMID, and SDSD benchmarks demonstrate overall improvements over Retinexformer and recent state-of-the-art methods. Code and pretrained weights are available at https://github.com/YoussefAboelwafa/M2Retinexformer

</details>


### [143] [Pyramid Self-contrastive Learning Framework for Test-time Ultrasound Image Denoising](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12567)
*Jiajing Zhang,Bingze Dai,Xi Zhang,Yue Xu,Wei-Ning Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Aperture-to-Aperture (A2A) framework for denoising ultrasound images via a one-shot test-time training approach, improving image quality without requiring pretraining or labeled datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the challenges of existing ultrasound denoising methods, which suffer from model limitations, domain shifts, and reliance on assumptions or large data sets, complicating their application under real-world noise conditions.

Method: The study proposes A2A, a test-time training framework that performs self-contrastive learning to separate anatomical details from noise randomness using shuffled sub-apertures in pyramid latent spaces. It trains directly on a single noisy input to eliminate domain shift.

Result: A2A achieved improvements of 69.3% in SNR and 34.4% in CNR in simulations and gains of 84.8% SNR and 25.7% CNR in in vivo studies, demonstrating its effectiveness across various imaging conditions and targets.

Conclusion: A2A provides a robust and adaptive solution for ultrasound image denoising, enabling clear visualization and functional assessments with minimal setup, which could enhance clinical usability.

Abstract: The inherent electronic and speckle noise complicates clinical interpretation of ultrasound images. Conventional denoising methods rely on explicit noise assumptions whose validity diminishes under composite noise conditions. Learning-based methods require massive labeled data and model parameters. These pre-defined and pre-trained manners entail an inevitable domain shift in complex in vivo environments, so they are limited to a specific noise type and often blur structural details. In this study, we propose a pure test-time training framework for one-shot ultrasound image denoising and apply it to synthetic aperture ultrasound (SAU), which synthesizes transmit focus from sub-aperture transmissions. Our Aperture-to-Aperture (A2A) framework disentangles anatomical similarity and noise randomness from shuffled sub-apertures through self-contrastive learning in pyramid latent spaces. The clean image is then decoded from the anatomy space, while discarding the noise space. A2A is trained at test time on one noisy sample of SAU signals, so it fundamentally eliminates the domain shift and pretraining costs. Simulation experiments, including electronic noise levels of 0 to 30 dB and different inclusion geometries, demonstrated an improvement of 69.3% SNR and 34.4% CNR by A2A. The in vivo results showed 84.8% SNR and 25.7% CNR gains using only two aperture data of the heart in six echocardiographic views, liver, and kidney.
