TL;DR: The paper investigates the use of large language models (LLMs) to analyze user reviews for usability requirements and their performance compared to human raters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: User-centered approaches improve product alignment with end-user needs, and LLMs offer new opportunities in understanding and analyzing requirements without the need for extensive manual labeling or ML training.

Method: The authors used an LLM to analyze a dataset of 300 user reviews, iteratively designed prompts based on Nielsen Usability Heuristics, and evaluated LLM performance in identifying usability as a non-functional requirement against human raters.

Result: LLMs demonstrated the ability to identify usability-relevant aspects with reasonable F-score performance, but their reliability depends heavily on the quality of the prompt used.

Conclusion: LLMs are promising for efficient and cost-effective requirements elicitation, though their effectiveness is tied closely to proper prompt engineering.

Abstract: It is known that user-centered approaches to requirements engineering in general lead to a better suited product for the end-users. LLM4RE provides promising approaches to support the requirements elicitation process (e.g. classification of requirements). Previous approaches focus on Machine-Learning (ML) or Deep-Learning (DL) aspects, which require intensive training with a large amount of manually labeled data. LLMs, on the other hand, are pre-trained on large amounts of user-generated text data, enabling a user-centric workflow to analyze requirements. In this paper, we explore the possibility of exploiting the improved natural language understanding of LLMs, rather than strict ML classification, together with the mass extraction of user reviews to analyze if the performance of LLMs in understanding user reviews is comparable to the performance of human raters. This enables a quick and cheap workflow for development teams to gather and process their userś requirements. This paper provides three major contributions: (1) We provide a completely coded dataset of 300 user reviews containing usability-relevant aspects from three different types of apps, that were labeled by two human raters and by an LLM. (2) We build an initial prompt, based on two prompt engineering iterations and specifically developed coding guidelines derived from the 10 Nielsen Usability Heuristics, for LLMs to filter usability relevant user reviews. (3) We determine that LLMs are generally able to recognize usability as a non-functional requirement in user reviews, in terms of their F-score, but the performance and reliability is strongly dependent on the prompt.

</details>


### [518] [Agentic Interpretation: Lattice-Structured Evidence for LLM-Based Program Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12694)
*Jacqueline L. Mitchell,Chao Wang*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'agentic interpretation', a framework enabling large language models (LLMs) to perform more disciplined and iterative program analysis by breaking down goals into localized claims and tracking judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can leverage external information such as documentation and security advisories, which traditional static analyzers cannot fully utilize. However, single-query approaches to using LLMs for program analysis are brittle and opaque, necessitating a more systematic framework.

Method: The method involves decomposing analysis tasks into smaller claims, organizing judgments in a finite-height lattice, and managing the process via a worklist algorithm. This integrates the rigor of lattice-based static analysis with LLM-driven reasoning.

Result: The paper presents a formal model of the agentic interpretation framework and showcases its application through an example involving code dependent on third-party components.

Conclusion: Agentic interpretation demonstrates promise in improving the reliability and transparency of LLM-driven program analysis, providing a more structured alternative to direct, one-shot methods.

Abstract: Large language models can consult information that fixed static analyzers cannot, such as documentation, current security advisories, version-specific metadata, and informal API contracts. This makes LLMs a compelling option for program analyses that depend on information beyond the source program, or that are otherwise not amenable to conventional static analyzers. However, directly asking an LLM for a one-shot whole-program analysis is brittle because it compresses many evidence-dependent judgments into a single opaque answer, rather than exposing which conclusions are supported or disputed and using intermediate findings to guide later, more focused searches. In this paper, we propose agentic interpretation, a framework that brings the discipline of lattice-based static analysis to LLM-driven program reasoning. At a high level, agentic interpretation decomposes a high-level analysis goal into localized claims, and tracks the LLM's judgment about each claim in a finite-height lattice. A worklist algorithm governs how claims and their judgments evolve during the analysis. We introduce a formal model of agentic interpretation, explore the design space it opens, and illustrate the approach with a worked example analyzing code that depends on opaque third-party components.

</details>


### [519] [Finding a Crab in the C: Assured Translation via Comparative Symbolic Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12731)
*Caleb Helbling,Graham Leach-Krouse,Michael Crystal*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper introduces 'cozy,' a comparative binary analysis tool to ensure semantic equivalence between binaries translated from unsafe languages to memory-safe languages by flagging and reviewing behavior differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges in maintaining equivalence between legacy code in unsafe languages and their automated translations into memory-safe languages, ensuring reliability and reducing risks.

Method: The method involves cozy performing simultaneous analysis between binaries from unsafe source code and their memory-safe translations, flagging behavioral differences for user review, and guaranteeing equivalence outside of flagged changes.

Result: cozy successfully identifies, flags, and formally verifies behavioral differences between unsafe and memory-safe translated binaries, assisting developers in reducing translation risks.

Conclusion: The tool ensures safe automated translation, bug correction, and reliable code reviews by bridging the semantic gap between unsafe and memory-safe binaries, making it valuable for modern software development.

Abstract: Modern high-assurance software systems development favors memory safe languages such as SPARK (ADA) or Rust. However, developers often encounter non-memory safe code (e.g., C) in legacy systems and libraries which would be prohibitively expensive or risky to re-write. In response, developers have begun turning to machine learning/AI systems and other automated code translators. Automated translation comes with its own risks, however. The original and ported code are not precisely the same, semantically - otherwise there would be no point in performing the translation. To reduce these risks, we have developed cozy, a comparative binary analysis tool that simultaneously analyzes a binary compiled from "unsafe" source code and a binary compiled from a translation of the source code to a memory safe language. cozy walks the developer through differences in the behavior of the two binaries, presenting each difference and asking the user to assess whether the difference is intentional (good) or erroneous. Outside of the flagged differences, the binaries are formally verified to be equivalent. Consequently, the review process guarantees equivalence modulo changes approved by the developer. cozy has applications to automated translation, bug correction, code reviews, operation authorization, and automatic translation.

</details>


### [520] [Project Life Cycles in Open-Source Software](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12738)
*Sanjiv Das,Andrii Ieroshenko,Piyush Jain,David Qiu,Michael Chin,Brian Granger*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper models developer engagement in open-source projects using methods similar to product life cycles and analyzes growth using differential equations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how developer engagement and growth dynamics work in open-source projects and provide insights for predicting lifetime value.

Method: The authors applied endogenous growth theory and systems of differential equations to analyze interactions between development growth and developer activity over time.

Result: The proposed model aligns well with empirical data from various open-source projects and successfully estimates lifetime developer engagement and growth.

Conclusion: This approach facilitates estimating the cumulative production value of open-source projects, offering a valuable tool for project evaluation.

Abstract: Using methods previously applied to product life cycles, this paper models developer engagement through the project life cycle for open-source projects, and detects similar dynamics in a cross section of projects. Endogenous growth theory is used to model growth dynamics in open-source software engineering, while incorporating the interactions between growth levels and developer activity over time using systems of differential equations. The solution to this model calibrates well to many open-source projects. The model generates an estimate of the lifetime developer engagement and growth, which supports estimating a lifetime production value of open-source projects.

</details>


### [521] [AgentLens: Revealing The Lucky Pass Problem in SWE-Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12925)
*Priyam Sahoo,Gaurav Mittal,Xiaomin Li,Shengjie Ma,Benjamin Steenhoek,Pingping Lin,Yu Hu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper critiques the traditional binary evaluation of software engineering agents and proposes a new framework called AgentLens for a more comprehensive process-level assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the inadequacies of current evaluation methods of software engineering agents that equate outcome-only metrics with process quality, failing to differentiate systematic approaches from chaotic or lucky successes.

Method: The authors analyzed 2,614 trajectories using a dataset and introduced AgentLens, which builds process references with contextual intention labeling across four action categories. They assessed trajectories by quality scores and decomposed mechanisms of poor-quality passes.

Result: Key insights show that up to 10.7% of passing trajectories are "lucky passes" characterized by disordered processes. AgentLens effectively distinguishes trajectory quality, and models vary significantly in quality ranking when assessed by this framework.

Conclusion: This work presents AgentLens as a novel and effective framework for distinguishing between lucky and methodical success in SWE-agent trajectories, redefining the evaluation criteria and offering data and tools for further exploration.

Abstract: Evaluation of software engineering (SWE) agents is dominated by a binary signal: whether the final patch passes the tests. This outcome-only view treats a principled solution and a chaotic trial-and-error process as equivalent. We show that this equivalence is empirically false. We evaluate 2,614 OpenHands trajectories from eight model backends on 60 SWE-bench Verified tasks. Of these, 47 have enough passing trajectories to construct task-level process references, yielding a 1,815-trajectory evaluation subset. Among passing trajectories in this subset, 10.7% exhibit behavior we call a Lucky Pass: regression cycles, blind retries, missing verification, or temporally disordered exploration, implementation, and verification.
  We introduce AgentLens, a framework for process-level assessment of SWE-agent trajectories, and release AgentLens-Bench, a dataset of 1,815 trajectories annotated with quality scores, waste signals, divergence points, and 47 task-level Prefix Tree Acceptor (PTA) references. AgentLens builds PTA references by merging multiple passing solutions for the same task, and uses a context-sensitive intent labeler to assign actions to Exploration, Implementation, Verification, or Orchestration based on trajectory history rather than tool identity alone.
  On AgentLens-Bench, the quality score separates passing trajectories into Lucky, Solid, and Ideal tiers and further decomposes Lucky Passes into five recurring mechanisms. Across the eight model backends, Lucky rates range from 0.5% to 23.2%, and some models move by as many as five rank positions when ranked by quality score instead of pass rate. We release the anonymized project repository, including the AgentLens-Bench dataset and AgentLens SDK, at https://github.com/microsoft/code-agent-state-trajectories/.

</details>


### [522] [Protocol-Driven Development: Governing Generated Software Through Invariants and Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12981)
*Jun He,Deying Yu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Protocol-Driven Development (PDD) introduces machine-enforceable protocols as the primary control mechanism for automating software governance rather than relying solely on natural-language specs or example tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional methods like natural-language specifications and example-based testing provide inadequate governance for automated software due to semantic ambiguities and limited behavioral coverage.

Method: It defines protocols (P = S, B, O) which enforce structural, behavioral, and operational invariants, ensuring generated implementations satisfy these constraints while producing verifiable evidence of compliance.

Result: PDD substitutes trust in generators with confidence derived from compliance with governing protocols and verifiable evidence chains, offering more robust admissibility.

Conclusion: By prioritizing protocol over transient implementation code, PDD establishes a governance-oriented framework for safer and more reliable automated software engineering.

Abstract: Automated program synthesis has reduced the cost of producing candidate implementations, but it introduces a harder governance problem: determining which generated artifacts are admissible in a software system. Natural-language specifications remain semantically ambiguous, and example-based tests sample only part of the behavioral space. Used alone, neither provides a sufficient control boundary for automated software construction. We introduce Protocol-Driven Development (PDD), a development model in which the primary software artifact is a machine-enforceable protocol rather than implementation code. We define a protocol as the triplet P = (S, B, O), where S specifies structural invariants, B specifies behavioral invariants, and O specifies operational invariants. Their conjunction defines the admissible implementation space of a software component. Under PDD, implementations are treated as replaceable realizations discovered through constrained search. An implementation is admitted if and only if it satisfies the governing protocol and produces a verifiable Evidence Chain of compliance. Admission is therefore grounded not in trust in the generator, but in protocol satisfaction and recorded evidence. By combining ideas from formal methods, property-based testing, policy-as-code, and software provenance, PDD defines a governance layer for automated software engineering. Its organizing principle is simple: code is transient; protocol is sovereign.

</details>


### [523] [Code-Centric Detection of Vulnerability-Fixing Commits: A Unified Benchmark and Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13138)
*Nils Loose,Joseph Bienhüls,Kristoffer Hempel,Felix Mächtle,Thomas Eisenbarth*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the capability of code language models to detect vulnerability-fixing commits (VFCs) across a unified framework, finding significant limitations in the models' ability to understand code vulnerabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vulnerability-fixing commits are critical for timely security patching, yet many fixes lack advisories, and databases delay by a median of 25 days. Detecting VFCs efficiently is important to address these gaps.

Method: The paper consolidated over 20 datasets of more than 180,000 commits and performed over 180 experiments with fine-tuned models ranging from 125M to 14B parameters. It also conducted attribution analyses and diverse evaluations across grouped, temporal, and compositional shifts.

Result: The study finds that models fail to develop transferable security-relevant understanding from code differences alone. Commit messages overly influence results, and when missing, enriched code does not boost attention toward diffs. All fine-tuned code-only models perform poorly, missing over 93% of vulnerabilities at a 0.5% false positive rate.

Conclusion: Code-centric models are insufficient for robust VFC detection. Larger datasets or generative approaches show minor improvements but do not solve inherent issues. The authors provide a unified framework to aid future research in this field.

Abstract: Automated detection of vulnerability-fixing commits (VFCs) is critical for timely security patch deployment, as advisory databases lag patch releases by a median of 25 days and many fixes never receive advisories. We present a comprehensive evaluation of code language model based VFC detection through a unified framework consolidating over 20 fragmented datasets spanning more than 180000 commits. Across over 180 experiments with fine-tuned models from 125 M to 14 B parameters, we find no evidence that models acquire transferable security-relevant code understanding from code changes alone. When commit messages are available, they dominate model attention, and when removed, an attribution analysis shows that enriching diffs with additional intra-procedural semantic context does not shift model attention toward the code changes. Group-stratified evaluation exposes approximately 17% performance drops compared to random splits, while temporal splits on aggregated datasets prove unreliable due to compositional shift in the underlying project distributions. At a false positive rate of 0.5% all fine-tuned code-only models miss over 93% of vulnerabilities. Larger and more diverse training data or generative approaches show preliminary improvements but do not resolve the underlying limitations. To support future research on code-centric VFC detection, we release our unified framework and evaluation suite.

</details>


### [524] [SWE-Cycle: Benchmarking Code Agents across the Complete Issue Resolution Cycle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13139)
*Hao Guan,Lingyue Fu,Shao Zhang,Yaoming Zhu,Kangning Zhang,Lin Qiu,Xunliang Cai,Xuezhi Cao,Weiwen Liu,Weinan Zhang,Yong Yu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: SWE-Cycle is a benchmark designed to test autonomous code agents' abilities in end-to-end software development, highlighting the challenges of integration across development phases. SWE-Judge evaluates their output using static and dynamic analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the shortcomings of current benchmarks that fail to accurately evaluate the real-world end-to-end autonomy of code agents and hide complexities by using pre-configured environments.

Method: Introduce SWE-Cycle, a benchmark with tasks that isolate and integrate development phases, and SWE-Judge, an evaluation agent capable of verifying functional correctness through static and dynamic testing.

Result: Code agents powered by six state-of-the-art LLMs showed a significant drop in performance when moving from isolated tasks to fully autonomous FullCycle execution, revealing challenges in handling dependencies and maintaining quality.

Conclusion: The framework of SWE-Cycle and SWE-Judge is a reliable way to measure the autonomous capabilities of code agents, exposing critical issues in their ability to execute complex, multi-phase development tasks.

Abstract: As autonomous code agents move toward end-to-end software development, evaluating their practical autonomy becomes critical. Current benchmarks hide friction by testing agents in pre-configured environments, and their static evaluation pipelines frequently fail when parsing fully autonomous trajectories. We address these limitations with SWE-Cycle, a benchmark of 489 rigorously filtered instances. SWE-Cycle evaluates agents across three isolated tasks, including environment reconstruction, code implementation, and verification test generation, as well as an end-to-end FullCycle task that integrates all three. The FullCycle task requires agents to work autonomously in a bare repository without human scaffolding. To reliably assess these complex execution paths, we developed SWE-Judge. By combining static code review with dynamic testing, this execution-capable evaluation agent accurately verifies functional correctness and eliminates the systematic measurement errors of traditional static parsers. We evaluate code agents powered by six state-of-the-art LLMs across these four tasks. The results reveal a sharp drop in solve rates when transitioning from isolated tasks to FullCycle execution, exposing critical bottlenecks in handling cross-phase dependencies and maintaining code quality. Together, SWE-Cycle and SWE-Judge provide a comprehensive framework for accurately measuring the end-to-end capabilities of autonomous software agents.

</details>


### [525] [UIBenchKit: A unified toolkit for design-to-code model evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13141)
*Chinh T. Le,Trevor Ong Yee Siang,Jingyu Xiao,Yuxuan Wan,Yintong Huo*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces UIBenchKit, a toolkit for standardized evaluation in design-to-code tasks, aiming to promote fair comparison and accelerate research progress.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of a standardized evaluation platform for design-to-code generation, which hinders fair comparisons, practical adoption, and systematic research.

Method: The authors developed UIBenchKit, an open-source toolkit offering a unified evaluation environment with features like a plug-and-play architecture and an analytical interface.

Result: Using UIBenchKit, they benchmarked existing tools and identified insights for improving design-to-code methods.

Conclusion: UIBenchKit enables consistent evaluation and aims to enhance future benchmarking and innovation in automated web engineering tasks.

Abstract: Recent years have seen substantial progress in automated design-to-code generation, with many methods proposed for generating HTML and CSS from webpage screenshots. However, the absence of a standardized evaluation platform makes it difficult to compare these methods fairly, limiting both practical adoption and systematic research progress. To bridge this gap, we introduce UIBenchKit, an open-source, integrated toolkit designed to unify the evaluation of design-to-code tasks. UIBenchKit abstracts the complexities of environment setup, model inference, and code rendering, offering researchers a plug-and-play architecture to compare various methods under consistent settings. In addition, it offers an analytical interface for comparison across multiple metrics. Using UIBenchKit, we conduct a benchmarking study of existing tools and derive several findings that highlight directions for future improvement. By providing a streamlined environment for both experimentation and evaluation, UIBenchKit aims to accelerate future benchmarking and innovations in web engineering. The evaluation platform and toolkit are available at the project page https://www.uibenchkit.com/.

</details>


### [526] [ReproScore: Separating Readiness from Outcome in Research Software Reproducibility Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13275)
*Sheeba Samuel,Daniel Mietchen,Jungsan Kim,Waqas Ahmed,Martin Gaedke*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces ReproScore, a framework for assessing the executability of research software artefacts in digital libraries, addressing the gap between repository completeness and execution success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of assessing whether millions of research software artefacts in digital libraries remain executable, while resolving the readiness-outcome conflation issue.

Method: ReproScore separates reproducibility readiness (RRS) from reproducibility outcome (ROS) and integrates them into the Composite Score (RCS). It uses 26 sub-metrics, execution-based probes, and versioned YAML profiles for flexibility and scalability.

Result: ReproScore was tested on 423 GitHub repositories, revealing that the static signals of the environment category can differentiate failure modes, though RRS shows minimal correlation with binary success outcomes, highlighting a readiness-outcome gap.

Conclusion: The architectural separation in ReproScore is validated as essential and effective for large-scale, reproducibility-aware curation in digital library workflows.

Abstract: Digital libraries curate millions of research software artefacts yet lack scalable infrastructure for assessing whether those artefacts remain executable. Existing automated assessment tools treat static repository completeness -- what a repository contains -- as a proxy for execution success -- whether it runs. We term this the readiness-outcome conflation and present ReproScore, a two-tier framework that explicitly separates reproducibility readiness (RRS) from reproducibility outcome (ROS), combining them into a coverage-adaptive Composite Score (RCS). RRS comprises 26 sub-metrics across five categories; ROS provides execution-based probes when sandbox infrastructure is available; a community rubric externalises weighting priorities as versioned YAML profiles. Evaluated on 423 GitHub repositories from a large-scale ground-truth corpus spanning five failure modes, two complementary findings emerge: the environment category strongly discriminates failure mode, confirming static signals capture meaningful structural differences; yet RRS exhibits near-zero binary success correlation, empirically quantifying the readiness-outcome gap at repository scale. Together, these findings validate the architectural separation as both necessary and non-trivial, positioning ReproScore as scalable infrastructure for reproducibility-aware curation in digital library workflows.

</details>


### [527] [Robust Mutation Analysis of Quantum Programs Under Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13279)
*Sophie Fortz,Eñaut Mendiluze Usandizaga,Shaukat Ali,Paolo Arcaini,Mohammad Reza Mousavi*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper investigates the impact of quantum hardware noise on mutation analysis for quantum programs through empirical studies using noisy and noiseless simulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the overlooked role of quantum hardware noise in mutation analysis for quantum programs, as prior studies assumed noiseless execution.

Method: The study involved 41 quantum programs tested on both noiseless and noisy simulators, simulating real IBM quantum devices and using various metrics and thresholds to assess mutant detection.

Result: Noise alters behavioral distances, complicating the detection of equivalent mutants. Density-matrix metrics achieved the best discrimination but aren't hardware-compatible; practical alternatives were less accurate but benefited from noise-aware adjustments.

Conclusion: Adaptation of mutation analysis to the noise characteristics of target quantum devices is crucial to improve quantum program evaluation.

Abstract: Mutation analysis has long been used in classical software testing and has recently been adopted for assessing the robustness of quantum software testing techniques. However, existing studies assume ideal, noiseless execution, overlooking the impact of quantum hardware noise. In this paper, we present an empirical study of noise-aware mutation analysis for quantum programs. We analyze how noise affects mutant detection using 41 quantum programs, executed on noiseless and noisy simulators emulating three IBM devices with different noise profiles. We compare several distance metrics and thresholding strategies to evaluate mutant detection under realistic noise. Our results show that noise significantly alters the behavioral distance between programs and mutants, making equivalent mutants harder to distinguish from real faults. Density-matrix metrics achieve the best discrimination, with misclassification rates up to 16.77%, but are not accessible on real hardware. Among practical alternatives, output-distribution metrics reach up to 73.03% accuracy and 74.89% F1-score. Noise-specific thresholds further improve detection compared to noiseless thresholds. We also find that noise effects correlate more with algorithm and circuit characteristics than with mutation types. Overall, our results highlight the need to adapt mutation analysis, and more generally quantum program comparison, to the noise profiles of target quantum devices.

</details>


### [528] [The Readability Spectrum: Patterns, Issues, and Prompt Effects in LLM-Generated Code](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13280)
*Hengzhi Ye,Fengyuan Ran,Weiwei Xu,Minghui Zhou*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper investigates the readability of code generated by large language models (LLMs), finding it comparable to human-written code but with distinct readability issues and limited effects of prompt design on readability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the understudied aspect of readability in LLM-generated code, which is essential as such code often requires human review before adoption.

Method: The authors built a comprehensive readability model combining textual, structural, program, and visual features, and used it to evaluate 5,869 scenarios from code bases like World of Code and LeetCode.

Result: LLM-generated code has comparable readability to human-written code but exhibits unique patterns of readability issues. Prompts with function signatures, constraints, and style descriptions have some influence, but prompt design overall has limited impact on readability.

Conclusion: Although LLM-generated code is readable and ready for integration into software workflows, there are distinct readability challenges that warrant further research to ensure long-term maintainability.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are transforming software development, the functional quality of generated code has become a central focus, leaving readability, one of critical non-functional attributes, understudied. Given that LLM-generated code still needs human review before adoption, it is important to understand its readability especially compared with human-written code and the role of prompt design in shaping it. We therefore set out to conduct a systematic investigation into the code readability of LLM-generated code. To systematically quantify code readability, We establish a comprehensive readability model that synthesizes textual, structural, program, and visual features of code. Based on the model, we evaluate the readability of code generated by the mainstream LLMs under 5,869 scenarios extracted from large code base including World of Code (WoC) and LeetCode. We find that current LLMs produce code with overall readability comparable to human-written code, but displaying distinct readability issue patterns. We further examine how different prompt dimensions affect the readability of LLM-generated code, and find that function signatures, constraints and style descriptions emerge as the most influential factors, while the overall impact of prompt design remains limited. Our findings indicate that, on one hand, LLM-generated code is at least comparable to human-written code in readability, validating its potential for systematic integration into software workflows from a non-functional perspective; on the other hand, distinct readability issue patterns and limited effectiveness of prompt engineering reveal a latent technical debt, highlighting the need for future research to improve the readability of LLM-generated code and thus ensure long-term maintainability.

</details>


### [529] [AI Harness Engineering: A Runtime Substrate for Foundation-Model Software Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13357)
*Hailin Zhong,Shengxin Zhu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper argues that autonomous software engineering relies not just on model capabilities but on the model-harness-environment system, introducing an AI Harness Engineering framework to bridge this gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to address the unreliability of autonomous software-engineering agents in real-world development environments, challenging the belief that improving foundation models alone will close this gap.

Method: The authors propose the AI Harness Engineering framework, formalizing a set of eleven component responsibilities and introducing a four-level runtime support system (H0-H3). They also develop a trace-based evaluation protocol for assessing software agent performance.

Result: In a controlled validation task, the proposed framework generates episode packages with evidence structures that become richer and more systematic at higher harness levels, facilitating detailed evaluation of software agent performance.

Conclusion: Reframing software engineering autonomy, the study emphasizes the importance of a well-designed runtime system, proposing a pathway for developing robust foundation-model agents capable of producing verifiable and maintainable changes.

Abstract: Foundation models have transformed automated code generation, yet autonomous software-engineering agents remain unreliable in realistic development settings. The dominant explanation locates this gap in model capability. We propose a different locus: software-engineering capability emerges from a model-harness-environment system, in which a runtime substrate -- the harness -- mediates how a foundation-model agent observes a project, acts on it, receives feedback, and establishes that a change is complete. We formalize this substrate as an AI Harness Engineering and identify eleven component responsibilities: task specification, context selection, tool access, project memory, task state, observability, failure attribution, verification, permissions, entropy auditing, and intervention recording. We operationalize the harness through a four-level ladder (H0-H3) that progressively exposes runtime support to the agent, and we propose a trace-based evaluation protocol that converts each agent run into an auditable episode package. Applied to a controlled validation task, the framework yields episode packages whose evidence structure varies systematically with harness level: lower levels produce only a final patch, higher levels produce reproduction logs, failure attributions, deterministic requirement checks, and structured verification reports. The framework reframes the central question of autonomous software engineering from whether a foundation model can produce a patch to whether the model-harness-environment system can produce a verifiably correct, attributed, and maintainable change. We outline a research program for the runtime systems that foundation-model software agents will require.

</details>


### [530] [SieveFL: Hierarchical Runtime-Aware Pruning for Scalable LLM-Based Fault Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13491)
*Mahdi Farzandway,Fatemeh Ghassemi*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: SieveFL is a new fault localization system that combines aggressive filtering with large language models (LLMs) to pinpoint bugs effectively while reducing token cost and computation demands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation behind this work is to address the inefficiency and limited precision of current fault localization methods. Statistical approaches struggle with precision, and LLMs face token cost and signal dilution issues when handling full project-scale data.

Method: SieveFL employs a five-stage hierarchical filtering workflow. It begins by generating a natural-language failure description, uses dense vector retrieval to narrow candidate files, eliminates non-executed methods via runtime traces, and finally utilizes an LLM for method screening and ranking.

Result: Using Defects4J as the testbed, SieveFL achieves a Top-1 accuracy of 41.8% across 395 bugs, improving upon the prior agent-based baseline by 2.1 percentage points. Its runtime pruning also removes 79% of candidate methods, halving input token usage while maintaining or improving ranking performance.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that effective fault localization can be achieved with the right filtering strategy without relying on proprietary frontier models or expensive hardware.

Abstract: Automated fault localization requires connecting an observed test failure to the responsible method across thousands of candidates--a task that purely statistical approaches handle with limited precision and that LLMs cannot yet handle at full project scale due to prohibitive token cost and signal dilution. We present SieveFL, a five-stage hierarchical framework that resolves this tension through aggressive pre-LLM filtering. SieveFL converts a failing test into a natural-language failure description, uses dense vector retrieval to narrow the search to a small set of suspicious files, and then eliminates any method not executed during the failing test via JaCoCo runtime traces. Only the surviving candidates are passed to the LLM, which screens each method individually and re-ranks the confirmed suspects in a single comparative pass. We evaluate SieveFL on 395 bugs from Defects4J v1.2.0 using a mid-sized, openly available MoE model deployed on a commodity workstation (32 GB RAM, 8 GB GPU) via Ollama--no frontier APIs or datacenter hardware required. Treating 12 incomplete runs as failures, SieveFL achieves Top-1 accuracy of 41.8% (165/395 bugs) and an MRR of 0.469, outperforming the strongest prior agent-based baseline (AgentFL) by 2.1 pp in Top-1. Runtime pruning removes 79% of candidate methods and reduces input token consumption by 49%, while simultaneously improving ranking quality: Top-1 is preserved exactly and Top-3 through Top-10 improve by up to 2.4 pp. These results demonstrate that, with the right filtering architecture, capable fault localization does not require proprietary frontier models.

</details>


### [531] [Scalable Deductive Verification of Data-Level Parallel Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13616)
*Lars B. van den Haak,Anton Wijs,Marieke Huisman*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents methods to enhance deductive verification for data-intensive programs by introducing quantifier rewrite techniques, handling overlapping arrays, and providing immutability specifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scalability challenges in verifying data-level programs involving arrays and matrices.

Method: Introduces quantifier rewriting, alias identification constructs for arrays, and immutability modeling, implemented in the VerCors verifier.

Result: Achieved significant speed improvements (average 9x faster, up to 150x in specific cases) and enabled previously unobtainable verification results.

Conclusion: The techniques enhance deductive verification scalability, making such processes feasible for more complex data-driven programs.

Abstract: This paper introduces several techniques that improve the scalability of the deductive verification of data-level programs working on arrays and matrices. First of all, we introduce a technique to rewrite expressions with (nested) quantifiers, so suitable triggers can be generated for these expressions. We have proven this rewrite technique correct in a theorem prover. Second, we make reasoning about potentially overlapping arrays easier, by providing specification constructs to indicate and verify that two arrays are not aliases, or that they are immutable, so they can be modelled as mathematical sequences. All our techniques are implemented in the VerCors program verifier. We illustrate how our techniques improve scalability through a large number of experiments. Using our techniques on a set of typical GPU kernels, we achieve a reduction of verification time by, on average, a factor of 9, with outliers being up to 150 times faster. Additionally, applying these techniques to earlier experiments and an earlier case study of a radio telescope pipeline permitted the verification of results which were previously unobtainable and significantly reduced the verification time.

</details>


### [532] [SkillOps: Managing LLM Agent Skill Libraries as Self-Maintaining Software Ecosystems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13716)
*Hongji Pu,Xinyuan Song,Liang Zhao*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces SkillOps, a framework for maintaining skill libraries used by large language model agents, addressing the problem of accumulating technical debt in skill libraries, and demonstrating improvements in task success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the issue of skill technical debt in skill libraries used by large language model agents, which can hinder skill usage and performance over time.

Method: SkillOps utilizes typed Skill Contracts, a Hierarchical Skill Ecosystem Graph, and library diagnostics across multiple dimensions, producing a refined skill library without modifying existing retrieval or planning agents' internal code.

Result: SkillOps achieved a task success rate of 79.5% on ALFWorld, outperforming baselines by 8.8% without extra task-time large language model calls. It also improved retrieval-heavy baselines by up to 2.9 percentage points.

Conclusion: SkillOps provides a low-overhead and method-agnostic framework for maintaining skill libraries, enhancing multi-step task performance while reducing technical debt and library-time computation costs.

Abstract: Large language model agents increasingly rely on skill libraries for multi-step tasks, yet these libraries can accumulate persistent defects as skills are added, reused, patched, and linked to changing dependencies. We call this failure mode skill technical debt: library-level defects that may not break a single skill locally but can harm future retrieval, composition, and execution. Existing skill-based agents mainly focus on task-time retrieval, planning, and repair, while library-time maintenance remains underexplored. We propose SkillOps, a method-agnostic plug-in framework for maintaining skill libraries. SkillOps represents each skill as a typed Skill Contract (P, O, A, V, F), organizes skills with a Hierarchical Skill Ecosystem Graph, and diagnoses library health across utility, compatibility, risk, and validation dimensions. Given a raw skill library, SkillOps produces a maintained library that can be used by existing retrieval or planning agents without changing their internal code. On ALFWorld, SkillOps achieves 79.5 percent task success as a standalone agent, outperforming the strongest baseline by 8.8 percentage points with no additional task-time large language model calls. As a plug-in layer, it improves retrieval-heavy baselines by 0.68 to 2.90 percentage points. The current rule-based maintenance implementation uses nearly zero library-time large language model calls or tokens, showing that skill-library maintenance can be added as a low-overhead architectural layer.

</details>


### [533] [(How) Do Large Language Models Understand High-Level Message Sequence Charts?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13773)
*Mohammad Reza Mousavi*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper evaluates the ability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to understand and perform semantic tasks related to High-Level Message Sequence Charts (HMSCs), reporting only modest overall understanding (52% accuracy) and highlighting specific limitations in handling advanced semantic concepts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether LLMs can consistently handle the formal semantics of architectural design specifications, particularly concerning High-Level Message Sequence Charts (HMSCs).

Method: The study examined three LLMs (Gemini-3, GPT-5.4, and Qwen-3.6) across 129 semantic tasks, testing various capabilities, from understanding basic semantic constructs to performing semantic-preserving transformations and reasoning about traces and labelled transition systems (LTSs).

Result: LLMs demonstrated varying performance: high accuracy (88%) in basic semantic concepts, but low accuracy (36-42%) in tasks requiring abstraction, composition, and reasoning concerning traces and LTSs. None of the models effectively handled co-region or explicit causal dependencies.

Conclusion: While LLMs show promise in understanding basic semantics of HMSCs, they are significantly limited in handling more complex semantic reasoning tasks, indicating substantial room for improvement in their application to formal architectural design semantics.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are being employed widely to automate tasks across the software development life-cycle. It is, however, unclear whether these tasks are performed consistently with respect to the semantics of the artefacts being handled. This question is particularly under-researched concerning architectural design specification. In this paper, we address this question for High-Level Message Sequence Charts (HMSCs). These are visual models with a rigorous formal semantics that have been used for various purposes, including as a foundation for Sequence Diagrams in the Unified Modelling Language (UML). We examine whether LLMs "understand" the semantics of HMSCs by examining three LLMs (Gemini-3, GPT-5.4, and Qwen-3.6) on how they perform 129 semantic tasks ranging from querying basic semantic constructs in HMSCs (i.e., events and their ordering) to semantic-preserving abstractions and compositions, and calculating the set of traces and trace-equivalent labelled transition systems. The results show that LLMs only have a modest understanding of the formal semantics of HMSCs (ca. 52% overall accuracy), with great variability across different semantic concepts: while LLMs seem to understand the basic semantic concepts of MSCs (ca. 88% accuracy), they struggle with semantic reasoning in tasks involving abstraction and composition (ca. 36% accuracy) and traces and LTSs (ca. 42% accuracy). In particular, all three LLMs struggle with the notions of co-region and explicit causal dependencies and never employed them in semantic-preserving transformations.

</details>


### [534] [Neurosymbolic Auditing of Natural-Language Software Requirements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13817)
*Bethel Hall,William Eiers*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper presents VERIMED, a pipeline combining large language models (LLMs) with SMT solvers to formalize and audit natural-language software requirements, especially in medical devices, enhancing safety and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the ambiguity, inconsistency, and underspecification of natural-language software requirements, especially in safety-critical domains like medical devices, which can lead to unsafe behavior and errors in formal verification and implementation.

Method: The authors propose VERIMED, a neurosymbolic pipeline that uses LLMs to translate natural-language requirements into formal logic and SMT solvers to perform analyses like ambiguity detection, vacuity checks, and safety violation testing.

Result: VERIMED demonstrates that stochastic variation in formalizations highlights ambiguity, and symbolic feedback with high granularity significantly improves requirement repair and verified accuracy (from 55.4% to 98.5%). It enables extensive, rigorous auditing on medical-device requirements.

Conclusion: VERIMED effectively reduces ambiguity in requirements and showcases how neurosymbolic techniques can enhance the safety and reliability of software in safety-critical domains like medical devices.

Abstract: Natural-language software requirements are often ambiguous, inconsistent, and underspecified; in safety-critical domains, these defects propagate into formal models that verify the wrong specification and into implementations that ship unsafe behavior. We show that large language models, equipped with an SMT solver, can audit such requirements: translating them into formal logic, detecting ambiguity through stochastic variation in the generated formalization, and exposing inconsistency, vacuousness, and safety violations through solver queries on the resulting specification. We present VERIMED, a neurosymbolic pipeline that operationalizes this idea for medical-device software requirements, and report two findings. First, stochastic variation across independent formalizations is a signal of ambiguity: requirements that admit multiple plausible interpretations produce SMT-inequivalent formalizations, and bidirectional SMT equivalence checking turns this disagreement into a solver-checkable test. Second, the usefulness of symbolic feedback depends on its granularity: in counterexample-guided repair on a hemodialysis question-answering benchmark, concrete SMT counterexamples raise verified accuracy from 55.4% to 98.5%. Over an extensive experimental evaluation on open-source hemodialysis safety requirements, we show that the LLM-based approach in VERIMED successfully reduces ambiguity-sensitive requirements and enables rigorous auditing of software requirements through SMT-based queries.

</details>


### [535] [EGSS: Entropy-guided Stepwise Scaling for Reliable Software Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.05242)
*Chenhui Mao,Yuanting Lei,Zhixiang Wei,Ming Liang,Zhixiang Wang,Jingxuan Xu,Dajun Chen,Wei Jiang,Yong Li*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper introduces Entropy-Guided Stepwise Scaling (EGSS), a method to boost TTS performance while cutting computational costs in tasks like code generation and bug fixing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the computational inefficiency and lack of a reliable solution selection in Agentic Test-Time Scaling (TTS), limiting its practical application in software engineering tasks.

Method: The proposed method, EGSS, uses entropy-guided adaptive search and test-suite augmentation to improve efficiency and effectiveness dynamically.

Result: EGSS enhances performance by 5–10%, reduces inference token usage by 28%, and achieves state-of-the-art results for open-source language models like GLM-4.6.

Conclusion: EGSS effectively improves both the accuracy and computational efficiency of TTS methods, making them more practical for real-world applications.

Abstract: Agentic Test-Time Scaling (TTS) has delivered state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on complex software engineering tasks such as code generation and bug fixing. However, its practical adoption remains limited due to significant computational overhead, primarily driven by two key challenges: (1) the high cost associated with deploying excessively large ensembles, and (2) the lack of a reliable mechanism for selecting the optimal candidate solution, ultimately constraining the performance gains that can be realized. To address these challenges, we propose Entropy-Guided Stepwise Scaling (EGSS), a novel TTS framework that dynamically balances efficiency and effectiveness through entropy-guided adaptive search and robust test-suite augmentation. Extensive experiments on SWE-Bench-Verified demonstrate that EGSS consistently boosts performance by 5-10% across all evaluated models. Specifically, it increases the resolved ratio of Kimi-K2-Intruct from 63.2% to 72.2%, and GLM-4.6 from 65.8% to 74.6%. Furthermore, when paired with GLM-4.6, EGSS achieves a new state-of-the-art among open-source large language models. In addition to these accuracy improvements, EGSS reduces inference-time token usage by over 28% compared to existing TTS methods, achieving simultaneous gains in both effectiveness and computational efficiency.

</details>


<div id='q-bio.NC'></div>

# q-bio.NC [[Back]](#toc)

### [536] [Information as Maximum-Caliber Deviation: A bridge between Integrated Information Theory and the Free Energy Principle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12536)
*Alexander Kearney*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: This paper bridges the Free Energy Principle (FEP) and Integrated Information Theory (IIT) through a new mathematical framework based on constrained entropy maximization (CMEP) and provides a unified explanation linking cognition, prediction error, and thermodynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The absence of rigorous mathematical mapping between FEP and IIT limits precision, testability, and their further application in understanding cognition and consciousness.

Method: The authors define information as $ψ$, a deviation in dynamics from a constrained MaxCal path ensemble, re-derive IIT's calculus via CMEP, and link it with active inference and predictive coding models using Markov chain and large deviation theory.

Result: The framework bridges FEP and IIT, showing $ψ$ is equivalent to prediction error under predictive coding models, and offers insights into the hill-shaped trajectory of Φ in neuronal responses to sensory input.

Conclusion: This study establishes a mathematically grounded connection between FEP, IIT, and thermodynamic frameworks, offering tools to extend these models to new dynamical regimes and study cognition and consciousness.

Abstract: The Free Energy Principle (FEP) is a leading framework for mathematically modeling self-organization and learning, while Integrated Information Theory (IIT) is a computational ontology of consciousness oriented around irreducible cause and effect. While conceptual unifications have been proposed and appear to be supported by empirical findings, the absence of a rigorous mathematical mapping places upper bounds on their precision and testability. This work proposes that information can be defined as the deviation $ψ$ of realized dynamics from a constrained maximum-caliber (MaxCal) path ensemble over a finite time horizon. Under this definition, each of the cause/effect repertoires central to IIT 3.0 emerge directly from MaxCal variational principles, allowing IIT's phenomenological calculus to be re-derived from constrained entropy-maximization (CMEP). This framework supplies a theoretical bridge to active inference, which is mathematically dual to CMEP under Langevin dynamics, and offers a principled route for extending IIT to new dynamical regimes. When the approach is applied under the Central Limit Theorem (CLT) for Markov chains and via large deviations theory (LDT) to Ising models, information $ψ$ is shown to be equivalent to prediction error under accompanying predictive coding models. This may hold relevance to the ``hill-shaped trajectory'' of $Φ$ observed in neuronal cultures adapting to sensory inputs. Together, these results provide a physically and mathematically grounded rationale for studying the convergence of FEP, IIT, and thermodynamic frameworks of cognition such as recent work grounding consciousness in violations of the Fluctuation-Dissipation Theorem (FDT).

</details>


### [537] [Why the Unfinished Keeps Returning: Canxianization and the Dynamics of Conscious Priority](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12543)
*Hengjin Cai,Tianqi Cai*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The study introduces 'Canxianization,' a process where certain perturbations repeatedly enter conscious awareness due to unresolved self-relevant factors, distinguishing it from memory persistence or emotional responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore why some conscious experiences persist or recur long after their initial cause has passed, beyond existing explanations like emotion, memory, or curiosity.

Method: The theory defines Canxianization as involving self-world boundary attribution, value-marking, blockage from closure, and metacognitive self-coupling. It also introduces indices like Recurrent Priority Index and Canxian Update Index for analysis.

Result: Canxianization explains recurrent conscious prioritization through unresolved self-world perturbations, identifying both productive and pathological patterns and introducing tests for artificial systems.

Conclusion: Canxianization is a unique process where unresolved, self-relevant experiences resist closure and return, offering insights into conscious recurrence and its applications in both human and artificial systems.

Abstract: Some conscious contents disappear after access; others return repeatedly, long after their triggering conditions have ceased. We propose Canxianization as the process by which a perturbation becomes closure-resistant self-relevant unfinishedness and thereby acquires recurrent conscious priority. The theory distinguishes this phenomenon from emotional arousal, memory strength, the Zeigarnik effect, curiosity, prediction error, and intrusive thought. A perturbation becomes canxianized when it is attributed to the self-world boundary, value-marked, blocked from causal or action closure, and metacognitively coupled to the self-model. We distinguish latent canxian strength from observed conscious recurrence, and introduce a Recurrent Priority Index and a Canxian Update Index to separate productive from pathological recurrence. Cold Canxianization, recurrence driven by structural incompleteness rather than affective arousal, is identified as a critical discriminant. Reset Resistance and Stake Transfer tests are proposed for artificial systems. Canxianization is not memory persistence; it is failed self-world repair. The unfinished does not merely remain. When it concerns the self and resists closure, it returns.

</details>


### [538] [Human face perception reflects inverse-generative and naturalistic discriminative objectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12619)
*Wenxuan Guo,Heiko H. Schütt,Kamila Maria Jozwik,Katherine R. Storrs,Nikolaus Kriegeskorte,Tal Golan*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: This paper investigates human face perception by using six deep neural network models trained on different tasks and comparing their predictions to human face-dissimilarity judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to uncover the computational mechanics behind human face recognition by leveraging deep neural network models as mechanistic hypotheses and distinguishing representational differences between models in the context of face perception.

Method: The researchers optimized face pairs to elicit contrasting predictions among six neural network models, tested these predictions on 864 human participants, and compared neural network predictions against human judgments under varied realism and pose conditions.

Result: Models trained to focus on high-level invariant structures and using datasets of natural images showed the highest alignment with human face-dissimilarity judgments, outperforming models with synthetic training.

Conclusion: Human face perception relies on mechanisms attuned to latent causes of facial appearance, effectively discounting irrelevant variations and shaped predominantly by exposure to natural image statistics.

Abstract: The perceptual representations supporting our ability to recognize faces remain a computational mystery. Deep neural networks offer mechanistic hypotheses for human face perception, but theoretically distinct models often make indistinguishable representational predictions for randomly sampled faces. To expose diagnostic differences among these hypotheses, we compared six neural network models sharing an architecture but trained on distinct tasks, using face pairs optimized to elicit contrasting model predictions ("controversial" pairs) alongside randomly sampled pairs. We tested model predictions against face-dissimilarity judgments from 864 human participants across stimulus sets differing in realism and pose variation. Models prioritizing high-level, invariant structures (trained via inverse rendering, face identification, or object classification) most robustly matched human judgments. Furthermore, models trained on natural images typically outperformed synthetic-trained counterparts. Together, these findings suggest that human face perception is shaped by mechanisms that infer latent causes of facial appearance, discount nuisance variation, and are tuned by natural image statistics.

</details>


### [539] [Predictive Coding Light+: learning to predict visual sequences with spike timing-dependent plasticity and synaptic delays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12732)
*Antony W. N'dri,Thomas Barbier,Céline Teulière,Jochen Triesch*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper introduces Predictive Coding Light+ (PCL+), a spiking neural network for unsupervised sequence processing and future prediction by maintaining past sensory information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding how biological and artificial systems predict future events by retaining past information, a crucial aspect of cognitive functionality.

Method: Developed Predictive Coding Light+ (PCL+), a spiking neural network that utilizes recurrent excitatory connections with delays to process sequences and retain recent past information in an unsupervised manner.

Result: The PCL+ network successfully reproduces findings on sequence learning in visual cortex and generates missing input predictions in a gesture recognition task.

Conclusion: Spiking neural networks, specifically PCL+, can effectively learn mechanisms for short-term memory retention and prediction capabilities in sequence tasks.

Abstract: The ability to predict the future is of great value for biological and artificial cognitive systems alike. However, successfully predicting the future typically requires maintaining a memory of the recent past. It is currently unclear how biological or artificial spiking neural networks can learn to maintain past sensory information to help predict the future. Here we propose Predictive Coding Light+ (PCL+), a spiking neural network architecture for unsupervised sequence processing that learns recurrent excitatory connections with delays to enable short-term retention of information. We show that the PCL+ network reproduces classic findings on sequence learning in visual cortex. Furthermore, it learns to ``fill in'' missing input in a challenging gesture recognition task. Overall, our work shows how spiking neural networks can learn recurrent excitatory connections with delays to maintain a record of the recent past and successfully predict the future.

</details>


### [540] [SpikeProphecy: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Autoregressive Neural Population Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12992)
*John R. Minnick,Jinghui Geng,Kamran Hussain,Jesus Gonzalez-Ferrer,Ash Robbins,Mohammed A. Mostajo-Radji,David Haussler,Jason K. Eshraghian,Mircea Teodorescu*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper introduces "SpikeProphecy," a benchmark for evaluating neural spike prediction models using new decomposed metrics instead of a simple aggregate Pearson correlation. It demonstrates their utility with analyses across 105 Neuropixels recordings (~89,800 neurons) and multiple baseline models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current neural population models are typically evaluated using a single Pearson correlation value, which masks important nuances of prediction quality in spike train forecasting.

Method: The authors propose "SpikeProphecy," a benchmark protocol that decomposes evaluation metrics into temporal fidelity, spatial pattern accuracy, and magnitude-invariant alignment. They apply it to a dataset with 105 Neuropixels sessions and evaluate seven model types, including transformers and spiking networks.

Result: The decomposition reveals how different brain regions vary in predictability, introduces insights into biophysical constraints on spike trains (sub-Poisson floor), and identifies shortcomings of KL-on-output-rates distillation for transferring knowledge between ANN and SNN models.

Conclusion: SpikeProphecy provides a meaningful and granular evaluation of spike prediction models, emphasizing the role of more nuanced evaluation metrics over simple aggregated scores.

Abstract: Neural population models, which predict the joint firing of many simultaneously recorded neurons forward in time, are typically evaluated by a single aggregate Pearson correlation $r$ between predicted and actual spike counts, a number that masks critical structure. We argue that how we evaluate spike forecasting matters as much as what we build, and introduce SpikeProphecy, the first large-scale benchmark for causal, autoregressive spike-count forecasting on real electrophysiology recordings. Our core contribution is a population metric decomposition that separates aggregate performance into temporal fidelity, spatial pattern accuracy, and magnitude-invariant alignment. The decomposition surfaces aspects of the underlying data that an aggregate scalar collapses together. We apply the protocol to 105 Neuropixels sessions (Steinmetz 2019 + IBL Repeated Site; ~89,800 neurons) with seven architecture baselines spanning four structural families: four SSMs (three diagonal and one non-diagonal), a Transformer, an LSTM, and a spiking network. The decomposition surfaces a brain-region predictability ranking that reproduces across all seven baselines and survives ANCOVA correction for firing-statistics constraints (region $ΔR^2 = 0.018$ above the firing-statistics covariates). It also exposes a sub-Poisson evaluation floor where rigorous metrics combine with genuine biophysical constraints on regular spike trains, and yields a negative result on KL-on-output-rates distillation for ANN-to-SNN transfer in this Poisson count domain.

</details>


### [541] [Implicit Behavioral Decoding from Next-Step Spike Forecasts at Population Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12999)
*John R. Minnick,Jesus Gonzalez-Ferrer,Kamran Hussain,Jinghui Geng,Ash Robbins,Mohammed A. Mostajo-Radji,David Haussler,Jason Eshraghian,Mircea Teodorescu*

Main category: q-bio.NC

TL;DR: The paper presents a model, Mamba, which predicts neural activity and decodes behavior more accurately compared to traditional methods using spike counts, tested on a large-scale dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve decoding accuracy of behavioral states and forecasting neural activity using neural populations in brain-computer interface applications.

Method: A neural network model named Mamba was trained on next-step spike counts to produce predicted rates, combined with a linear decoder for behavior classification.

Result: Mamba achieved a decoding accuracy of 75.7% for mouse choice and 66.1% for stimulus side, outperforming linear decoders directly using raw spike counts under matched temporal contexts.

Conclusion: Mamba is an efficient and accurate method for predicting neural activity and decoding behavior, fitting within computational constraints for real-time BCI applications.

Abstract: Closed-loop brain-computer interfaces often require both a forecast of upcoming neural population activity and a readout of the animal's behavioral state. A single Mamba forecaster, trained only on next-step spike counts at Neuropixels scale, can deliver both in one forward pass. A lightweight per-session linear head reading the model's predicted rates decodes behavior better than the same linear classifier reading the raw spike counts, under matched temporal context. We test on the Steinmetz visual-discrimination benchmark, which spans 39 sessions, roughly 27,000 neurons, and 1,994 held-out trials. Across three training seeds, Mamba's predicted rates decode mouse choice at 75.7$\pm$0.2% trial vote, roughly 2.3 times chance level, and stimulus side at 66.1$\pm$0.6%, about twice chance. Compared to a matched 500 ms-context linear decoder on the raw spike counts, Mamba wins at trial vote by 4-6 pp on response and 4-6 pp on stimulus side. A session-start calibration block of about 100-150 trials brings the readout within 1-2 pp of asymptote, and the full pipeline fits inside the 50 ms bin budget on workstation-class GPUs typical of tethered chronic Neuropixels recordings.

</details>


<div id='stat.ML'></div>

# stat.ML [[Back]](#toc)

### [542] [Online Conformal Prediction: Enforcing monotonicity via Online Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12668)
*Eduardo Ochoa Rivera,Ambuj Tewari*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces two novel methods for online conformal prediction that provide nested prediction sets across multiple coverage levels, ensuring stable and efficient uncertainty quantification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many applications require calibrated uncertainty estimates across a range of coverage levels to suit heterogeneous risk tolerances, which existing methods do not address effectively.

Method: The authors propose two online conformal prediction approaches using an online optimization framework to enforce nested prediction sets and control quantile estimation errors.

Result: The proposed methods achieve stable coverage across coverage levels, strictly nested prediction sets, and improved efficiency over existing baselines in synthetic and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: The methods provide a new solution for simultaneous uncertainty quantification, ensuring both interpretability and improved statistical efficiency, making them suitable for diverse applications with varying risk requirements.

Abstract: Conformal prediction provides a principled framework for uncertainty quantification with finite-sample coverage guarantees. While recent work has extended conformal prediction to online and sequential settings, existing methods typically focus on a single coverage level and do not ensure consistency across multiple confidence levels. In many real-world applications, such as weather forecasting, macroeconomic prediction, and risk management, different users operate under heterogeneous risk tolerances and require calibrated uncertainty estimates across a range of coverage levels. In such settings, it is desirable to produce prediction sets corresponding to different coverage levels that are nested and valid simultaneously. In this paper, we propose two novel online conformal prediction methods that output \emph{nested prediction sets} across a range of coverage levels, enabling simultaneous uncertainty quantification across the entire risk spectrum. Beyond interpretability, jointly estimating multiple coverage levels is known to improve statistical efficiency in classical quantile regression by enforcing non-crossing constraints and sharing information across quantiles. Our approaches leverage an online optimization perspective with small regret that translates to quantile estimation error control while enforcing nestedness of prediction sets. Empirical results on synthetic and real-world datasets, including applications in forecasting tasks with heterogeneous risk requirements, demonstrate that our method achieves stable coverage across all levels, strictly nested prediction sets, and improved efficiency compared to existing online conformal baselines.

</details>


### [543] [A Unified Framework for Critical Scaling of Inverse Temperature in Self-Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12697)
*Tomohiro Hayase,Ryo Karakida*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper offers a unified theory for selecting the proper scale for length-dependent logit rescaling in long-context self-attention to stabilize operations and explains existing competing scaling laws.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing analyses and methods for length-dependent logit rescaling in self-attention provide conflicting scaling laws based on context length, creating uncertainty about the appropriate approach.

Method: The authors introduce a general theory that uses the gap-counting function $N_n$ to count competitors near the maximum. They define an upper-tail accumulation scale and demonstrate its role in determining critical inverse-temperature scales for softmax concentration.

Result: The proposed framework consolidates previous competing scaling laws under a single theoretical structure, bridging idealized scenarios and practical transformer implementations, thus acting as a diagnostic tool.

Conclusion: A unified understanding of length-dependent logit rescaling has been established, clarifying how scaling works and offering practical diagnostics for self-attention scaling models.

Abstract: Length-dependent logit rescaling is widely used to stabilize long-context self-attention, but existing analyses and methods suggest conflicting inverse-temperature laws for the context length $n$, ranging from $(\log n)^{1/2}$ to $\log n$ and $(\log n)^2$. We provide a general theory showing that the desirable scale is determined by the gap-counting function $N_n$ of each attention row. Counting how many competitors lie within each gap from the maximum, we define an upper-tail accumulation scale and prove that it gives the critical inverse-temperature scale for softmax concentration: below this scale, the top competitors remain unseparated, whereas above it, the attention entropy collapses. This framework unifies prior scaling laws as different $N_n$ and yields a direct diagnostic for attention-score families, from idealized theoretical models to more practical transformers.

</details>


### [544] [ISOMORPH: A Supply Chain Digital Twin for Simulation, Dataset Generation, and Forecasting Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12768)
*Zhizhen Zhang,Hyemin Gu,Benjamin J. Zhang,Daniel Elenius,Michael Tyrrell,Theo J. Bourdais,Houman Owhadi,Markos A. Katsoulakis,Tuhin Sahai*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: ISOMORPH is introduced as the first open-source public digital twin of a logistics network with interpretable parameters and modular features, enhancing time series forecasting (TSF) benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of supply-chain logistics coverage in open TSF benchmarks.

Method: ISOMORPH simulates a logistics network evolving through discrete time and is built with Markov chain dynamics in a state space. It incorporates modular scenarios and datasets for high configurability.

Result: ISOMORPH generates realistic dynamics like the bullwhip effect, exhibits features like variance amplification and regime shifts absent from existing benchmarks, and evaluates four foundation models exceeding standard forecasting references.

Conclusion: ISOMORPH enhances TSF benchmarks by offering dynamics unavailable in existing datasets, supports foundation model evaluation, and enables uncertainty quantification, making it a valuable research tool in logistics.

Abstract: Open time-series forecasting (TSF) benchmarks cover retail, energy, weather, and traffic, but supply-chain logistics remains underserved. We introduce ISOMORPH, the first public digital twin of a multi-echelon logistics network with fully interpretable, user-configurable parameters and modular topology, demand process, and control rules. The simulator advances a directed routing graph in discrete time: demand arrives at the destination, is served from stock or recorded as backlog, and triggers replenishment through the network. The state vector tracks per-node on-hand inventory with outstanding orders, in-transit shipments, and a smoothed demand estimate, so the dynamics close as a Markov chain on a tractable state space whose transition kernel acts linearly on the empirical distribution of the state. The released data reproduces the bullwhip effect at empirically consistent magnitudes, and three conservation laws encoded in the Markov chain serve as verification tools when users extend the simulator. We release datasets at two catalogue scales ($C=50$ and $C=200$) with six scenario sweeps producing 30 additional rollouts and 20 Latin-hypercube perturbations, exhibiting dynamics absent from fixed TSF benchmarks: variance amplification, cascading bottlenecks, regime shifts, and cross-channel coupling through shared macro shocks. Zero-shot evaluation of four foundation models (Chronos, Moirai, TimesFM, Lag-Llama) shows MASE values exceeding public GIFT-Eval references at low-to-moderate horizons, supporting incorporation into existing benchmarks. The same pairing produces forecast confidence bands via Latin-hypercube perturbation of demand-side knobs, forward UQ from parameter uncertainty unavailable on standard TSF datasets, demonstrating that foundation models can serve as fast surrogates for the digital twin's forward UQ. Code (MIT): https://github.com/tuhinsahai/ISOMORPH.

</details>


### [545] [Robust Sequential Experimental Design for A/B Testing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12899)
*Qianglin Wen,Xiangkun Wu,Chengchun Shi,Ting Li,Niansheng Tang,Yingying Zhang,Hongtu Zhu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper focuses on robust experimental design methods to improve sample efficiency in A/B testing while accounting for model misspecification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing experimental designs in A/B testing are highly dependent on correctly specified models, which poses challenges under model misspecification.

Method: The authors propose a unified framework applicable to both contextual bandit and dynamic settings. They ensure the worst-case mean squared error of the treatment effect estimation is bounded.

Result: Analytical proofs validate the framework theoretically, while empirical evaluations on synthetic and real-world data showcase its effectiveness.

Conclusion: The study presents a robust and effective experimental design technique that mitigates risks in settings with model misspecification, enhancing reliability in treatment effect estimations.

Abstract: Experimental design has emerged as a powerful approach for improving the sample efficiency of A/B testing, yet existing designs rely critically on correctly specified models. We study robust sequential experimental design under model misspecification and develop a unified framework that covers both contextual bandit and dynamic settings. Theoretically, we prove that our design bounds the worst-case mean squared error of the estimated treatment effect. Empirically, we demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach using synthetic and real-world datasets from a leading technology company.

</details>


### [546] [The Mechanism of Weak-to-Strong Generalization: Feature Elicitation from Latent Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12908)
*Ryoya Awano,Taiji Suzuki*

Main category: stat.ML
