TL;DR: This work studies Weak-to-Strong (W2S) generalization in reward-model learning and demonstrates its capability to fine-tune a strong model using a weaker model while preserving pre-trained features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address the open question of whether multi-step Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) can enable feature learning in strong models while also maintaining diverse pre-trained capabilities in a Weak-to-Strong (W2S) generalization setup.

Method: The study focuses on fine-tuning a pre-trained strong model with low-dimensional subspace representations using the outputs of a task-specialized weak model in reward-model learning involving two-layer neural networks. Theoretical analyses and numerical experiments using synthetic data support the approach.

Result: The strong model efficiently learns the target task κ by leveraging its pre-trained subspace features and retains general capabilities. Additionally, W2S avoids catastrophic forgetting of off-target features, unlike standard supervised fine-tuning.

Conclusion: The paper establishes W2S generalization in the feature-learning regime and demonstrates its effectiveness, both theoretically and empirically, in preserving a model's general pre-trained knowledge while learning specialized tasks.

Abstract: Weak-to-strong (W2S) generalization, in which a strong model is fine-tuned on outputs of a weaker, task-specialized model, has been proposed as an approach to aligning superhuman AI systems. Existing theoretical analyses either fix the student's representations or operate in restricted settings. Whether multi-step SGD can succeed in feature learning while preserving diverse pre-trained capabilities remains open. We study W2S in the setting of reward-model learning with two-layer neural networks. The strong model has pre-trained representations organized into low-dimensional subspaces $V_k$, and is fine-tuned under the supervision of a weak model specialized on task $κ$. We prove that the strong model efficiently learns task $κ$, eliciting its pre-trained knowledge while retaining general capabilities. This establishes W2S generalization in the feature-learning regime, in the sense that the strong model acquires the target feature direction through W2S training, rather than having it given a priori. Moreover, W2S preserves pre-trained off-target features, whereas standard supervised fine-tuning causes catastrophic forgetting when off-target feature directions are correlated with the target's. Numerical experiments on synthetic data confirm our theoretical results.

</details>


### [547] [When Should an AI Workflow Release? Always-Valid Inference for Black-Box Generate-Verify Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12947)
*Young Hyun Cho,Will Wei Sun*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper proposes a wrapper for iterative AI generate-evaluate-revise workflows to manage release decisions with statistical reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI workflows require iteration to improve outputs but lack robust statistical methods to decide when to release a result.

Method: The proposed wrapper uses a hard-negative reference pool and an e-process to calibrate scores and allow valid decisions under optional stopping.

Result: The wrapper provides finite-sample control over the probability of incorrect releases and improves task feasibility handling in a coding case study.

Conclusion: The wrapper enhances the reliability of release decisions while maintaining the ability to responsibly handle uncertain tasks.

Abstract: LLM-enabled AI workflows increasingly produce outputs through iterative generate-evaluate-revise loops. Each iteration can improve the candidate, but it also creates a release decision: when to stop and output the current result? This raises a statistical challenge because deployment-time evaluator scores are adaptively generated and repeatedly monitored, yet the likelihood models or exchangeability assumptions typically used for calibration are unavailable. We propose an always-valid release wrapper for existing generator-evaluator pipelines. The wrapper builds a hard-negative reference pool of high-scoring failures, calibrates deployment-time evaluator scores against this pool, and accumulates the resulting evidence with an e-process. This separates two roles: the reference pool turns black-box scores into conservative evidence, while the e-process provides validity under optional stopping. In theory, we show that a conservative reference pool yields finite-sample control of the probability of releasing on infeasible tasks, that is, tasks for which the given workflow is not capable of producing a reliable solution. We also characterize conditions under which the same conservative rule still achieves nontrivial release on feasible tasks. In an MBPP+ coding-agent case study, the wrapper reduces premature incorrect release relative to baseline stopping rules while still releasing on tasks for which the workflow repeatedly accumulates moderate supporting evidence.

</details>


### [548] [Coreset-Induced Conditional Velocity Flow Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12951)
*Xiao Wang,Zihua She,Jianxi Su*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces CCVFM, a generative model that enhances hierarchical flow matching with a coreset-informed source distribution for efficient multimodal target generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors sought to address the inefficiency of hierarchical rectified flow in transporting isotropic Gaussian noise to multimodal target velocities from scratch by replacing the inner source with a data-driven surrogate.

Method: They employ an entropic Sinkhorn coreset to condense the target distribution into Gaussian mixtures, removing the need for a learned neural sampler. A correction flow is then trained to address the residual gap between the surrogate and target distributions.

Result: The paper demonstrates that CCVFM achieves competitive generative performance across multiple datasets (e.g., MNIST, CIFAR-10, ImageNet-32, CelebA-HQ) while using fewer steps and maintaining model quality.

Conclusion: CCVFM efficiently combines a coreset-informed source distribution with lightweight flow correction, offering both theoretical and empirical advantages for generative modeling tasks.

Abstract: We propose Coreset-Induced Conditional Velocity Flow Matching (CCVFM), a generative model that augments hierarchical rectified flow with a data-informed source distribution. Hierarchical flow matching models the full conditional velocity law in velocity space, but its inner flow is asked to transport isotropic Gaussian noise to a multimodal target velocity distribution from scratch. Our key observation is that this inner source can be replaced by a closed-form surrogate built from a coreset of the target. CCVFM first compresses the target into weighted atoms using an entropic Sinkhorn coreset and lifts them to a Gaussian mixture. The induced conditional velocity law is then a closed-form Gaussian mixture that can be sampled without a learned neural sampler. A lightweight correction flow, trained from this exact surrogate source, then refines the remaining surrogate-to-target residual rather than learning an entire noise-to-data map. We prove that the surrogate transport cost equals the target--surrogate Wasserstein gap under an explicit compression assumption, whereas the noise-source analogue has a dimension-scale lower bound. We further characterize the conditional second moment of the direct surrogate-source training target and show that its source-dependent excess is small when the surrogate conditional law is close to the true conditional velocity law in mean and covariance. Empirically, on MNIST, CIFAR-10, ImageNet-32, and CelebA-HQ, the proposed method reaches competitive few-step generation under matched architectures.

</details>


### [549] [Adaptive Kernel Density Estimation with Pre-training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13092)
*Ruitong Zhang,Ke Deng*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces pre-training using neural networks to improve density estimation accuracy in high-dimensional settings, addressing challenges in traditional kernel smoothing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional density estimation methods struggle in high dimensions due to difficulties in selecting appropriate location-adaptive kernels.

Method: The authors propose using a pre-trained neural network to recommend location-adaptive kernels for sample points, improving efficiency and accuracy in high-dimensional density estimation.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate that the pre-trained approach is effective when the target distribution aligns with the pre-training family, although performance may drop when distributions differ; fine-tuning can overcome this limitation.

Conclusion: Pre-training enables accurate density estimation in high dimensions with adaptive kernels, effectively leveraging neural networks to improve statistical methods.

Abstract: Density estimation in high-dimensional settings is an important and challenging statistical problem.Traditional methods based on kernel smoothing are inefficient in high dimensions due to the difficulties in specifying appropriate location-adaptive kernels. In this work, we introduce pre-training, a key idea behind many cutting-edge AI technologies, to the context of non-parametric density estimation. By establishing a pre-trained neural network that can recommend an appropriate location-adaptive kernel for each sample point, efficient density estimation with adaptive kernels is achieved in high dimensions. A wide range of numerical experiments show that this strategy is highly effective for improving density-estimation accuracy, when the target distribution is close to the distribution family for pre-training. When the target distribution is substantially different from the pre-training distribution family, the benefit from the proposed pre-training strategy may be diluted, but can be reactivated by an additional fine-tuning procedure.

</details>


### [550] [State-of-art minibatches via novel DPP kernels: discretization, wavelets, and rough objectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13127)
*Hoang-Son Tran,Pranav Gupta,Rémi Bardenet,Subhroshekhar Ghosh*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper introduces wavelet-based determinantal point processes (DPPs) for subsampling tasks, providing better accuracy and a practical method to create discrete kernels for machine learning purposes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to address two issues in DPP-based subsampling for machine learning: the limited number of variance-reducing continuous DPPs and the lack of an effective way to construct discrete DPPs while inheriting the variance reduction properties.

Method: The authors propose new Euclidean space wavelet-based DPPs with improved accuracy guarantees and develop a mechanism to convert these continuous DPPs into discrete kernels that retain low-rank structure and enable efficient sampling.

Result: The proposed methods enhance the utility of DPPs for ML tasks, extend their applicability to less regular objective functions, and allow efficient and variance-preserving subsampling methods.

Conclusion: Wavelet-based DPPs and the novel kernel conversion method make DPP-based subsampling more practical and effective for diverse machine learning applications, achieving provably better variance reduction and adaptability to regularity.

Abstract: Determinantal point processes (DPPs) have emerged as a kernelized alternative to vanilla independent sampling for generating efficient minibatches, coresets and other parsimonious representations of large-scale datasets. While theoretical foundations and promising empirical performance have been demonstrated, there are two challenges for current proposals for DPP-based coresets or minibatches. The first is the need for families of DPPs with certain key variance reduction properties, usually constructed in a continuous setting, of which there are few known examples. The second is the need for an ad-hoc construction of a discrete DPP defined on a given dataset, that inherits such variance reduction. In this work, we contribute to the programme of establishing DPPs as a subsampling toolbox for ML by advancing on these two fronts. First, we propose new DPPs on the Euclidean space based on wavelets, with provably better accuracy guarantees than the best known rates. Second, we introduce a general method to convert such continuous DPPs, which are more amenable to proving analytical statements, into discrete kernels, which are pertinent for subsampling tasks such as minibatch and coreset constructions. This conversion mechanism simultaneously preserves the desired variance decay and reveals a low-rank decomposition of the discrete kernel, which makes sampling the corresponding DPP computationally inexpensive. En route, we enlarge the class of ML tasks amenable to improvements via DPP-based minibatches and coresets to include objective functions with arbitrarily low regularity, and rate guarantees that explicitly adapt to this regularity.

</details>


### [551] [Amortized Neural Clustering of Time Series based on Statistical Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13128)
*Ángel López-Oriona,Ying Sun*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper proposes a new approach for time series clustering using amortized neural inference, minimizing reliance on traditional clustering methods and heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The clustering of time series often depends on traditional methods like K-means, which require predefined objectives and are sensitive to heuristics. This paper aims to reduce these dependencies while addressing the challenges of predefined cluster shapes and numbers.

Method: The approach trains neural networks to infer an optimal partitioning rule based on statistical features (e.g., autocorrelations). It also includes a version that automatically determines the number of clusters.

Result: Empirical studies demonstrate that the method achieves competitive or superior clustering accuracy compared to traditional methods, even when those methods are given the true number of clusters.

Conclusion: The framework allows for automated, adaptive, and data-driven time series clustering, reducing the need for prior algorithm selection, calibration, and domain-specific clustering knowledge.

Abstract: This paper introduces an algorithm-agnostic approach to feature-based time series clustering via amortized neural inference. By training neural networks to approximate the optimal partitioning rule from simulated data, the proposed framework reduces reliance on conventional clustering methods, such as $K$-means, $K$-medoids, or hierarchical clustering, and their associated objective functions and heuristics. Leveraging statistical features, such as autocorrelations and quantile autocorrelations, the approach learns a data-driven affinity structure from which clustering partitions can be recovered, without requiring explicit prior specification of cluster shapes or structures. In addition, one version of the method can automatically determine the number of clusters, avoiding ad-hoc selection procedures. Comprehensive empirical studies show that the proposed framework achieves competitive or superior clustering accuracy relative to traditional methods, even in challenging scenarios where competing techniques are provided with the true number of clusters. An application to financial time series of stock returns illustrates its practical utility. By reducing the need for algorithm selection and calibration, the proposed framework opens new possibilities for automated, adaptive, and data-driven clustering of temporal data across scientific and industrial domains.

</details>


### [552] [On Hallucinations in Inverse Problems: Fundamental Limits and Provable Assessment Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13146)
*David Iagaru,Nina M. Gottschling,Anders C. Hansen,Josselin Garnier*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper investigates why AI hallucinations occur in imaging inverse problems and introduces theoretical and practical methods to understand and measure them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the challenges caused by AI hallucinations—realistic but incorrect details—in imaging inverse problems, which undermine reliability, especially in the absence of ground truth data.

Method: The authors derive a theoretical framework with conditions for hallucinations and computable bounds on their magnitude. They also propose algorithms to estimate minimum hallucination magnitudes and assess the faithfulness of reconstructed details.

Result: The experiments span three imaging tasks and demonstrate that the proposed methods effectively apply to diverse scenarios, including the evaluation of modern generative models.

Conclusion: This work provides a principled framework and tools to quantify and evaluate AI hallucinations, offering insights for improving the reliability of AI-driven imaging solutions.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has transformed imaging inverse problems, from medical diagnostics to Earth observation. Yet deep neural networks can produce hallucinations, realistic-looking but incorrect details, undermining their reliability, especially when ground truth data is unavailable. We develop a theoretical framework showing that such hallucinations are not merely artifacts of particular models, but can arise from the ill-posed nature of the inverse problem itself. We derive necessary and sufficient conditions for hallucinations, together with computable bounds on their magnitude that depend only on the forward model. Building on this theory, we introduce algorithms to: (1) estimate the minimum hallucination magnitude achievable by any reconstruction model for a given input; (2) assess the faithfulness of reconstructed details by a given reconstruction model. Experiments across three imaging tasks demonstrate that our approach applies broadly, including to modern generative models, and provides a principled way to quantify and evaluate AI hallucinations.

</details>


### [553] [Generative Modeling of Approximately Periodic Time Series by a Posterior-Weighted Gaussian Process](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13150)
*Elias Reich,Saverio Messineo,Stefan Huber*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new stochastic model for approximately periodic time series using Gaussian Processes (GP) with a novel kernel.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modeling repetitive structures in industrial and cyber-physical systems is difficult due to their mixed characteristics—regular structural patterns and variability between repetitions.

Method: The presented model decouples structures within repetitions from variations between repetitions using a two-stage construction and a novel kernel in Gaussian Processes (GP).

Result: The proposed model generates realistic synthetic trajectories from toy datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness.

Conclusion: The approach effectively addresses the challenge of modeling approximately periodic behaviors by balancing structural regularities and variability.

Abstract: Discrete automated processes in industrial and cyber-physical systems often exhibit a repetitive structure in which successive repetitions follow a common trajectory while differing in duration, amplitude, and fine-scale dynamics. Such \emph{approximately periodic} behavior poses a challenge for Gaussian Processes (GP) modeling: strictly periodic models suppress inter-repetition variability, while non-periodic models fail to capture the strong structural regularities required for generation. In this work, we propose a stochastic generative model for approximately periodic time series. The model is based on a GP whose posterior is modulated by a novel kernel. Our approach decouples intra-repetition structure from inter-repetition variability through a two-stage construction which yields a generative distribution with a identical mean function across repetitions, while allowing smooth variation between repetitions. The modeling choices are supported by an implementation in which realistic synthetic trajectories are generated from toy datasets.

</details>


### [554] [Kernel-based guarantees for nonlinear parametric models in Bayesian optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13160)
*Rafael Oliveira*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper provides a theoretical framework to analyze nonlinear parametric models used for Bayesian optimization under adaptive data collection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a theoretical gap in analyzing nonlinear parametric models, as current analyses primarily focus on simpler models like Gaussian processes, ignoring nonlinear models often used in practice.

Method: A kernel-based framework is proposed that uses kernels over parameter space to build the theory for adaptively trained nonlinear parametric models under regularized convex losses.

Result: The framework provides confidence bounds for trained models and convergence guarantees for nonlinear acquisition and surrogate models, including randomized policies.

Conclusion: This approach bridges the theoretical gap for nonlinear parametric models, enabling a unified analysis in adaptive optimization contexts like Bayesian optimization.

Abstract: Modern Bayesian optimization and adaptive sampling methods increasingly rely on nonlinear parametric models, yet theoretical guarantees for such models under adaptive data collection remain limited. Existing analyses largely focus on Gaussian processes, kernel machines, linear models, or linearized neural approximations, leaving a gap between theory and the nonlinear models used in practice. We develop a kernel based framework for analyzing regularized nonlinear parametric models trained on adaptively collected data. Our approach uses kernels over the parameter space to induce reproducing kernel Hilbert space structures over the corresponding model class, yielding confidence bounds for models trained with broad classes of regularized convex losses. We show how these bounds can support convergence guarantees for nonlinear acquisition and surrogate models, including randomized regularized policies that select points by maximizing a trained random model. These results provide a unified route to analyzing nonlinear parametric models in Bayesian optimization and related adaptive optimization settings.

</details>


### [555] [Coupling-Informed Transport Maps for Bayesian Filtering in Nonlinear Dynamical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13174)
*Dengfei Zeng,Lijian Jiang,Shuyu Sun,Dunhui Xiao*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces a likelihood-free transport filtering method leveraging block-triangular transport maps to address nonlinear, non-Gaussian filtering challenges, minimizing maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) and avoiding particle collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve filtering in nonlinear, non-Gaussian scenarios while avoiding issues like particle collapse encountered in traditional methods.

Method: Exploits block-triangular structures and employs a training-free transport filter using gradient flows to analytically compute the transport map for optimal MMD minimization.

Result: The method performs better in approximating non-Gaussian filtering posteriors, avoids particle collapse, and scales to high-dimensional problems through domain localization.

Conclusion: The proposed approach is effective and superior to traditional methods for nonlinear, non-Gaussian filtering, validated with numerical examples.

Abstract: A likelihood-free transport filtering method is proposed based on the couplings between state and observation variables. By exploiting a block-triangular structure in the transport map, the analysis step of filtering is reformulated as the minimization of the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between the true joint measure and its transport-based approximation. To circumvent the non-convexity in the MMD optimization, we introduce a training-free transport filter method via gradient flows, which leads to an analytic computation for the transport map that implies the steepest descent direction of the MMD. The proposed approach accurately approximates non-Gaussian filtering posteriors and avoids particle collapse. We provide a convergence analysis for the expectation of the MMD between the approximated posterior and the truth posterior. Finally, we extend the method to high-dimensional problems through domain localization. Numerical examples demonstrate the superior performance of our approach over conventional filtering methods in nonlinear, non-Gaussian scenarios.

</details>


### [556] [LLMs as Implicit Imputers: Uncertainty Should Scale with Missing Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13188)
*Stef van Buuren*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper examines how large language models (LLMs) handle uncertainty under incomplete context and evaluates them through multiple imputation (MI) criteria. It finds entropy-based uncertainty measures more reliable than confidence-based ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in settings where LLMs operate under incomplete or degraded context and to introduce better uncertainty evaluation methods for these conditions.

Method: A controlled experiment on the SQuAD dataset assessing the effects of varying context availability. The study utilizes two answer-level uncertainty measures: sampling-based confidence and response entropy.

Result: Confidence levels remained high despite reduced accuracy under increasing information gaps, failing to reflect uncertainty. Entropy, however, effectively scaled with context removal and explained more variance in accuracy.

Conclusion: Entropy is a more reliable measure of uncertainty than confidence in LLM applications under incomplete context conditions.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in settings where the available context is incomplete or degraded. We argue that an LLM generating answers under incomplete context can be viewed as an implicit imputer, and evaluated against a criterion from the multiple imputation (MI) literature: uncertainty should scale with the amount of missing information. We assess this criterion on SQuAD, using a controlled framework in which context availability is varied across five levels. We evaluate two answer-level uncertainty measures that can be estimated from repeated sampling: sampling-based confidence (empirical mode frequency) and response entropy. Confidence fails to reflect increasing missingness: it remains high even as accuracy collapses. Entropy, by contrast, increases with context removal, consistent with the MI analogy, and explains substantially more variance in accuracy than confidence across all evidence levels (quadratic $R^2$ gap up to 0.057). We further introduce a black-box diagnostic $ρ_R(α)$ that estimates the proportion of baseline uncertainty resolved by context level $α$, requiring only repeated sampling with and without context. These results suggest that entropy is a more responsive black-box uncertainty measure than confidence under incomplete context.

</details>


### [557] [The Sample Complexity of Multiple Change Point Identification under Bandit Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13252)
*Maximilian Graf,Victor Thuot*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces an adaptive algorithm to identify change points in a noisy piecewise-constant function efficiently under target precision and confidence levels, with theoretical and empirical insights into its sample complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identifying change points efficiently within noisy, piecewise-constant functions is crucial for many applications but is challenging due to the need for precision and confidence while minimizing samples.

Method: The proposed adaptive algorithm detects intervals likely containing change points, refines their locations to the desired precision, and establishes theoretical sample budgets and their dependencies.

Result: The study provides non-asymptotic upper and lower sample complexity bounds, demonstrating that sample complexity is influenced by both jump magnitudes and relative positions of the change points.

Conclusion: The paper expands previous findings by showing that sample complexity depends not just on jump magnitudes (asymptotically for δ → 0) but also on the spatial arrangement of change points for general δ and η.

Abstract: We study multiple change point localization under bandit feedback. An unknown piecewise-constant function on a compact interval can be queried sequentially at adaptively chosen inputs, and each query returns a noisy evaluation of the function. The goal is to identify a prescribed number of discontinuities, known as change points, within a target precision $η$ and confidence level $1-δ$, while using as few samples as possible. We propose an adaptive algorithm that first detects intervals likely to contain change points and then refines their locations to precision $η$. We establish non-asymptotic upper bounds on its sample budget, together with corresponding lower bounds. Prior work shows that jump magnitudes alone determine the asymptotic sample complexity as $δ\to 0$. We reveal that this picture is incomplete beyond this regime. We demonstrate, both empirically and theoretically, that for general $δ$ and $η$, the complexity is jointly governed by the jumps and the relative positions of the change points.

</details>


### [558] [Learning Perturbations to Extrapolate Your LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13284)
*Zetai Cen,Chenfei Gu,Jin Zhu,Ting Li,Yunxiao Chen,Chengchun Shi*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper proposes a novel method to enhance large language models' extrapolation performance by applying continuous learnable transformations to token prefixes instead of discrete perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for enhancing extrapolation in large language models focus on fixed, discrete perturbations, which limit flexibility and adaptability.

Method: The proposed framework perturbs token prefixes using a learnable transformation of a continuous latent vector in embedding space, and optimizes model parameters using stochastic gradient descent with unbiased estimating equations.

Result: The proposed approach demonstrated significant improvements in out-of-domain performance across synthetic and real-world datasets compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Learnable continuous perturbations provide a flexible and effective strategy for boosting extrapolation capability in large language models, outperforming existing fixed perturbation approaches.

Abstract: Recent advancements in large language models demonstrate that injecting perturbations can substantially enhance extrapolation performance. However, current approaches often rely on discrete perturbations with fixed designs, which limits their flexibility. In this work, we propose a framework where token prefixes are perturbed by a learnable transformation of a continuous latent vector within an embedding space. To overcome the challenge of an intractable marginal likelihood, we derive unbiased estimating equations for model parameters and optimize them via stochastic gradient descent. We establish the statistical properties of the resulting estimator in over-parameterized regimes. Empirical evaluations on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our proposal yields significant gains in out-of-domain settings over a range of state-of-the-art baseline methods.

</details>


### [559] [On the Limits of Latent Reuse in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13448)
*Yifeng Yu,Lu Yu*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper investigates the reliability of reusing latent spaces for diffusion models under distribution shifts, emphasizing the impact of subspace misalignment and noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the uncertainty of reusing trained latent spaces for datasets with distribution shifts in diffusion models.

Method: The study evaluates latent reuse reliability by analyzing principal angle misalignment between dataset subspaces and noise amplification at different diffusion time scales. It also explores mixed source-target training.

Result: Freezing latent spaces is influenced by misalignment and ambient noise. Mixed training was shown to depend on shared latent dimensions and the geometry of distributions.

Conclusion: Theoretical insights are provided on when latent reuse is feasible, guiding the need for shared representations under certain conditions.

Abstract: Diffusion models are often trained in low-dimensional latent spaces, which are then reused for related but shifted datasets. In this work, we study when such latent reuse remains reliable under distribution shift. We consider a source-target setting in which both datasets are approximately low-dimensional but may lie near different subspaces. We show that freezing and reusing a source latent space induces a target-domain score error governed by two quantities: the principal-angle misalignment between the source and target subspaces, and the target ambient noise amplified by the diffusion time scale. Motivated by these limits, we further study mixed source-target training and characterize how the required shared latent dimension depends on the relative geometry of the two distributions. Our results provide theoretical guidance on when latent reuse is reliable and when learning a shared representation may be necessary.

</details>


### [560] [Reframing preprocessing selection as model-internal calibration in near-infrared spectroscopy: A large-scale benchmark of operator-adaptive PLS and Ridge models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13587)
*Gregory Beurier,Robin Reiter,Camille Noûs,Lauriane Rouan,Denis Cornet*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces operator-adaptive calibration for NIRS, integrating spectral preprocessing into calibration models to achieve faster, robust, and interpretable results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable calibration in NIRS heavily depends on preprocessing methods, which are often inefficient, unstable, and non-transparent in practice due to reliance on large external pipeline searches.

Method: The proposed framework encodes preprocessing as linear spectral operators, handling nonlinear corrections as fold-specific branches. Implementations include variants of PLS and Ridge regression, evaluated on over 50 NIRS datasets compared to conventional approaches.

Result: Operator-adaptive PLS with ASLS preprocessing achieved a median RMSEP/PLS ratio of 0.960 with superior performance in 42 out of 57 datasets, while an AOM-Ridge selector outperformed tuned Ridge by a median 2.22% improvement over 52 datasets.

Conclusion: Operator-adaptive calibration reduces dependence on hyperparameter searches, ensures interpretable coefficients, and supports robust, practical, and auditable NIRS method development.

Abstract: Near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) is rapid and non-destructive, but reliable calibration still depends heavily on spectral preprocessing. In routine practice, preprocessing is often selected by large external pipeline searches that are costly, unstable on small calibration sets, and difficult to audit. We introduce operator-adaptive calibration, a framework that moves linear preprocessing selection inside the calibration model. Candidate treatments are encoded as linear spectral operators, while nonlinear or sample-adaptive corrections such as SNV, MSC, and ASLS are handled as fold-local branches to prevent leakage.
  We instantiate the framework for PLS and Ridge regression. For PLS, covariance identities enable fast NIPALS and SIMPLS variants while preserving original-wavelength coefficients. For Ridge, operator-adaptive kernels yield a dual formulation with recoverable original-space coefficients. The approach was evaluated on more than 50 heterogeneous NIRS datasets against conventional PLS, Ridge, CatBoost, and CNN baselines under documented search budgets.
  Compact operator-adaptive PLS with ASLS branch preprocessing achieved a median RMSEP/PLS ratio of 0.960 with 42 wins on 57 datasets, while a deployable AOM-Ridge selector improved over tuned Ridge by a median 2.22% with 35 wins on 52 datasets. The proposed models reduce dependence on large preprocessing-HPO campaigns, produce traceable operator choices, retain interpretable coefficients, and fit in seconds for compact AOM-PLS. Operator-adaptive calibration therefore offers a practical route to faster, more robust, and more auditable NIRS method development.

</details>


### [561] [Causal Learning with the Invariance Principle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13589)
*Francesco Montagna,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: This paper explores causal discovery using structural causal models (SCM) and shows that two auxiliary environments suffice for identifying causal structures and enabling counterfactual inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inferring causal directions is challenging because it is an ill-posed problem. The paper aims to address this by leveraging structural assumptions about causality.

Method: The authors propose using acyclic and invariant causal relations across environments, demonstrating that two auxiliary environments are sufficient for causal graph identification.

Result: The theoretical framework shows that causal graphs and functional mechanisms can be identified with two auxiliary environments. The results also ensure correct counterfactual inference.

Conclusion: The study advances causal discovery, proving that minimal environment assumptions can identify causal structure and improve counterfactual reasoning.

Abstract: Causal discovery, the problem of inferring the direction of causality, is generally ill-posed. We use the language of structural causal models (SCM) to show that assuming that the causal relations are acyclic and invariant across multiple environments (e.g., the way minimum wage affects employment rate is stable across different geographical regions), \textit{only} two auxiliary environments are sufficient to infer the causal graph for arbitrary nonlinear mechanisms. Moreover, we demonstrate that this implies identifiability of the SCM functional mechanisms: as a corollary, we show that \textit{two} auxiliary environments are sufficient to guarantee correct counterfactual inference. We empirically support our theoretical results on synthetic data.

</details>


### [562] [Conformal Anomaly Detection in Python: Moving Beyond Heuristic Thresholds with 'nonconform'](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13642)
*Oliver Hennhöfer,Maximilian Kirsch,Christine Preisach*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'nonconform', a Python package that converts anomaly detection scores into calibrated p-values using conformal methods. It provides an accessible introduction to the field and integrates with popular tools for practical use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practitioners often lack statistically rigorous tools for converting anomaly detection scores into meaningful decisions. This paper seeks to fill that gap by enabling valid statistical interpretations in anomaly detection workflows.

Method: The paper proposes 'nonconform', a Python package that applies conformal anomaly detection strategies, integrating with existing tools like 'scikit-learn' and 'pyod'. It supports multiple calibration methods and extensions for enhanced robustness.

Result: The paper demonstrates that the 'nonconform' package achieves statistically principled anomaly detection, illustrating its practical utility through empirical results and code examples.

Conclusion: The work makes conformal anomaly detection more accessible and reproducible by bridging theoretical concepts with practical tools, contributing to both experimental and production settings.

Abstract: Most anomaly detection systems output scores rather than calibrated decisions, leaving practitioners to choose thresholds heuristically and without clear statistical interpretation. Conformal anomaly detection addresses this limitation by converting anomaly scores into calibrated p-values that are valid under the statistical assumption of data exchangeability, with a growing literature extending this idea beyond that setting. We present 'nonconform', a Python package for applying conformal anomaly detection within existing machine-learning workflows, and use it as the basis for an implementation-grounded introduction to the field. The package integrates with 'scikit-learn', 'pyod', and custom anomaly detectors, and provides a unified interface for calibration, p-value generation, and false discovery rate control. It supports several conformalization strategies, ranging from simple split-conformal calibration to more data-efficient and shift-aware extensions. Through a progression from foundational concepts to advanced conformalization strategies, complemented by code examples, the paper connects the statistical ideas behind conformal anomaly detection to their practical use in 'nonconform'. Empirical results demonstrate that the implemented methods enable statistically principled anomaly detection. Together, the package and exposition aim to make core conformal anomaly detection workflows more accessible and reproducible in experimental and production-oriented settings.

</details>


### [563] [What is Learnable in Valiant's Theory of the Learnable?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13840)
*Steve Hanneke,Anay Mehrotra,Grigoris Velegkas,Manolis Zampetakis*

Main category: stat.ML

TL;DR: The paper revisits Valiant's 1984 learning model, develops a new characterization for learnability with membership queries, and proves that certain classes, like $d$-dimensional halfspaces, become learnable in it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify and explore the theoretical nuances of Valiant's original learning model, which differs from PAC learning and presents unique challenges and opportunities for understanding learnability.

Method: The authors characterize learnability using a new query-compression scheme and analyze the introduction of membership queries in Valiant's original framework, extending it to arbitrary domains and testing specific classes like $d$-dimensional halfspaces.

Result: Learnability in Valiant's model is strictly more inclusive than PAC learning but less inclusive than its variant without membership queries. The authors developed an efficient algorithm for learning $d$-dimensional halfspaces within this model.

Conclusion: This study highlights the theoretical richness of Valiant's learnability model, demonstrating its unique properties and expanding understanding of learning theory through new algorithms and characterizations.

Abstract: Valiant's 1984 paper is widely credited with introducing the PAC learning model, but it, in fact, introduced a different model: unlike PAC learning, the learner receives only positives, may issue membership queries, and must output a hypothesis with no false positives. Prior work characterized variants, including the case without queries. We revisit Valiant's original model and ask: *Which classes are learnable in it?*
  For every finite domain, including Valiant's Boolean-hypercube setting, we show that a class is learnable if and only if every realizable positive sample can be certified by a poly-size adaptive query-compression scheme. This is a new variant of sample compression where the learner certifies samples via a short interaction with the membership oracle. Our characterization shows that learnability in Valiant's model is strictly sandwiched between learnability in the PAC model and the variant of Valiant's model without membership queries. This is one of the rare cases where introducing membership queries changes the set of learnable classes, and not just the sample or computational complexity.
  Next, we study the natural extension of the model to arbitrary domains. While we do not obtain an exact characterization, our techniques readily generalize and show that the same strict sandwiching persists. Finally, we show that $d$-dimensional halfspaces, which are not learnable without queries, are learnable with queries: we give a $\mathrm{poly}(d) \tilde{O}(1/ε)$ sample and $\mathrm{poly}(d) \mathrm{polylog}(1/ε)$ query algorithm, and prove that at least $Ω(d)$ samples or queries are necessary. To our knowledge, this is the first algorithm for halfspaces in Valiant's model.
  Together, these results uncover a surprisingly rich theory behind Valiant's original notion of learnability and introduce ideas that may be of independent interest in learning theory.

</details>


<div id='cs.CC'></div>

# cs.CC [[Back]](#toc)

### [564] [Prime Successor Irreducibility: Turing Machine Complexity, Kolmogorov Complexity, and Weakness-Based Formulations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12504)
*Ben Goertzel,Bill Lauritzen*

Main category: cs.CC

TL;DR: This paper explores the computational irreducibility of the prime sequence, suggesting that finding the next prime cannot be significantly optimized beyond sequential testing, supported by complexity-theoretic, Kolmogorov, and statistical formulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To better understand the computational complexity and unpredictability inherent in the sequence of prime numbers, which has key implications in number theory and related fields.

Method: The authors develop multiple frameworks: (1) PSI-T for Turing-machine complexity, (2) PSI-K based on Kolmogorov complexity proving incompressibility of prime gaps, and (3) weakness-based formulations analyzing structures like collision probabilities and logical entropy. These are connected via sieve-theoretic methods.

Result: The authors establish rigorous lower bounds on prime gap entropy in specified intervals, derive incompressibility results for typical prime gaps, and demonstrate sparse probabilities in certain prime gap distributions. The findings reinforce the view of primes as locally unpredictable.

Conclusion: The paper unites statistical, complexity-theoretic, and sieve-theoretic perspectives to formalize the computational hardness of predicting prime gaps, emphasizing their computational irreducibility without assuming randomness.

Abstract: We develop conjectures and theorems expressing the idea that the prime sequence exhibits computational irreducibility in the transition from one prime to its successor. Informally, given a prime pp p, no general algorithm can compute the least prime greater than pp p substantially faster than sequentially testing candidates for primality, except possibly on sparse input sets.
  Our framework proceeds along complementary lines. First, we formalize Prime Successor Irreducibility in a Turing-machine complexity model (PSI-T), asserting lower bounds on running time relative to a sequential baseline. Second, we propose a Kolmogorov-complexity formulation (PSI-K), asserting that typical prime gaps are algorithmically incompressible at their scale; we prove PSI-K(c, $δ$) unconditionally for all fixed c<1 using standard sieve bounds. Third, we develop weakness-based formulations: PSI-W (sparse-set anti-concentration) shows no small menu of gap values captures a noticeable fraction of primes, while PSI-W-LE shows collision probabilities decay and logical entropy tends to 1. These extend to prime constellations and consecutive gap vectors. Finally, a sieve-theoretic framework connects local obstruction patterns to Selberg weakness parameters.
  The PSI-K and weakness formulations connect irreducibility to classical statistical questions about prime gaps. Using the relationship between Kolmogorov complexity and Shannon entropy, we derive rigorous lower bounds on prime gap entropy in dyadic intervals [X,2X]. Together, these formulations provide a unified complexity-theoretic perspective on the apparent local unpredictability of the prime sequence, without asserting randomness or independence.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [565] [PG-LRF: Physiology-Guided Latent Rectified Flow for Electro-Hemodynamic PPG-to-ECG Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12541)
*Xiaoda Wang,Minxiao Wang,Kaiqiao Han,Defu Cao,Ching Chang,Yidan Shi,Runze Yan,Xiao Luo,Yan Liu,Xiao Hu,Yizhou Sun,Wei Wang,Carl Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of generating ECG signals from PPG data using a novel physiology-guided latent rectified flow framework (PG-LRF), improving ECG reconstruction and disease classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ECG is essential for cardiac assessment but impractical for daily monitoring. PPG, though wearable, lacks ECG’s diagnostic features and is noisy. Bridging the gap between these technologies can improve health monitoring.

Method: The authors propose PG-LRF, which introduces an electro-hemodynamic simulator and a Physiology-Aware AutoEncoder. They structure the framework to co-model ECG and PPG via shared cardiac dynamics and enforce consistency during generative transport.

Result: Experiments on the MC-MED dataset show that PG-LRF improves PPG-to-ECG generation fidelity and enhances cardiovascular disease classification performance.

Conclusion: PG-LRF demonstrates its ability to produce ECG signals that are both accurate and physiologically consistent, addressing the limitations in existing PPG-to-ECG methods.

Abstract: Electrocardiography (ECG) is the clinical standard for cardiac assessment but requires dedicated hardware that does not scale to daily-life monitoring. Photoplethysmography (PPG) is ubiquitous in wearables but lacks ECG-specific diagnostic morphology and is corrupted by motion and sensor noise. PPG-to-ECG generation aims to bridge this gap by recovering electrical morphology and timing from peripheral pulse signals. However, existing methods largely rely on statistical alignment and data-driven generation. They fail to explicitly structure the latent space around physiology-aware electro-hemodynamic factors and lack constraints from forward physiological dynamics. To address these challenges, we propose PG-LRF, a physiology-guided latent rectified flow framework. PG-LRF introduces an electro-hemodynamic simulator that co-models ECG and PPG through shared cardiac phase dynamics. Guided by this simulator, a Physiology-Aware AutoEncoder learns a structured electro-hemodynamic latent space. Then we integrate this simulator guidance into a PPG-conditioned latent rectified flow, enforcing ECG-side morphology consistency and ECG-to-PPG forward hemodynamic consistency during generative transport. Experiments on the large-scale MC-MED dataset demonstrate that PG-LRF significantly improves PPG-to-ECG generation and downstream cardiovascular disease classification, proving its ability to generate ECGs that are both signal-faithful and physiologically plausible under the ECG-to-PPG hemodynamic pathway

</details>


### [566] [ChannelKAN: Multi-Scale Dual-Domain Channel Prediction via Hybrid CNN-KAN Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12553)
*Nanqing Jiang,Zhangyao Song,Tao Guo,Xiaoyu Zhao,Yinfei Xu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: ChannelKAN is a new hybrid model combining CNNs and Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) to enhance massive MIMO-OFDM systems' channel prediction accuracy in high-mobility scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the reliability and spectral efficiency of massive MIMO-OFDM systems by addressing the challenge of jointly capturing short-term local variations and long-range nonlinear dependencies in CSI sequences.

Method: The ChannelKAN model combines CNNs and KANs, using dual-domain expansion to enhance frequency and delay-domain representations, multi-scale frequency information enhancement to retain dominant spectral components, and dual-feature fusion for adaptive integration.

Result: ChannelKAN achieves better performance in normalized mean square error (NMSE), spectral efficiency (SE), and bit error rate (BER) as compared to traditional methods like RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs, CNNs, and Transformers on 3GPP-compliant QuaDRiGa datasets.

Conclusion: ChannelKAN is a robust and efficient solution for CSI prediction in massive MIMO-OFDM systems, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining CNNs and KANs with novel modules for enhanced feature extraction and prediction accuracy.

Abstract: Accurate channel state information (CSI) prediction is essential for improving the reliability and spectral efficiency of massive MIMO-OFDM systems in high-mobility scenarios. Existing deep learning methods struggle to jointly capture short-term local variations and long-range nonlinear dependencies in CSI sequences. To address this challenge, we propose ChannelKAN, a hybrid CNN-KAN channel prediction model with multi-scale frequency domain information enhancement. The key insight is that CNNs and Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) are naturally complementary: CNNs extract intra-time-step local spatial-frequency correlations, while KANs with learnable Chebyshev polynomial activations fit inter-time-step nonlinear temporal evolution in a holistic manner. Specifically, a dual-domain expansion module first generates complementary frequency-domain and delay-domain CSI representations. A multi-scale frequency information enhancement module then retains dominant spectral components at multiple scales to strengthen key features and suppress noise. Next, a CNN-KAN feature extraction module captures local correlations via cascaded convolutions and models long-range dependencies via Chebyshev KAN layers. Finally, a dual-domain fusion module adaptively integrates features from both branches to produce the prediction. Experiments on 3GPP-compliant QuaDRiGa datasets demonstrate that ChannelKAN outperforms RNN, LSTM, GRU, CNN, and Transformer baselines in normalized mean square error (NMSE), spectral efficiency (SE), and bit error rate (BER) across various velocities and signal-to-noise ratios. Ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness of each proposed module.

</details>


### [567] [Active Sensing with Meta-Reinforcement Learning for Emitter Localization from RF Observations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12569)
*M. Shamail J. Khan,Nisha L. Raichur,Lucas Heublein,Christian Wielenberg,Alexander Mattick,Tobias Feigl,Christopher Mutschler,Felix Ott*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces a reinforcement learning (RL) framework for GNSS interference localization in challenging environments, achieving an 80.1% success rate in simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address GNSS interference localization in environments like indoors or multipath-rich areas, where traditional localization methods struggle.

Method: The method models the problem as a partially observable decision process and combines high-dimensional RF sensing with reinforcement learning using DQN and PPO approaches.

Result: The approach achieved a localization success rate of 80.1% on a simulated dataset with realistic propagation effects.

Conclusion: Reinforcement learning, especially when trained with simulation, is a promising approach for robust GNSS interference localization in complex environments.

Abstract: Global navigation satellite system (GNSS) interference poses a serious threat to reliable positioning, especially in indoor and multipath-rich environments where source localization is highly challenging. In this paper, we formulate GNSS interference localization as an active sensing problem and propose a reinforcement learning (RL) framework in which an agent sequentially explores the environment to infer the position of an emitter source from radio frequency (RF) observations acquired with a 2x2 patch antenna. The localization task is modeled as a partially observable decision process, since single-snapshot measurements are often ambiguous under multipath propagation and changing channel conditions. To address this, the proposed framework combines high-dimensional RF sensing with deep RL and recurrent policy learning. We investigate both value-based and policy-based approaches, namely Deep Q-Networks (DQN) and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), and study their behavior under domain shift. The approach is evaluated on a simulated dataset generated with the Sionna ray-tracing module, which provides realistic propagation effects and diverse environment configurations. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves a localization success rate of 80.1%, demonstrating the potential of RL for adaptive GNSS interference localization. Overall, the results highlight simulation-assisted training as a promising direction for robust interference localization in challenging propagation environments.

</details>


### [568] [Compact Latent Manifold Translation: A Parameter-Efficient Foundation Model for Cross-Modal and Cross-Frequency Physiological Signal Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13248)
*Bo Cui,Xiaowen Song,Yaowen Zhang,Shunzhe Zhang,B. J. F. van Beijnum,Monique Tabak,Ying Wang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces Compact Latent Manifold Translation (CLMT), a parameter-efficient framework to bridge modality and frequency gaps in physiological time series analyses, outperforming larger models in tasks such as cross-modal PPG-to-ECG synthesis and signal super-resolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address issues of modality and frequency gaps in physiological signal analysis caused by heterogeneous recording devices and to overcome the limitations of existing foundation models such as high computational costs and modality entanglement.

Method: The authors propose a two-stage discrete translation framework: (1) Universal Tokenizer with Hierarchical Residual Vector Quantization to decouple signals into structured discrete latent manifolds and (2) Context-Prompted Latent Translator to map discrete tokens across modalities, leveraging static physiological priors.

Result: Extensive evaluations show significant performance gains, including improvement of R-peak detection F1-score from 0.37 to 0.83 in PPG-to-ECG synthesis, and achieving a Pearson correlation of 0.9956 in extreme cross-frequency super-resolution (25Hz to 100Hz).

Conclusion: The CLMT model demonstrates that a compact, discrete-based approach can yield state-of-the-art performance in complex physiological signal tasks while being computationally efficient, paving the way for edge-deployable medical models.

Abstract: The analysis of physiological time series, such as electrocardiograms (ECG) and photoplethysmograms (PPG), is persistently hindered by modality and frequency gaps stemming from heterogeneous recording devices. Existing foundation models typically rely on continuous latent spaces, which frequently suffer from severe modality entanglement, lack high-fidelity cross-frequency generative capacity, and impose high computational costs that prohibit edge-device deployment. In this paper, we propose Compact Latent Manifold Translation (CLMT), a highly parameter-efficient (0.09B) unified framework that bridges these gaps through a novel two-stage discrete translation paradigm. First, we introduce a Universal Tokenizer utilizing Hierarchical Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) to decouple heterogeneous signals into isolated, well-structured discrete latent manifolds, effectively preventing inter-modality interference. Second, a Context-Prompted Latent Translator maps these discrete tokens across modalities by integrating static physiological priors, reframing complex signal synthesis as a pure latent sequence translation task. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our 0.09B model significantly outperforms massive baselines. In cross-modal PPG-to-ECG synthesis, it resolves temporal phase drift and dramatically improves the clinical R-peak detection F1-score from 0.37 (baseline) to 0.83. Furthermore, in extreme cross-frequency super-resolution (25Hz to 100Hz), it successfully recovers high-frequency diagnostic landmarks, achieving an unprecedented Pearson correlation of 0.9956. By learning a universal discrete language for biological signals with a fraction of the computational footprint, our approach sets a new trajectory for edge-deployable, multi-modal medical foundation models.

</details>


### [569] [Recurrent Transformer-Based Near- and Far-Field THz Wideband Channel Estimation for UM-MIMO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12578)
*Dmitry Artemasov,Alexander Shmatok,Kirill Andreev,Alexey Frolov,Manjesh K. Hanawal,Nikola Zlatanov*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces a block recurrent transformer model for hybrid-field channel estimation in 6G networks using terahertz communications and UM-MIMO, achieving significant performance improvements over existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of hybrid-field channel estimation caused by enlarged Rayleigh distances, near- and far-field coexistence, and limited digital chains in hybrid beamforming in 6G terahertz UM-MIMO networks.

Method: A single-block recurrent transformer model with state memory, trained to generalize to a wide range of wireless channel conditions, is used iteratively for hybrid-field channel estimation.

Result: The proposed method delivered approximately 5 dB and 7.5 dB improvements in normalized mean squared error (NMSE) over state-of-the-art techniques in narrowband and wideband scenarios, respectively.

Conclusion: The use of a block recurrent transformer for hybrid-field channel estimation in 6G UM-MIMO networks shows significant improvements in accuracy and robustness, addressing the challenges of hybrid beamforming systems.

Abstract: The integration of terahertz communications and ultra-massive multiple-input multiple-output (UM-MIMO) systems in 6G networks is motivated by their ability to enable unprecedented data rates, mitigate spectrum congestion, and enhance overall network performance. However, the enlarged antenna apertures and higher carrier frequencies in these systems increase the Rayleigh distance, causing users to span both the near-field and conventional far-field regions. Accurate spatial precoding thus requires exact channel estimation at the base station - a task made more challenging by the hybrid coexistence of near- and far-field effects and the limited number of digital chains available in hybrid beamforming architectures. In this paper, we propose a block recurrent transformer model to address this challenge. We demonstrate that a single transformer block equipped with state memory can be trained once and then iteratively applied for hybrid-field channel estimation. Furthermore, we train the model such that it generalizes to wireless channels with varying scatterer distances, different numbers of propagation paths, and wideband operation. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves performance gains of approximately 5 dB and 7.5 dB in normalized mean squared error (NMSE) over state-of-the-art solutions in narrowband and wideband scenarios, respectively.

</details>


<div id='q-fin.MF'></div>

# q-fin.MF [[Back]](#toc)

### [570] [Yield Curves Dynamics Using Variational Autoencoders Under No-arbitrage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12764)
*Fusheng Luo,H'elyette Geman*

Main category: q-fin.MF

TL;DR: The paper presents a generative framework combining deep learning and fixed-income modeling constraints, excelling in term structure forecasting and macroeconomic regime analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of combining the flexibility of deep learning with the rigor of fixed-income modeling, and to mitigate arbitrage violations and manifold collapse in term structure predictions.

Method: A two-stage physics-informed generative framework: (1) Dynamically constrained CVAEsT+LS for extracting term structure manifolds. (2) A Neural SDE governed by a no-arbitrage PDE for latent dynamics.

Result: The framework achieved 6.58 bps Mean Tenor RMSE in forecasting term structures, reduced arbitrage issues, and excelled in regime detection and scenario generation across multiple currencies.

Conclusion: The proposed approach offers a scalable solution adhering to theoretical constraints while excelling in forecasting and regime detection, advancing the field of term structure modeling.

Abstract: This paper introduces a physics-informed generative framework that resolves the fundamental conflict between the statistical flexibility of deep learning and the rigorous theoretical constraints of fixed-income modeling. We demonstrate that standard generative models and unconstrained statistical extrapolations suffer from "manifold collapse" and severe arbitrage violations when forecasting term structures across diverse macroeconomic regimes. To overcome this, we propose a two-stage architecture. First, a Student-t Conditional Variational Autoencoder with Dynamic Level Injection (CVAEsT+LS) extracts a robust, heavy-tailed term structure manifold, effectively decoupling macroeconomic shape dynamics from absolute base rates. Second, the latent dynamic evolution is governed by a continuous-time Neural Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) strictly penalized by a No-Arbitrage Partial Differential Equation (PDE). Empirical results across multiple sovereign currencies (USD, GBP, JPY) confirm that our synergistic approach drastically reduces out-of-sample forecasting errors -- achieving an exceptional 6.58 bps Mean Tenor RMSE -- and successfully overcomes the massive parallel drift and zero-lower-bound violations exhibited by the classical HJM model in extreme environments. Furthermore, through phase space vector field analysis, we demonstrate the model's superior capability in unsupervised macroeconomic regime detection and high-quality continuous-time scenario generation. Ultimately, this research provides a highly scalable, mathematically sound evolutionary engine for term structure modeling.

</details>


<div id='physics.plasm-ph'></div>

# physics.plasm-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [571] [TokaMind for Power Grid: Cross-Domain Transfer from Fusion Plasma](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.11033)
*JC Wu,Norton Lee,Kai Siang Chen*

Main category: physics.plasm-ph

TL;DR: TokaMind, a transformer model initially trained on tokamak plasma data, is evaluated across new domains like industrial degradation and power grid datasets. It shows robust performance in transferred tasks, especially under specific conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to assess whether the pre-trained TokaMind model can generalize its learned representations to domains with distinct but analogous structures, extending its utility beyond its original nuclear fusion data.

Method: A comparative evaluation of TokaMind's performance was conducted on four structurally diverse datasets, along with an analysis of factors influencing its transferability. A focus on physically meaningful feature design was highlighted.

Result: TokaMind demonstrated strong generalization, achieving notable scores in power grid event classification. Single-window performance surpassed CNN baselines, and confidence measures improved F1 scores significantly. Transferability depends heavily on aligned domain characteristics.

Conclusion: The study validates TokaMind's cross-domain potential and introduces a framework for understanding its transferability. It highlights the importance of domain alignment and structured evaluation protocols for PMU datasets.

Abstract: TokaMind is a multi-modal transformer (MMT) foundation model pre-trained on tokamak plasma diagnostics data from MAST, where it was shown to outperform CNN-based approaches on fusion benchmarks. We investigate whether its learned representations generalize to physically distinct but structurally analogous domains. Through systematic experimentation across four domains-industrial bearing degradation, NASA CMAPSS turbofan degradation, and two independent power grid PMU datasets-we identify four transfer-favoring characteristics that help explain where TokaMind's pretrained representations are most effective. Power grid synchrophasor data matches this target-domain profile most directly, while industrial degradation datasets demonstrate that TokaMind can still yield useful performance under partial alignment, especially when task design and feature construction expose physically meaningful degradation structure. On the GESL/PNNL 500-event benchmark with provider-aware evaluation, TokaMind achieves test $\text{F1} = 0.837 \pm 0.040$ (3~seeds) for severe event classification. Our central finding, however, is not the aggregate score: classification difficulty is structurally determined by provider-level grid topology, not model capacity. In the single-window early-warning regime, TokaMind outperforms a CNN baseline (F1~0.889 vs.~0.878)--a reversal that disappears as more event windows are provided. Furthermore, Critical Slowing Down (CSD) indicators, used as a confidence gate rather than a classification label, improve F1 from 0.696 to 0.750 at 63% coverage-outperforming the CNN baseline (0.636) at any coverage level. These results establish the first cross-domain validation of TokaMind outside nuclear fusion and propose a transferability framework and revised evaluation protocol for multi-source PMU datasets.

</details>


<div id='cs.DS'></div>

# cs.DS [[Back]](#toc)

### [572] [Min-Max Optimization Requires Exponentially Many Queries](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13806)
*Martino Bernasconi,Matteo Castiglioni,Andrea Celli,Alexandros Hollender*

Main category: cs.DS

TL;DR: This paper examines the query complexity required to find approximate stationary points for min-max optimization of nonconvex-nonconcave functions, showing the need for exponentially many queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding the computational difficulty of finding approximate solutions in nonconvex-nonconcave min-max optimization problems.

Method: The analysis focuses on the query complexity required by algorithms using gradient-based oracle access to find approximate stationary points.

Result: It establishes that any such algorithm requires an exponential number of queries with respect to $1/\varepsilon$ or $d$ to achieve an $\varepsilon$-approximate solution.

Conclusion: Min-max optimization for nonconvex-nonconcave functions is computationally challenging due to its exponential query complexity.

Abstract: We study the query complexity of min-max optimization of a nonconvex-nonconcave function $f$ over $[0,1]^d \times [0,1]^d$. We show that, given oracle access to $f$ and to its gradient $\nabla f$, any algorithm that finds an $\varepsilon$-approximate stationary point must make a number of queries that is exponential in $1/\varepsilon$ or $d$.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [573] [Beyond VMAF: Towards Application-Specific Metrics for Teleoperation Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13525)
*Ines Trautmannsheimer,Richard Grauberger,Frank Diermeyer*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper proposes a way to improve teleoperation video quality by retraining a model (VMAF) based on subjective human ratings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address challenges in teleoperation caused by poor video quality, which directly affects safety and performance in automated driving.

Method: An online study captured subjective ratings for video quality, which were then used to adapt the VMAF model specifically for teleoperation scenarios.

Result: The retrained model showed improved alignment with human quality ratings, with reduced errors (15%-27% improvement in RMSE and MAD).

Conclusion: Adapting quality metrics like VMAF with domain-specific data can significantly enhance their predictive accuracy in critical applications like teleoperation, though outlier cases remain a challenge.

Abstract: Automated driving has made remarkable progress, yet situations still arise where human intervention is necessary. Teleoperation provides a scalable solution to address such cases, enabling remote operators to support vehicles without being physically present. In this context, video transmission forms the operator's primary source of situational awareness, making video quality a decisive factor for both safety and task performance. In an online study, participants rated compressed video sequences from the Zenseact Dataset and provided subjective quality ratings. These ratings were then used to retrain the Video Multi-Method Assessment Fusion (VMAF) model, yielding an adapted variant tailored to teleoperation. The retrained model demonstrated improved alignment with human ratings compared to the original 4K VMAF. In particular, RMSE decreased from 10.36 to 8.83, and MAD from 8.71 to 6.38, corresponding to improvements of 15% and 27%, respectively. These results highlight that incorporating domain-specific data can enhance the predictive power of established quality metrics in safety-critical applications. At the same time, Outlier cases emerged in which videos received high objective scores despite noticeable degradations in regions critical for the driving task.

</details>


### [574] [What Do You Think I Think? Accounting for Human Beliefs Using Second-Order Theory of Mind](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12745)
*Patrick Callaghan,Reid Simmons,Henny Admoni*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a model enabling agents to detect human belief errors and cognitive biases, improving interaction and feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address interaction breakdowns caused by mismatched understanding between agents and humans due to cognitive biases.

Method: The paper employs the I-POMDP framework to model second-order Theory of Mind (ToM-2) and account for erroneous human beliefs and cognitive biases during interactions, enabling agents to adapt their feedback.

Result: User studies showed that ToM-2 learners improve teacher action informativeness and are perceived as providing more useful feedback.

Conclusion: Equipping agents with ToM-2 capabilities enhances interaction by accounting for human cognitive biases and providing adaptive feedback.

Abstract: Discrepancies between an agent's actual knowledge and what a person thinks the agent knows can hinder interactions. If an agent could detect such discrepancies, it could provide feedback to account for them and improve current and future interactions. Using the I-POMDP as a framework for a second-order Theory of Mind (ToM-2), this work endows an agent with the ability to model the evolution of a person's erroneous beliefs about an agent and the cognitive biases and heuristics (CBH) from which they arise. In doing so, the agent can detect when CBH might be at play during an interaction and adaptively generate feedback that accounts for them. An in-person user study shows how a ToM-2 learner can account for the effects of a teacher's CBH to significantly improve the informativeness of teacher actions, and subjective results suggest people find the ToM-2 learner's feedback more useful.

</details>


### [575] ["It became a self-fulfilling prophecy": How Lived Experiences are Entangled with AI Predictions in Menstrual Cycle Tracking Apps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13261)
*Wendy Zhou,Pelin Karaturhan,Alexandra Weilenmann,Jichen Zhu*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper explores AI's role in menstrual cycle tracking apps (MCTAs) via user interviews; it uncovers user reliance on AI predictions despite notable flaws and offers design suggestions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how AI-based predictions and explanations in MCTAs influence users' perceptions and experiences, especially given the lack of prior studies in this area.

Method: The authors conducted 14 semi-structured user interviews and a group autoethnography to explore human-AI interaction in MCTAs.

Result: Findings reveal (1) users interpret their experiences through AI predictions despite potential inaccuracies, (2) interfaces and AI explanations lack tools for critical reflection, and (3) non-normative users feel alienated in their interactions with MCTAs.

Conclusion: The study highlights the need for redesigning predictive AI features and explanatory interfaces in MCTAs to foster better user awareness, engagement, and inclusivity.

Abstract: In menstrual cycle tracking apps (MCTAs), AI-based predictions and insights have become increasingly popular. These features enable users to receive personalized information about their bodies and mental states. However, there is currently little research on how these predictive AI features and explanations affect users' lived experiences. This paper examines human-AI entanglement in MCTAs through 14 semi-structured user interviews and a group autoethnography. These methods uncover the processes leading to this phenomenon. Our results reveal that: (1) users understand their lived experiences in light of AI predictions, although these predictions can be faulty due to imperfect logging practices, (2) the user interface features and AI explanations do not support awareness or critical engagement with this entanglement and meaning-making, and (3) non-normative MCTA users report a sense of isolation in this entangled interaction. Based on our findings, we propose design implications for predictive AI features and explanations.

</details>


### [576] [Beyond Anthropomorphism: Exploring the Roles of Perceived Non-humanity and Structural Similarity in Deep Self-Disclosure Toward Generative AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13574)
*Satoru Shibuya*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper explores factors influencing deep self-disclosure to generative AI, focusing on perceived non-humanity and structural similarity, which were shown to significantly impact disclosure likelihood and depth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate psychological factors, beyond anthropomorphism, that influence how users disclose personal information to generative AI.

Method: Conducted a cross-sectional survey with 2,400 participants analyzing associations through logistic regression and ANOVA.

Result: Participants perceiving high non-humanity and structural similarity (Segment D) were more likely to disclose deeply than the baseline group, with both occurrence and depth of disclosure significantly differing.

Conclusion: Trust-related behaviors with AI, such as self-disclosure, may depend on psychological perceptions like structural similarity and non-humanity rather than anthropomorphism, but findings are associative and need further experimental validation.

Abstract: This study investigates deep self-disclosure toward generative AI by examining perceived non-humanity and structural similarity as psychological factors beyond anthropomorphism.
