We further demonstrate the utility of this framework in synthesizing realistic phishing campaigns within evolving communication networks. The study reveals how threats exploit structural vulnerabilities, highlighting the potential of our framework for developing next-generation defenses. Our code is available at https://github.com/Graph-COM/NSL.

</details>


### [632] [Representing Higher-Order Networks: A Survey of Graph-Based Frameworks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12509)
*Takaaki Fujita,Florentin Smarandache*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: This book explores higher-order graph models to better represent complex systems, emphasizing mathematical, structural, and modeling aspects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical graph models fail to adequately capture the rich, complex interactions in real-world phenomena.

Method: Surveying and unifying diverse higher-order graph frameworks, with corrections and additions in this 2.0 Edition.

Result: The book provides a systematic comparison and analysis of various higher-order network models.

Conclusion: It aims to unify concepts for modeling complex systems, helping researchers choose appropriate tools for theory and applications.

Abstract: Many real-world phenomena are naturally modeled by graphs and networks. However, classical graph models are often limited to pairwise interactions and may not adequately capture the richer structures that arise in practice. Higher-order graph formalisms extend this framework by incorporating multiway, hierarchical, temporal, multilayer, recursive, and tensor-based interactions, thereby providing more expressive representations of complex systems. This book presents a comprehensive overview of mathematical notions that can be used to model higher-order networks. It surveys foundational concepts, extensional frameworks, and newly introduced formalisms, with an emphasis on their structural principles, relationships, and modeling roles. The aim is to provide a unified perspective that helps readers compare diverse higher-order network models and identify appropriate tools for theoretical study and practical applications. This book is Edition 2.0. It mainly includes the addition of several concepts, as well as corrections and improvements of typographical errors and explanations.

</details>


### [633] [Beyond Individual Mimicry: Constructing Human-Like Social network with Graph-Augmented LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12512)
*Haoran Bu,Litian Zhang,Chuxuan Zhang,Zhanyuan Liu,Hui Pang,Xi Zhang*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: This paper introduces GraphMind, a method to equip large language model (LLM)-driven social bots with the ability to mimic human-like social network structures, challenging both text-based and graph-based detection systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-driven social bots excel in local interactions but fail to create realistic global social network structures, making them vulnerable to graph neural network-based detection.

Method: GraphMind is proposed to explicitly enable LLM-driven bots to learn and emulate human-like social network structures. A botnet, GraphMind-Botnet, is constructed to test the efficacy of current social bot detection algorithms.

Result: Experiments with GraphMind-Botnet reveal substantially decreased performance in both text-based and graph-based detection systems.

Conclusion: The study underscores the importance of accurate social link construction in LLM-driven bots and exposes significant weaknesses in present bot detection mechanisms.

Abstract: Driven by large language models (LLMs), social bot can autonomously engage in local interactions, whose human-like behaviors enable them to evade social bot detection. However, while these botnets exhibit realistic local social interactions, they fail to preserve human-like social network. This is because LLM-based bots are graph-unaware and cannot coordinate over global interactions, which makes those botnets vulnerable to graph neural network (GNN)-based detection. To address this limitation, we propose GraphMind, which equips LLM-driven social bots to explicitly learn and fit human-like social network structures. Building on this foundation, we further construct GraphMind-Botnet, a LLM-driven botnet designed to evaluate the performance of existing social bot detection algorithms. Experiments on datasets derived from GraphMind-Botnet show that both text-based and graph-based detection models show substantially degraded performance in distinguishing. Our results highlight the critical role of social link construction in LLM-driven social network generation, while exposing fundamental weaknesses in existing bot detection mechanisms.

</details>


### [634] [SP-GCRL: Influence Maximization on Incomplete Social Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12513)
*Haohua Niu,Yuxuan Yang,Lingfeng Zhang,Hao Li,Jiao Liang,Zongfu Luo,Luca Rossi*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SP-GCRL, a novel framework addressing influence maximization challenges on noisy and incomplete social graphs, showing superior scalability and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Challenges in influence maximization arise due to incomplete or noisy social graphs and non-stationary diffusion dynamics, necessitating a robust, efficient, and scalable solution.

Method: The paper proposes SP-GCRL, which includes a social-propagation-aware nonlinear diffusion model, dual structural contrastive learning, a GAT-based regression surrogate, and an end-to-end seed selection policy using DDQN.

Result: SP-GCRL outperformed heuristic and learning-based baselines across different network budgets and structures, showing both effectiveness and scalability on real-world networks.

Conclusion: SP-GCRL effectively addresses the primary challenges of incomplete graphs and dynamic diffusion, providing a scalable and efficient solution for influence maximization.

Abstract: Influence maximization (IM) in real platforms is challenged by incomplete, noisy social graphs and non-stationary diffusion dynamics. We propose SP-GCRL, a social-propagation-aware graph contrastive reinforcement learning framework that learns end-to-end seed selection under partial observability.We first introduce a social-propagation-aware nonlinear diffusion function to model reinforcement/diminishing effects and probability drift under repeated exposure; we then construct dual structural views and perform contrastive learning to obtain node representations robust to missing edges and weak ties, while replacing expensive strategy metrics with a GAT-based regression surrogate to improve efficiency and scalability; finally, we use DDQN to learn an end-to-end seed selection policy on top of these representations. Experiments on multiple real-world networks show that SP-GCRL achieves significant gains over heuristic and learning-based baselines across budgets and topologies, while maintaining strong large-scale scalability.

</details>


### [635] [WhatsApp Vaccine Discourse (WhaVax): An Expert-Annotated Dataset and Benchmark for Health Misinformation Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12510)
*Jônatas H. dos Santos,Julio C. S. Reis,Philipe Melo,João F. H. Olivetti,Thales H. Silva,Matheus Gontijo Guimaraes,Glaucio de Souza,Marcos A. Gonçalves,Fabricio Benevenuto,Filipe B. B. Zanovello,Marco A. G. Rodrigues,Cristiano X. Lima*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: The paper introduces WhaVax, a dataset of vaccine-related WhatsApp messages from Brazilian public groups, annotated by experts and designed for misinformation research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the need for high-quality datasets for misinformation analysis in encrypted communication platforms like WhatsApp, especially in the context of health discourse during the pandemic.

Method: Data was collected using a keyword-based approach, cleaned with semantic deduplication, and annotated through a multi-stage process by medical experts. Several models, including classical and Large Language Models (LLMs), were benchmarked.

Result: The WhaVax dataset exhibits strong reliability for downstream tasks, provides unique insights into linguistic and group-level patterns of misinformation, and demonstrates competitive performance of LLMs under data scarcity.

Conclusion: WhaVax is a valuable resource for studying misinformation in private messaging environments and highlights the importance of domain-specific alignment and data availability.

Abstract: We introduce WhaVax, a new expert-annotated dataset of vaccine-related WhatsApp messages collected from large Brazilian public groups spanning multiple pandemic years. The dataset was constructed through a rigorous, carefully designed pipeline that integrates keyword-based data collection, semantic deduplication to remove near-duplicate content, and a multi-stage annotation protocol conducted by medical specialists. This process produced a high-quality gold-standard corpus, characterized by substantial inter-annotator agreement and strong reliability for downstream analysis. Additionally, we provide a detailed characterization of WhatsApp misinformation, revealing distinctive linguistic, structural, lexical, temporal, and group-level patterns, as well as a meaningful layer of ambiguous cases that reflect the complexity of health discourse in private messaging. We also benchmark classical models, fine-tuned Small Language Models, and zero- or few-shot Large Language Models under realistic data-scarcity constraints, demonstrating that strong embeddings and LLM approaches perform competitively, while domain alignment and data availability remain critical factors. This study provides a rare, high-quality resource to support misinformation research and computational modeling in encrypted communication environments.

</details>


### [636] [PERCEIVE: A Benchmark for Personalized Emotion and Communication Behavior Understanding on Social Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12525)
*Jian Liao,Yujin Zheng,Suge Wang,Jianxing Zheng,Deyu Li*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: The paper introduces PERCEIVE, a large-scale bilingual benchmark aimed at analyzing reader-centric emotional responses to social media content across five dimensions, highlighting limitations in current models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors aim to address the limitations of current emotion analysis in social media, which largely focuses on the author and disregards diverse reader-specific emotional perceptions and social contexts.

Method: They developed PERCEIVE, a bilingual benchmark that integrates author-created content, reader emotional feedback, communication behavior, user attributes, and social graphs for personalized, reader-centric emotion analysis.

Result: The findings show that current state-of-the-art models, including LLMs with advanced reasoning, struggle significantly with the complexities of the reader-aware emotion analysis task posed by PERCEIVE.

Conclusion: PERCEIVE serves as a critical resource for advancing socially-intelligent NLP by enabling the modeling of emotion and behavior within social contexts, giving direction for future research.

Abstract: Current emotion analysis in social media is predominantly author-centric, failing to capture the subjective nature of emotional responses across diverse readers. This paradigm overlooks the crucial link between individual perception, communication behavior, and the underlying social network. To bridge this gap, we introduce PERCEIVE, a novel bilingual (English and Chinese) large-scale benchmark that, to the best of our knowledge, is the first to integrate five critical dimensions for social perception: author-created content, genuine readers' emotional feedback (derived from their comments), communication behavior, user attributes, and the social graph. This benchmark enables a paradigm shift towards truly personalized, reader-centric analysis, where different readers' emotional responses to the same content are naturally captured through their real-world interactions. By annotating emotions from reader comments and synchronously capturing communication intent, PERCEIVE provides a unique resource to model the intrinsic coupling between emotion and behavior, grounded in social context. We establish a comprehensive evaluation protocol, testing state-of-the-art methods, including large language models (LLMs) with advanced reasoning enhancement. Our findings reveal significant shortcomings in existing approaches when handling this multifaceted, user-aware task. PERCEIVE offers a foundational resource and clear direction for future research in socially-intelligent NLP, pushing models towards a more unified understanding of emotion on social media.

</details>


### [637] [Linking Extreme Discourse to Structural Polarization in Signed Interaction Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12814)
*Zhijin Guo,Li Zhang,Tyler Bonnet,Janet B. Pierrehumbert,Xiaowen Dong*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: The paper integrates language and structural interaction analysis using a novel signed-network pipeline to measure polarization in online communities, showcasing its application to Reddit Brexit discussions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior studies on polarization primarily focus on either linguistic or structural dimensions, often leaving a gap in connecting the two. This research seeks to bridge this gap by creating a pipeline that transforms language into a structured framework to analyze polarization.

Method: The authors propose a language-grounded signed-network pipeline that uses large language model (LLM) stance scores to derive signed edge weights. Two measures – spectral Eigen-Sign score and partition-based frustration score – are employed to quantify polarization. They analyze discourse and temporal variations in Reddit Brexit discussions using this framework.

Result: The measures align generally but differ in their sensitivity to edge magnitude. The continuous weighted edges reveal nuanced patterns of intensity, which are less visible with binary representations. They also identify correlations between language signals like toxicity and future polarization levels.

Conclusion: This study demonstrates a unified approach to understanding polarization dynamics by connecting discourse and network structure in a single framework. It provides insights into how language signals influence polarization over time, potentially aiding forecasting efforts.

Abstract: Polarization in online communities is often studied through either language or interaction structure, but the two views are rarely connected in a unified measurement pipeline. Prior work links them by building interaction graphs from human judgments of agreement and disagreement, leaving a gap between language as observed text and structure as an engineered representation of that text. We address this gap with a language-grounded signed-network pipeline that derives continuous signed edge weights from LLM stance scores and quantifies structural polarization using two complementary measures: a spectral Eigen-Sign score and a partition-based frustration score. After normalization, the two measures show substantial agreement while retaining important differences in their sensitivity to edge magnitude. Applying the framework to Reddit Brexit discussions, we analyze how window-level discourse signals, including toxicity, extreme scalar claims, and perplexity, relate to temporal variation in structural polarization. Edge-level and ablation analyses show that continuous, confidence-weighted signed edges reveal intensity-sensitive patterns that are muted under sign-only representations. We further report an exploratory one-step-ahead forecasting analysis suggesting that lagged language signals may contain information about future polarization beyond structural persistence. Together, the results demonstrate how discourse and signed-network structure can be connected in a single framework for measuring and interpreting polarization dynamics over time.

</details>


### [638] [When Do LLMs Generate Realistic Social Networks? A Multi-Dimensional Study of Culture, Language, Scale, and Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12898)
*Sai Hemanth Kilaru,Sriram Theerdh Manikyala,Raghav Upadhyay,Sri Sai Kumar Ramavath,Srivika Nunavathu,Dalal Alharthi*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: This study explores how large language models (LLMs) generate synthetic social networks and examines the influence of prompt design, cultural framing, prompt language, and model size on network characteristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To clarify how factors like prompt design, cultural context, and model specifics influence LLM-generated social networks, and how these outputs correspond to sociological theories.

Method: The researchers designed four mechanisms for tie formation and generated 192 directed networks using pre-defined personas across various cultural contexts, languages, prompting structures, and GPT-4.1 model scales.

Result: Cultural framing affects homophily and connectivity; political affiliation heavily influences network ties; prompt structure and model size shape dynamics differently. Hindi prompt language uniquely emphasizes religion homophily.

Conclusion: Prompt-related parameters, often seen as technical, encode significant sociological assumptions, and LLM-generated networks exhibit both realistic clustering and embedded demographic biases.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as substitutes for human subjects in behavioral simulations, including synthetic social network generation. Yet it remains unclear how their relational outputs depend on prompt design, cultural framing, prompt language, and model scale. Building on homophily theory and structural balance theory, we formalize four LLM-based tie-formation mechanisms: sequential, global, local, and iterative, and treat them as distinct conditional distributions over edge sets.
  Using a fixed roster of 50 demographically grounded personas, we generate 192 verified directed networks across four cultural contexts, four prompt languages, three GPT-4.1 variants, and four prompting architectures, with two seeds per condition. We find that cultural framing shifts inbreeding homophily and largest-component connectivity. Political affiliation dominates tie formation under three methods, while the global method substitutes age, showing that prompt architecture functions as a substantive sociological variable. Model scale produces a stable divergence ranking, with the smallest variant behaving qualitatively differently rather than merely noisily. Prompt language alone sharply shifts religion homophily, especially under Hindi prompting, while leaving political homophily nearly invariant.
  LLM-generated networks match real social graphs on clustering and modularity better than standard graph baselines, yet encode demographic biases above empirical levels. These results show that prompt choices often treated as implementation details encode substantive sociological assumptions.

</details>


### [639] [Structural Diversity Drives Disruptive Scientific Innovation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12514)
*Yichun Peng,Saike He,Peijie Zhang,Kang Zhao,Yi Yang,Ning Zhang,Qingpeng Zhang,Daniel Dajun Zeng,Hao Peng*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Structural Diversity (SD) as a metric for scientific collaboration and demonstrates its effectiveness in fostering innovation, using a century-scale dataset and causal analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understanding the optimal structure of team collaboration that leads to breakthrough scientific innovation remains underexplored; traditional metrics fail to capture the deeper collaborative architecture.

Method: The researchers analyzed a dataset of 260 million publications spanning a century, leveraged causal inference, and exploited a quasi-natural experiment from a 2012 NSF policy change to measure the impact of Structural Diversity.

Result: Structural Diversity consistently predicts disruptive innovation, outperforms traditional metrics, interacts positively with team size, and mitigates limitations typically seen with larger teams through effective knowledge integration.

Conclusion: Structural Diversity serves as both a theoretical metric and practical approach for designing collaborative scientific teams, offering a pathway to systematically enhance creative discoveries and innovations.

Abstract: Scientific innovation increasingly depends on collaboration, yet the organizational structure that fosters breakthrough ideas remains poorly understood. Existing metrics - such as team size or compositional diversity - capture readily observable characteristics but not the deeper architecture of collaboration. We introduce Structural Diversity (SD): the extent to which a team bridges multiple distinct knowledge communities within its prior collaboration network. Using a century-scale dataset of 260 million scientific publications (1900-2025) and combining causal inference with a quasi-natural experiment based on a U.S. National Science Foundation policy change in 2012, we show that SD is a powerful and robust predictor of disruptive innovation, outperforming traditional team novelty indicators such as team freshness and edge density. Moreover, SD positively interacts with team size and is able to mitigate the well-known "curse of scale" by transforming scale from a liability into a resource for creative synthesis. We find that one mechanism underlying this effect is Disciplinary Integration (DI): teams with higher SD can more effectively combine heterogeneous knowledge into novel configurations. Our findings position SD as both a new theoretical construct and an actionable design principle for organizing scientific collaboration. By linking the architecture of team assembly to the dynamics of creative discovery, our work offers a structural explanation for how collective intelligence can be systematically engineered to foster disruptive innovation.

</details>


### [640] [Real-World Challenges in Fake News Detection: Dealing with Posts by Cold Users](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12511)
*Sai Keerthana Karnam,Abhirup Kundu,Jashn Arora,Manish Jain,Animesh Mukherjee*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: This paper addresses the issue of detecting fake news and rumors on social media while overcoming the challenge posed by cold users (new or inactive ones) by proposing a novel socially-aware model (UEN).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing presence of fake news and rumors on social media highlights the urgent need for effective detection models, especially those that do not overly depend on prior user behavior, to address the cold user problem.

Method: The authors propose a socially-aware context representation scheme called USER EVIDENCE NETWORK (UEN) that approximates missing behavioral data of cold users based on interactions from other users.

Result: The approach effectively addresses the cold user problem and provides robust solutions for fake news and rumor detection on real-world platforms.

Conclusion: The study emphasizes the importance of new algorithms that integrate socially-aware approximations for cold users, making platforms more robust in combating misinformation.

Abstract: Social media serves as a primary source of information in the current digital era. Many people consume a vast range of information in a very short span, yet, amidst the stream of genuine information, fake news and rumors continue to spread. The need for effective detection models is becoming increasingly critical. Past user behavior and user engagement on a post are strong signals that SOTA approaches leverage for fake news detection and other post classification tasks. However, these approaches lean too heavily on knowing this past behavior, and thus suffer from a cold user problem, or users that are new or have minimal footprint on the platform. In this paper, we make three core contributions. We first establish the value of user behavior, both content and user-user interactions, in the task of fake news and rumor detection. We then establish the extensive prevalence of cold users in the real-world datasets, and show the need for newer algorithms considering cold users. We next propose a novel socially-aware context representation scheme - USER EVIDENCE NETWORK (UEN) - to detect the spread of misinformation and unverified information while efficiently navigating this cold user challenge. We introduce techniques that approximate missing or absent behavior data of a new user from existing users' interactions. By carefully addressing the cold user challenge, our work provides robust approaches targeting fake news and rumor detection for real-world platforms.

</details>


<div id='econ.EM'></div>

# econ.EM [[Back]](#toc)

### [641] [The Payment Heterogeneity Index: An Integrated Unsupervised Framework for High-Volume Procurement Oversight and Decision Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12547)
*Kyriakos Christodoulides*

Main category: econ.EM

TL;DR: The paper presents the Payment Heterogeneity Index (PHI), an unsupervised method using structural heterogeneity for detecting irregularities in post-award payments in public procurement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fraud and corruption risks in public procurement are exacerbated by transaction volumes and limited oversight, particularly in unexplored post-award payments where existing detection methods are restrictive.

Method: The Structural Heterogeneity Index (SHI) was developed with four key components (modality, asymmetry, tail behaviour, and structural dispersion), and its instance, PHI, was applied to UK municipal procurement to identify anomalies in payment structures.

Result: PHI identified a high-risk supplier cohort with statistically significant structural anomalies, unmasked patterns hidden from alternative metrics, and was validated by forensic review.

Conclusion: PHI offers a transparent, interpretable, and effective screening mechanism for detecting irregularities in post-award payments, even in the absence of labeled datasets.

Abstract: Public procurement is vulnerable to error, fraud and corruption, yet high transaction volumes overwhelm oversight. While research often focuses on tender-stage anomalies, post-award payments remain underexplored. Since labelled datasets are rare and existing methods such as Benford's Law face restrictive assumptions, there is a need for additional interpretable, unsupervised frameworks that augment oversight and simplify management. This paper introduces the Structural Heterogeneity Index (SHI), a composite statistic for one-dimensional samples defined by four components: modality, asymmetry, tail behaviour, and structural dispersion. The Payment Heterogeneity Index (PHI) is its multiplicative instance for post-award payments. PHI combines a tail-behaviour component, sensitive to outliers and point clustering, with a structural-dispersion component summarising payment regime architecture. Structural dispersion is computed via Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) estimation, integrating within-regime variability, prevalence, and separation from the dominant mode. Applied to UK municipal procurement data, PHI isolates a financially significant cohort (10.1% of high-volume suppliers) whose structural signatures deviate from the population and interact with recurring payment anchors. Permutation and Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests confirm that high-PHI suppliers exhibit statistically significant structural differences. A forensic review by a Certified Fraud Examiner supports the plausibility of the prioritised cases. Comparison shows PHI uniquely identifies regime separation obscured by metrics like the Coefficient of Variation (\r{ho}=0.310). PHI functions as an effective discovery tool where no confirmed labels exist, offering a transparent, lightweight screening mechanism for post-award oversight.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.stat-mech'></div>

# cond-mat.stat-mech [[Back]](#toc)

### [642] [Beyond Explained Variance: A Cautionary Tale of PCA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13520)
*Gionni Marchetti*

Main category: cond-mat.stat-mech

TL;DR: The paper critiques PCA for visualizing nonlinear low-dimensional manifolds and proposes t-SNE, persistent homology, and a generative probabilistic-geometric model as alternatives for the example of fossil mammalian teeth data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) may not effectively visualize nonlinear manifolds, which calls for exploring alternative techniques for high-dimensional data visualization, as demonstrated on fossil teeth data.

Method: The authors compared PCA with t-SNE and persistent homology, also proposing a generative model in which the data are uniformly sampled from a unit circle, using cosine distance distributions for validation.

Result: t-SNE and persistent homology revealed a non-clustered ring-like structure with intrinsic dimensionality of one, consistent with a generative model showing arcsine-distributed cosine distances.

Conclusion: The results suggest that t-SNE, persistent homology, and the proposed generative model provide a more accurate representation of nonlinear structures in data compared to PCA on similar datasets.

Abstract: We address shortcomings of principal component analysis (PCA) for visualizing high-dimensional data lying on a nonlinear low-dimensional manifold via two-dimensional scatterplots, focusing on a fossil teeth dataset from the early mammalian insectivore Kuehneotherium. While the PCA scatterplot reported by Jolliffe and Cadima (Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A, 2016) shows clustering in the region where PC2 < 0, our analysis based on t-SNE and persistent homology (PH) reveals a ring-like structure with no evident clustering and intrinsic dimensionality equal to one. We further propose a generative probabilistic-geometric model in which the data are sampled uniformly from a unit circle. Under this model, pairwise cosine distances follow an arcsine distribution, in qualitative agreement with the observed U-shaped distribution, thereby independently supporting the analysis based on tt t-SNE and persistent homology.

</details>


<div id='cs.SD'></div>

# cs.SD [[Back]](#toc)

### [643] [BioSEN: A Bio-acoustic Signal Enhancement Network for Animal Vocalizations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12534)
*Tianyu Song,Ton Viet Ta,Ngamta Thamwattana,Hisako Nomura,Linh Thi Hoai Nguyen*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: BioSEN is a specialized audio enhancement model for bioacoustic signals, outperforming speech enhancement models in efficiency and performance for bioacoustic applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of enhancing noisy bioacoustic recordings, which differ significantly from human speech and are essential for biodiversity monitoring and conservation.

Method: The proposed BioSEN model uses three specialized modules: a multi-scale dual-axis attention unit for time-frequency feature extraction, a bio-harmonic multi-scale enhancement unit for harmonic structures, and an energy-adaptive gating connection unit to separate vocalizations from noise.

Result: BioSEN matches or surpasses state-of-the-art speech enhancement models in performance on three bioacoustic datasets, with considerably lower computational requirements.

Conclusion: BioSEN is effective and efficient for enhancing bioacoustic audio, offering valuable potential for applications in biodiversity monitoring and conservation.

Abstract: Most work in audio enhancement targets human speech, while bioacoustics is less studied due to noisy recordings and the distinct traits of animal sounds. To fill this gap, we adapt speech enhancement methods and build BioSEN, a model made for bioacoustic signals. BioSEN has three modules: a multi-scale dual-axis attention unit for time-frequency feature extraction, a bio-harmonic multi-scale enhancement unit for capturing harmonic structures, and an
  energy-adaptive gating connection unit that uses frequency weights to keep vocalizations from being removed as noise. Tests on three bioacoustic datasets show that BioSEN matches or exceeds state-of-the-art speech enhancement models while using far less computation. These results show BioSEN's strength for bioacoustic audio enhancement and its promise for biodiversity monitoring and conservation.

</details>


### [644] [EVA-Bench: A New End-to-end Framework for Evaluating Voice Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13841)
*Tara Bogavelli,Gabrielle Gauthier Melançon,Katrina Stankiewicz,Oluwanifemi Bamgbose,Fanny Riols,Hoang H. Nguyen,Raghav Mehndiratta,Lindsay Devon Brin,Joseph Marinier,Hari Subramani,Anil Madamala,Sridhar Krishna Nemala,Srinivas Sunkara*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: EVA-Bench is a novel evaluation framework designed for voice agents, focusing on realistic audio simulations and quality measurement across failure modes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is no existing benchmark that comprehensively evaluates voice agents in terms of realistic simulations and addressing voice-specific failure modes.

Method: EVA-Bench combines bot-to-bot simulated dialogues validated automatically with novel metrics (EVA-A for accuracy and EVA-X for experience) to assess voice agents across architectures. It includes scenarios, perturbation suites, and pass measurements.

Result: The study shows no agent exceeds 0.5 on both EVA-A and EVA-X metrics, significant differences between peak and reliable performance, and robustness gaps exposed by accent/noise perturbations.

Conclusion: EVA-Bench provides a robust, open-source framework to evaluate and compare voice agents, revealing key limitations in current systems and aiding future improvements.

Abstract: Voice agents, artificial intelligence systems that conduct spoken conversations to complete tasks, are increasingly deployed across enterprise applications. However, no existing benchmark jointly addresses two core evaluation challenges: generating realistic simulated conversations, and measuring quality across the full scope of voice-specific failure modes. We present EVA-Bench, an end-to-end evaluation framework that addresses both. On the simulation side, EVA-Bench orchestrates bot-to-bot audio conversations over dynamic multi-turn dialogues, with automatic simulation validation that detects user simulator error and appropriately regenerates conversations before scoring. On the measurement side, EVA-Bench introduces two composite metrics: EVA-A (Accuracy), capturing task completion, faithfulness, and audio-level speech fidelity; and EVA-X (Experience), capturing conversation progression, spoken conciseness, and turn-taking timing. Both metrics apply to different agent architectures, enabling direct cross-architecture comparison. EVA-Bench includes 213 scenarios across three enterprise domains, a controlled perturbation suite for accent and noise robustness, and pass@1, pass@k, pass^k measurements that distinguish peak from reliable capability. Across 12 systems spanning all three architectures, we find: (1) no system simultaneously exceeds 0.5 on both EVA-A pass@1 and EVA-X pass@1; (2) peak and reliable performance diverge substantially (median pass@k - pass^k gap of 0.44 on EVA-A); and (3) accent and noise perturbations expose substantial robustness gaps, with effects varying across architectures, systems, and metrics (mean up to 0.314). We release the full framework, evaluation suite, and benchmark data under an open-source license.

</details>


### [645] [NAACA: Training-Free NeuroAuditory Attentive Cognitive Architecture with Oscillatory Working Memory for Salience-Driven Attention Gating](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13651)
*Zhongju Yuan,Geraint Wiggins,Dick Botteldooren*

Main category: cs.SD

TL;DR: NAACA, a neuro-inspired auditory attention model, improves audio event detection by selectively focusing on salient cues, achieving significant performance gains while reducing processing demands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Audio Language Models struggle with analyzing long audio recordings due to an attention bottleneck, where dominant background patterns overshadow rare but important events.

Method: The study introduces NAACA, a training-free NeuroAuditory model centered around Oscillatory Working Memory (OWM), which assesses perceptual salience based on adaptive energy fluctuations and triggers higher-level cognition only for significant events.

Result: NAACA achieves a significant improvement in precision for audio event detection on the XD-Violence dataset (53.50% to 70.60% AP) and demonstrates qualitative robustness on the USoW dataset.

Conclusion: NAACA demonstrates that neuro-inspired models can effectively filter auditory salience, improving both efficiency and the detection of rare events in complex audio environments.

Abstract: Audio provides critical situational cues, yet current Audio Language Models (ALMs) face an attention bottleneck in long-form recordings where dominant background patterns can dilute rare, salient events. We introduce NAACA, a training-free NeuroAuditory Attentive Cognitive Architecture that reframes attention allocation as an auditory salience filtering problem. At its core is OWM, a neuro-inspired Oscillatory Working Memory that maintains stable attractor-like states and triggers higher-cognition ALM processing only when adaptive energy fluctuations signal perceptual salience, triggering higher-level reasoning. On XD-Violence, NAACA improves AudioQwen's average precision (AP) from 53.50% to 70.60% while reducing unnecessary ALM invocations. Furthermore, qualitative case studies on the Urban Soundscapes of the World (USoW) dataset show that OWM captures novel events and subcategory shifts while remaining robust to transient pauses and ambient urban noise.

</details>


<div id='physics.optics'></div>

# physics.optics [[Back]](#toc)

### [646] [DeepFilters: Scattering-Aware Pupil Engineering with Learned Digital Filter Reconstruction for Extended Depth of Field Microscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13619)
*Joseph L. Greene,Suet YIng Chan,Qilin Deng,Jeffrey Alido,Alexandra Lion,Guorong Hu,Ruipeng Guo,Tongyu Li,Kivilcim Kiliç,Ian Davison,Lei Tian*

Main category: physics.optics

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'DeepFilters,' a framework for extended depth of field microscopy that achieves high performance in scattering tissue by co-optimizing optics and digital reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional and deep optics approaches for encoding axial information in microscopy degrade in challenging scattering environments like biological tissues, creating the need for a more robust and generalized solution.

Method: DeepFilters employs a scattering-aware framework that jointly optimizes a parameterized pupil filter and a reconstruction network using a calibrated, differentiable forward model. It integrates empirical scattering kernels, physics-based regularization, and hybrid genetic-gradient initialization.

Result: The proposed approach extends the depth of the point spread function (PSF) from 16 microns to over 400 microns in clear media. It also allows signal recovery up to 120 microns deep in biological tissues, validated on fixed brain slices and sea urchin embryos.

Conclusion: DeepFilters provides a robust solution for extended depth of field microscopy in both clear and scattering media, with significant potential for biological imaging applications.

Abstract: Extended depth of field microscopy encodes axial information into a single acquisition through engineered point spread functions, but conventional and deep optics approaches are subject to degradation in scattering tissue. We introduce DeepFilters, a scattering-aware deep optics framework that jointly optimizes a parameterized pupil filter and a digital-filter-based reconstruction network through a calibrated differentiable forward model to achieve broad generalization without retraining. Incorporating empirical scattering kernels, physics-guided regularization, and a hybrid genetic-gradient initialization strategy, DeepFilters extends the PSF from 16 micron to >400 micron in clear media and enables signal recovery beyond 120 micron deep in biological tissues, validated across fixed brain slices and sea urchin embryos.

</details>


<div id='cs.NI'></div>

# cs.NI [[Back]](#toc)

### [647] [Swarm Network-as-a-Service (SNaaS)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13341)
*Balsam Alkouz,Osama Amin,Basem Shihada*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Swarm Network-as-a-Service (SNaaS), a framework using drones for on-demand connectivity with stringent SLAs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address increasing demand for scalable, on-demand connectivity with strict service guarantees in dynamic scenarios.

Method: The authors model interactions as services, design an SDN-based architecture, and propose drone orchestration strategies using a queuing-theory heuristic.

Result: The proposed framework demonstrates improved latency, fewer SLA violations, and smoother adaptation in dynamic conditions compared to fixed configurations.

Conclusion: SNaaS effectively provides scalable, adaptive, and reliable on-demand connectivity, outperforming traditional fixed setups in varying load scenarios.

Abstract: Emerging on-demand connectivity scenarios increasingly require networking solutions with stringent service-level guarantees. We propose Swarm Network-as-a-Service (SNaaS), a service-oriented framework that leverages fleets of drones to provide on-demand connectivity at scale. SNaaS explicitly models drone-to-device and drone-to-drone interactions as composable services, enabling consumers to request connectivity through Service-Level Agreements (SLAs). We formalize atomic and composite SNaaS services, present an SDN-inspired architecture that integrates the service-oriented triad of provider, consumer, and registry. We introduce a composition framework that orchestrates drones into end-to-end services. Within this framework, we define and analyze three composition strategies, i.e., direct, clustered, and parallel, and propose a queuing-theory-based heuristic for selecting the most suitable strategy under varying load conditions. A dedicated enforcement module continuously monitors queue stability and SLA latency, adaptively reconfiguring the swarm when violations occur. Experiments using real air-to-ground measurements show that the framework consistently outperforms fixed compositions, achieving lower latency, fewer SLA violations, and smoother adaptation as load and swarm size increase.

</details>


### [648] [Large Language Models for Agentic NetOps and AIOps: Architectures, Evaluation, and Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12729)
*Muhammad Bilal,Jon Crowcroft,Ruizhi Wang,Xiaolong Xu,Schahram Dustdar*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: This paper discusses how large language models are reshaping network operations (NetOps) and AI for IT operations (AIOps) by focusing on autonomy constraints, operational decision reliability, and secure deployments. It highlights evaluation methodologies tailored to agentic systems and examines associated risks like security and governance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to explore the integration of large language models in NetOps and AIOps tasks, focusing on the challenges of operational reliability, secure execution, and robust evaluation methods for agentic systems.

Method: The paper organizes literature around concepts like autonomy hierarchy, tool scope, evidence traces, and assurance contracts. It further suggests workflow-centric evaluations and emphasizes risk considerations in agentic system deployments.

Result: A recurring pattern emerges that operational reliability relies more on the supporting mechanisms than on the language model itself. Proposed evaluation methods include sandbox testing, rollback-aware scoring, and safety validations.

Conclusion: Intelligent NetOps and AIOps must treat system autonomy as a constrained operational control problem, emphasizing reliable, auditable, and secure deployment processes along with robust evaluations of agentic systems.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly being used to support network operations (NetOps) and artificial intelligence for IT operations (AIOps), including incident investigation, root-cause analysis, configuration synthesis, and limited self-healing. In both NetOps and AIOps, this shift is changing how tasks are managed. Agent-based operations work as workflows, from gathering evidence to taking action, following permissions, policies, and checks, and providing rollback options when necessary. This is crucial because operational decisions can have instant impacts. To make the argument concrete, we organise the relevant literature around the hierarchy of autonomy, tool scope, evidence traces, and assurance contracts. These contracts define what an agent may observe, propose, and execute. They also define the checks that must pass before any action is allowed. A consistent pattern appears across work on telemetry query recommendation, diagnosis, root-cause analysis, configuration synthesis, change planning, and limited self-healing. Operational reliability does not come chiefly from the model itself. It depends on the machinery around the model. We also argue that evaluation should go beyond static question answering. Agentic NetOps and AIOps systems require workflow-centred evaluation, including trace quality, bounded tool use, safe proposal generation, replay in sandboxed environments, and canary trials with rollback-aware scoring. Without these measures, a system may appear robust yet remain too fragile. Finally, we examine security, privacy, and governance risks that become acute when agents sit close to operational control surfaces. Taken together, the survey concludes that progress in intelligent NetOps and AIOps will depend on treating autonomy as a constrained operational control problem, whose outputs must be reliable, auditable, and securely deployable.

</details>


### [649] [NeuroRisk: Physics-Informed Neural Optimization for Risk-Aware Traffic Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12862)
*Yingming Mao,Ximeng Liu,Jingyi Cheng,Xiyuan Liu,Jiashuai Liu,Yike Liu,Zhen Yao,Yuzhou Zhou,Siyuan Feng,Qiaozhu Zhai,Shizhen Zhao*

Main category: cs.NI

TL;DR: The paper presents NeuroRisk, a deep learning-based model for more efficient and feasible risk-aware Traffic Engineering (TE) in production Wide-Area Networks (WANs), focusing on tackling utilization and availability trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional methods for Traffic Engineering in WANs either compromise on efficiency by limiting failure scenario selection or are computationally expensive. There is a need for a risk-aware TE solution that is both efficient and aligns with strict availability targets.

Method: The paper introduces NeuroRisk, a deep unrolled optimizer that incorporates domain-specific physics through the Sort-and-Select structure. It uses gated edge-local reservations for feasibility and gradient-aligned, permutation-invariant representations for scenario sets.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that NeuroRisk achieves optimality gaps close to solvers while being significantly faster (by factors of $10^2-10^5$) and outperforming other neural baselines on throughput and risk objectives.

Conclusion: NeuroRisk successfully balances computational efficiency and feasibility, making it a practical and effective solution for risk-aware TE in WAN operations.

Abstract: In production Wide-Area Networks (WANs), correlated failures dominate availability losses, forcing operators to reserve large safety margins that leave substantial capacity underutilized. Achieving high utilization under strict availability targets therefore requires risk-aware Traffic Engineering (TE) over dozens to hundreds of probabilistic failure scenarios-yet solving this problem at operational timescales remains elusive. We demonstrate that existing risk-aware formulations can be unified under an embedded Sort-and-Select structure, exposing a fundamental trade-off between expressiveness and tractability: classical optimizers either restrict scenario selection for efficiency or incur prohibitive decomposition costs. While deep learning appears promising, prior Deep TE methods mainly target maximum link utilization and rely on scaling-based feasibility, which fundamentally breaks under explicit capacity constraints and scenario-dependent risk. We present NeuroRisk, a physics-informed deep unrolled optimizer that exploits the structure of Sort-and-Select. NeuroRisk enforces feasibility via gated edge-local reservations and represents scenario sets through permutation-invariant, gradient-aligned cues. Evaluations on production-style WANs show that NeuroRisk achieves small optimality gaps relative to the solver with orders of magnitude speedup $(10^2- 10^5 \times)$ on risk objectives, while outperforming neural baselines on nominal throughput.

</details>


<div id='math.OC'></div>

# math.OC [[Back]](#toc)

### [650] [TRUST-TAEA: A trustworthiness-guided two-archive evolutionary algorithm with variable-grouping sparse search for large-scale multi-objective optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13324)
*JunYi Cui*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces TRUST-TAEA, a trustworthiness-guided two-archive evolutionary algorithm, to address challenges in large-scale multi-objective optimization, showcasing superior performance in both benchmarks and practical applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing two-archive evolutionary algorithms address some challenges but still suffer from inefficient search, incomplete front coverage, and late-stage archive drift. This motivates the need for a more reliable and problem-structure-aware approach for better convergence, diversity, and stability.

Method: The paper defines archive trustworthiness by integrating evolutionary progress with convergence-archive maturity. TRUST-TAEA is designed to utilize this trustworthiness to guide variable-grouping sparse search, anchor-probing compensatory search, and archive stabilization.

Result: Experiments on the LSMOP benchmark suite (500-5000 decision variables with two or three objectives) and a three-objective microgrid scheduling case demonstrate TRUST-TAEA's competitive performance in achieving convergence, diversity, and stability. It also achieves the best practical results in terms of balancing cost, emissions, and grid-power fluctuation.

Conclusion: TRUST-TAEA effectively leverages archive trustworthiness to provide better performance in both theoretical benchmarks and real-world applications, suggesting its potential for addressing challenges in large-scale multi-objective optimization.

Abstract: Large-scale multi-objective optimization remains challenging because high-dimensional decision spaces, complex variable interactions, and limited function evaluation budgets make it difficult to balance convergence, diversity, and stability. Existing two-archive evolutionary algorithms can alleviate the conflict between convergence and diversity, but they often underuse archive reliability and problem-structure information, leading to inefficient search, incomplete front coverage, and late-stage archive drift. To address these issues, this paper proposes TRUST-TAEA, a trustworthiness-guided two-archive evolutionary algorithm. Archive trustworthiness is defined by integrating evolutionary progress with convergence-archive maturity, and is used to coordinate variable-grouping sparse search, anchor-probing compensatory search, and archive stabilization. TRUST-TAEA is evaluated on the LSMOP benchmark suite with 500--5000 decision variables and two or three objectives. Experimental results show that TRUST-TAEA achieves superior or highly competitive performance in terms of convergence, diversity, and stability. A three-objective day-ahead scheduling case of a grid-connected microgrid further demonstrates its practical applicability, where TRUST-TAEA obtains the best IGD$^+$ value and generates a feasible dispatch strategy balancing cost, emissions, and grid-power fluctuation.

</details>


### [651] [Nonsmooth Set-Gradient Ascent to the Pareto Front via Layered Hypervolume and Magnitude Indicators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13468)
*Michael T. M. Emmerich*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper presents a new nonsmooth set-gradient ascent method for optimizing layered set indicators in multiobjective optimization, improving movement toward the Pareto front.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation lies in improving methods for multiobjective optimization by addressing limitations in layered set indicators and providing theoretical insights into nonsmoothness mechanisms in this context.

Method: The proposed method uses a set-gradient approach optimized for layered set indicators involving hypervolume and magnitude indicators, with strategies for handling nonsmoothness, exact gradient formula derivation, and finite-difference implementations.

Result: The method demonstrates effective ascent directions for Pareto optimization, with derived gradients for the magnitude indicator matching hypervolume gradients in time complexity and providing robust numerical results across benchmarks.

Conclusion: The study offers a theoretically grounded and computationally efficient approach for Pareto front navigation in multiobjective optimization, validated with numerical examples and reproducible code.

Abstract: A nonsmooth set-gradient ascent method is developed for moving finite approximation sets toward the Pareto front in multiobjective optimization. The method optimizes layered set indicators: a base indicator is evaluated on successive nondomination layers, and the layer values are combined with rapidly decreasing weights. This gives ascent directions to nondominated and dominated points while preventing deeper layers from compensating for deterioration of the first front. Two base indicators are treated: the hypervolume indicator and the magnitude indicator of the dominated set, whose expansion over coordinate projections contains extent, projected-area, and volume terms. The scalar objectives are nonsmooth because nondomination layers change combinatorially and the active orthogonal-union geometry changes piecewise. On fixed strata, where layer assignments and active geometry remain unchanged, the indicators are piecewise smooth and chamberwise continuous. For the magnitude indicator, an exact gradient formula is derived as a linear combination of hypervolume gradients of projected shadow sets. Thus, for fixed objective dimension, magnitude gradients have the same asymptotic time complexity as hypervolume gradients. Lexicographic layer aggregation is related to a unary infinitesimal encoding. For finite-$ε$ surrogates, the main nonsmoothness mechanisms are isolated and chamberwise Lipschitz continuity on bounded sets is proved; a two-point counterexample shows that hard-layer scalarization is not globally continuous across layer switches. The theory motivates a projected finite-difference implementation with repulsion and recovery from stagnation. Numerical examples and reproducible code cover two- and three-objective settings, including objective-space tests, curved fronts, a supersphere benchmark, and traces comparing layered magnitude and hypervolume ascent.

</details>


### [652] [Uncovering Symmetry Transfer in Large Language Models via Layer-Peeled Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12756)
*Zhehang Du,Hangfeng He,Weijie Su*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper investigates how the optimization strategy for training large language models (LLMs) induces geometric structures in their weights and embeddings. Using a mathematical surrogate model, the authors analyze how symmetries in target distributions shape these structures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether the common cross-entropy loss optimization process for next-token prediction in LLMs induces an inherent geometric structure in the models' learned parameters, and to provide theoretical explanations for observed phenomena.

Method: The authors use a constrained layer-peeled optimization model as a mathematically tractable surrogate for LLMs, analyzing nonconvex optimization programs and leveraging group-theoretic insights to reveal how symmetries in target distributions influence the learned structures.

Result: The mathematical analysis shows that specific symmetries (like cyclic-shift or permutation symmetries) in target distributions lead to distinct geometric formations in the context embeddings and weight matrices. Empirical results demonstrate that real-world LLMs exhibit these predicted symmetries despite lacking explicit regularization for them.

Conclusion: The optimization strategy for LLMs inherently induces geometric structures in weights and embeddings. These structures reflect symmetries present in training data and align well with theoretical predictions from the surrogate modeling approach.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are pretrained by minimizing the cross-entropy loss for next-token prediction. In this paper, we study whether this optimization strategy can induce geometric structure in the learned model weights and context embeddings. We approach this problem by analyzing a constrained layer-peeled optimization program, which serves as a mathematically tractable surrogate for LLMs by treating the output projection matrix and last-layer context embeddings as optimization variables. Our analysis of this nonconvex optimization program demonstrates that symmetries in the target next-token distributions are transferred to the global minimizers of the layer-peeled model in a precise group-theoretic sense. Specifically, we prove that when the target tokens exhibit a cyclic-shift symmetry (such as the seven days of the week or the twelve months of the year), the optimal logit matrix is exactly circulant, and the Gram matrices of both the output projections and the context embeddings form circulant geometries as well. Next, for exchangeable target distributions invariant under the symmetric group and, more generally, under two-transitive group actions, we show that the global optimal output projection matrix forms a simplex equiangular tight frame, while the optimal logit matrix and context embeddings inherit the permutation symmetries present in the input data. A key technical step is to reduce the constrained nonconvex factorized problem to an explicit logit-level convex characterization for cyclic symmetry and to a symmetry-based lower bound for permutation symmetry, together with a sharp characterization of the optimal factorization. Finally, we empirically demonstrate that open-source LLMs naturally exhibit symmetries consistent with our theoretical predictions, despite being trained without any explicit regularization promoting such geometric structure.

</details>


### [653] [Adam-SHANG: A Convergent Adam-Type Method for Stochastic Smooth Convex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12878)
*Yaxin Yu,Long Chen,Minfu Feng*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: Adam-SHANG is an improved Adam-variant algorithm with better stability and competitive performance in convex and non-convex optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the stability and convergence limitations in existing Adam-type optimization methods, especially for stochastic optimization.

Method: Proposed Adam-SHANG, incorporating momentum, adaptive preconditioning, and curvature-aware correction via a lagged-preconditioner. Introduced both conservative spectral-bound and practical trace-ratio stepsize conditions.

Result: Mathematically proved convergence in stochastic smooth convex settings. Experimentally confirmed improved performance in deep learning tasks, competing against Adam and AdamW.

Conclusion: Adam-SHANG enhances stability and convergence for convex optimization and performs competitively in non-convex settings, offering a practical alternative to existing methods.

Abstract: We propose Adam-SHANG, a Lyapunov-guided Adam-type method that couples momentum, adaptive preconditioning, and a curvature-aware correction through a more stable lagged-preconditioner update. For stochastic smooth convex optimization, we prove convergence in expectation under an admissible stepsize condition that can always be satisfied by a conservative spectral bound, without imposing global monotonicity on the second-moment sequence. To obtain a less conservative practical rule, we introduce a computable trace-ratio stepsize, motivated by a local coordinatewise alignment condition. The same structural update is also tested beyond the convex setting with simplified parameters. Experiments validate the predicted stochastic decay and show competitive training performance against Adam and AdamW on deep learning tasks.

</details>


### [654] [Proximal-Based Generative Modeling for Bayesian Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13278)
*Boyang Zhang,Zhiguo Wang,Ya-Feng Liu*

Main category: math.OC

TL;DR: The paper introduces a Proximal-based Generative Modeling (PGM) framework to address limitations in score-based diffusion models for inverse problems, offering improved reconstruction and faster sampling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Score-based diffusion models excel in generation tasks but face challenges in inverse problems due to the intractability of time-dependent likelihood scores, necessitating an alternative framework.

Method: The paper proposes PGM leveraging the equivalence between Gaussian convolution in diffusion and Moreau-Yosida regularization. It uses Moreau score and Moreau score matching for sampling and proximal operator learning.

Result: PGM removes early-stopping bias from score-based diffusion models, achieves non-asymptotic convergence, and significantly improves reconstruction quality and sampling time in experiments.

Conclusion: PGM circumvents explicit likelihood evaluation, resolves limitations of existing score-based methods, and demonstrates theoretical and practical superiority in generative modeling.

Abstract: Score-based diffusion models demonstrate superior performance in generative tasks but encounter fundamental bottlenecks in inverse problems due to the analytical intractability of the time-dependent likelihood score. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel proximal-based generative modeling (PGM) framework that rigorously circumvents explicit likelihood evaluation. Our framework is built upon a theoretical equivalence between Gaussian convolution in diffusion processes and Moreau-Yosida regularization in nonsmooth optimization. This enables a new sampling mechanism driven by the proposed Moreau score, which admits a closed-form expression via proximal operators. Moreover, we introduce Moreau score matching to learn the proximal operators that rely solely on samples drawn from the prior distribution. Theoretically, PGM eliminates the early-stopping bias inherent in the score-based diffusion model and achieves non-asymptotic convergence. Experiments demonstrate that PGM significantly surpasses state-of-the-art methods in reconstruction quality and sampling time.

</details>


<div id='math.ST'></div>

# math.ST [[Back]](#toc)

### [655] [Non-asymptotic quantisation of spherically symmetric distributions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12568)
*Luc Pronzato,Anatoly Zhigljavsky*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper addresses the challenges of optimal quantisation in high-dimensional spaces, introducing a method using random quantisers to achieve high performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zador's theorem provides insights on optimal quantisation, but its practical application is hindered by the super-exponential growth of sample size $n$ with dimension $d$.

Method: The authors analyzed spherically symmetric distributions to show that random quantisers uniformly distributed on a sphere with a suitable radius $r$ can significantly reduce distortion. Analytical techniques and numerical optimizations were employed to compute expected distortion and determine optimal $r$.

Result: Random quantisers on spherical distributions demonstrate superior performance for moderate $n$, with distortions calculable via precise triple integrals and optimization methods.

Conclusion: The findings highlight a practical approach to addressing quantisation in high-dimensional data, leveraging random quantisers and spherical symmetry, and providing valuable approximations for future applications.

Abstract: Zador's celebrated theorem is a cornerstone of optimal quantisation, establishing both the weak limit of the empirical distribution of an $n$-point optimal quantiser in $R^d$ and the decay rate of the associated $L_s$-mean quantisation error. However, for large dimensions $d$, observing this asymptotic behaviour demands an astronomically large sample size $n$, which grows super-exponentially with $d$. Through a detailed analysis of the quantisation problem for spherically symmetric distributions, we demonstrate that for moderate $n$ random quantisers uniformly distributed on a sphere of suitable radius $r$ achieve exceptional performance. The expected distortion, expressed as a triple integral, can be computed with arbitrary precision, and the optimal radius $r$ can be efficiently determined numerically. Leveraging results from extreme-value theory, we derive approximations for $r$, particularly in scenarios where $n$ scales with $d$. Depending on the growth rate of $n$, $r$ may either converge to zero or approach a limiting value that is independent of $s$.

</details>


### [656] [Optimal sequential tests yield log-optimal e-processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12720)
*Ashwin Ram,Aaditya Ramdas*

Main category: math.ST

TL;DR: The paper explores the relationship between optimal sequential tests and log-optimal e-processes, proving these can be aggregated into each other.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in understanding whether asymptotically optimal sequential tests can be converted into asymptotically log-optimal e-processes, complementing previous results.

Method: The authors introduce the concept of WAIT e-processes, designed as weighted aggregates of stopping time indicators that meet the criteria of nondecreasing behavior under alternatives.

Result: The authors demonstrate that asymptotically optimal sequential tests can be aggregated into asymptotically log-optimal e-processes, bridging a theoretical gap in sequential testing.

Conclusion: The results unify previous findings by showing equivalence between different forms of optimality in sequential testing and e-processes, offering a refined theoretical understanding.

Abstract: It has been recently shown that e-processes are sufficient for sequential testing in the following sense: every level-$α$ sequential test can be obtained by thresholding an e-process at $1/α$. However, in the above result, neither does the test have to be asymptotically optimal (in terms of stopping times) nor does the e-process have to be asymptotically log-optimal. It has separately been shown that asymptotically log-optimal e-processes yield asymptotically optimal sequential tests. In this paper, we prove the converse, arguably completing the story: it is possible to aggregate asymptotically optimal sequential tests into asymptotically log-optimal e-processes. This is accomplished by using a new class of WAIT e-processes: those that are Weighted Aggregates of Indicators of stopping Times that begin at zero, are nondecreasing and increase to infinity under the alternative at the optimal rate. Importantly, the paper discusses several nuances in the varied definitions of asymptotic (log-)optimality.

</details>


<div id='stat.ME'></div>

# stat.ME [[Back]](#toc)

### [657] [When to Trust Confidence Thresholding: Calibration Diagnostics for Pseudo-Labelled Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12780)
*Marcell T. Kurbucz*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper develops a method to analyze and predict bias caused by confidence thresholding in pseudo-labeling pipelines for downstream regression tasks, providing a diagnostic toolkit with simulations and real-world applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address and predict the bias introduced by confidence thresholding in pseudo-labeling, a common method for handling downstream regression estimands in cases with limited labeled data.

Method: The authors derive a closed-form expression for attenuation bias induced by thresholding and predict it using residual score variance. They propose sensitivity bounds under calibration drift and structural separations between classifier features and controls.

Result: They validate their method through five controlled simulations and a real-world application on the UCI Adult dataset, showing the practical utility and accuracy of their predictions.

Conclusion: The paper provides a practical decision rule using residual score variance and classifier output, allowing practitioners to assess the safety of confidence thresholding.

Abstract: Calibrated probability outputs of trained classifiers are increasingly used as inputs to downstream regression estimands such as effects, prevalences, or disparities for a latent group observed only on a small labelled subset. A standard practice is to threshold the calibrated score at a confidence cutoff and treat the hard label as the truth. Building on a recent identification result for the underlying moment equation, we develop a calibration-aware diagnostic apparatus for pseudo-labelling pipelines. We derive a closed-form expression for the attenuation bias that confidence thresholding induces in the downstream regression coefficient, and show that the bias can be predicted, before any inference is run, from the residual score variance $V^{*}=\mathbb{E}[\operatorname{Var}(p\mid X)]$ on the unlabelled set after partialling out the downstream controls $X$. We further obtain a sharp sensitivity bound under bounded calibration drift, and identify the boundary $V^{*}=0$, which holds iff $p$ is a deterministic function of $X$; this motivates a structural separation between classifier features $W$ and downstream controls $X\subsetneq W$. Five controlled simulations and a UCI Adult illustration trace the predictions. The contribution is operational: a $(V^{*}, κ)$ decision rule that practitioners can compute from any classifier output to decide whether confidence thresholding is safe.

</details>


### [658] [Towards a holistic understanding of Selection Bias for Causal Effect Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13430)
*Yiwen Qiu,Filip Kovacevic,Shimeng Huang,Peter Spirtes,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: stat.ME

TL;DR: The paper addresses selection bias in observational studies, particularly focusing on recovering causal effects like average treatment effects (ATE) in biased datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selection bias, such as 'healthy volunteer bias' in biobank data, can lead to biased causal inferences, making it important to explore how causal effects can be properly recovered from such datasets.

Method: The authors provide necessary and sufficient conditions for identifying ATE under selection bias. They leverage weak assumptions about probability classes to characterize propensity scores and selection probabilities, extending graphical identifiability criteria.

Result: Their findings relax previous constraints and offer a more comprehensive toolkit for addressing causal identification challenges in the presence of selection bias.

Conclusion: The study significantly advances the understanding and feasibility of identifying causal effects in datasets affected by selection bias, using less restrictive assumptions compared to prior works.

Abstract: Selection bias is pervasive in observational studies. For example, large scale biobanks data can exhibit ``healthy volunteer bias'' when respondents are healthier and of higher socio-economic status than the population they are meant to represent. Recovering causal effects from such sub-population is an important problem in causal inference, as estimating average treatment effects (ATE) from selected populations can result in a severely biased estimate of the ATE from the whole population.
  In this paper, we investigate the identifiability of the ATE under selection bias. We provide necessary and sufficient conditions for ATE identifiability, leveraging weak assumptions on probability classes to characterize propensity score and selection probability. Compared to previous works, our results extend existing graphical identifiability criteria and offer a more comprehensive understanding of causal effect identification with strictly weaker conditions in the presence of selection bias.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [659] [On Capacity and Delay of Wireless Networks with Node Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12080)
*Wei Li,Min Sheng,Junyu Liu,Jiandong Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper studies the impact of random node failures on wireless ad hoc networks, finding that both network capacity and delay are affected and deriving key scaling properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand how random node failures influence the performance of wireless ad hoc networks and to explore the relationships among capacity, delay, and fault tolerance.

Method: The authors analytically derive the scaling relationships of network capacity and delay in relation to the total number of nodes, node failure probability, and propose conditions for compensating capacity losses.

Result: The study shows that both network capacity and delay scale as \( \Theta(\sqrt{\frac{n(1-q)}{\log n}}) \), and that compensating for capacity loss due to failures requires deploying extra redundant nodes.

Conclusion: The findings underscore that even with equivalent active node counts, failure-prone networks perform worse than failure-free networks, and achieving a balance between high capacity and low delay remains challenging.

Abstract: One key challenge in designing resilient large-scale wireless ad hoc networks is to understand how random node failures affect fundamental network performance. In this work, we show that both network capacity and delay scale as \scalebox{0.65}{$\textstyle Θ\left(\sqrt{\frac{n(1-q)}{\log n}}\right)$}, where $n$ is the total number of nodes and $q$ is the node failure probability. The network capacity degenerates to the classical result given by P. Gupta and P. R. Kumar when $q=0$. Based on these results, we find that even with the same number of non-faulty nodes, a network with $n$ nodes and node failure probability $q$ has lower network capacity than a failure-free network with $n(1-q)$ nodes. To compensate for the network capacity loss caused by random node failures, at least $ε(n,q) nq$ redundant nodes are required, where $ε(n,q)>1$. We further prove that the optimal trade-off between network capacity and delay remains $O(1)$ regardless of node failures, implying that high network capacity and low delay cannot be achieved simultaneously. These results demonstrate robustness against stochastic variations in wireless channels.

</details>


### [660] [On the Generalization of Knowledge Distillation: An Information-Theoretic View](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13143)
*Bingying Li,Haiyun He*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper provides a theoretical framework for understanding knowledge distillation by introducing a distillation divergence and deriving generalization bounds for student models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap in understanding the theoretical foundations of knowledge distillation, which is widely applied in practice but lacks formal guarantees.

Method: The authors model the teacher and student training processes as stochastic kernels, introduce a distillation divergence based on Kullback-Leibler divergence, and derive both upper and lower generalization bounds under different assumptions. They further incorporate loss sharpness to enhance the bounds and analyze a case study in a linear Gaussian setting.

Result: The study yields generalization bounds for student models while identifying specific contributions from bias, variance, and rank bottleneck components in the distillation divergence. It also establishes the role of teacher model flatness in improving bounds.

Conclusion: This work provides theoretical insights and practical guidelines for designing efficient and effective knowledge distillation processes, bridging a gap between theory and application.

Abstract: Knowledge distillation is widely used to improve generalization in practice, yet its theoretical understanding remains elusive. In the standard distillation setting, a teacher model provides soft predictions to guide the training of a student model. We model teacher and student training as coupled stochastic processes and introduce a distillation divergence, defined as the Kullback-Leibler divergence between these two stochastic kernels. Within this framework, we derive two generalization bounds for the student model relative to the teacher's generalization gap: an upper bound under a sub-Gaussian assumption via algorithmic stability, and a lower bound under a central condition with sharper dependence on the distillation divergence. We further develop a loss-sharpness-aware bound with an explicit tightness regime, showing that the teacher's local flatness can strictly tighten the bound. Additionally, in a linear Gaussian case study, the distillation divergence admits an interpretable decomposition into bias, variance, and rank-bottleneck costs, yielding practical guidance for distillation design.

</details>


<div id='physics.med-ph'></div>

# physics.med-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [661] [Generating synthetic computed tomography for radiotherapy: SynthRAD2025 challenge report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13555)
*Viktor Rogowski,Maarten L. Terpstra,Niklas Wahl,Florian Kamp,Erik van der Bijl,Arthur Jr. Galapon,Christopher Kurz,Bowen Xin,Zhengxiang Sun,Hollie Min,Gregg Belous,Jason Dowling,Yan Xia,Siyuan Mei,Fuxin Fan,Arthur Longuefosse,Javier Sequeiro Gonzalez,Miguel Diaz Benito,Alvaro Garcia Martin,Fabien Baldacci,Valentin Boussot,Cédric Hémon,Jean-Claude Nunes,Jean-Louis Dillenseger,Zhiyuan Zhang,Jinghua Cai,Han Bing,Tan Zuopeng,Ricardo Brioso,Daniele Loiacono,Guillaume Landry,Adrian Thummerer,Matteo Maspero*

Main category: physics.med-ph

TL;DR: The paper evaluates synthetic CT (sCT) methods for radiation therapy, comparing MRI-to-CT and CBCT-to-CT workflows using benchmarks across diverse cases, analyzing metrics like image similarity, segmentation accuracy, and dosimetric impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
